Notice something important here: the angels had delivered the news to him, but he doesn't respond to the angels. He doesn't turn around to them and continue the conversation with them. They came to speak to him on behalf of Allah ﷻ, but Prophet Zakariya (AS) knows he can speak to Allah ﷻ directly. He was already talking to Allah ﷻ when the angels arrived, and there's no reason to stop now. So he continues his direct conversation with his Lord.

He's asking for an ayah - a sign that will confirm this miracle is truly beginning. He wants something tangible to mark the start of this impossible blessing.

### The Sign: Silence

Allah ﷻ responds directly to him (not through the angels this time): قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا - "Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three days except by gesture."

The word used here is تُكَلِّمَ, which is more specific than just "speak." كَلَّمَ means to address somebody in a way where you take a dominant or significant role in the conversation. It's directed speech where you have an active part. Allah ﷻ will temporarily take away his ability to have these directed conversations with people. He'll only be able to make رَمْز - gestures, signs, indications.

### The Command to Remember

Then comes the beautiful instruction: وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا - "And remember your Lord much." Make abundant dhikr of Allah ﷻ.

وَسَبِّحْ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ - "And exalt [Him] in the evening and the morning." The word سَبِّحْ means to declare Allah's perfection and transcendence. عَشِيّ refers to the evening hours after Maghrib, while إِبْكَار refers to the early morning hours.

Here's something profound: Allah ﷻ told him he wouldn't be able to speak to people during the day. Then immediately He tells him what to do with that time - remember Allah abundantly, make tasbih in the evening and morning. The day is covered with this instruction. But what about the night?

Notice that يَوْم (day) in Arabic specifically means daytime, distinct from لَيْل (night). The instruction about three أَيَّام (days) refers to the daylight hours. The instruction about evening (ashiyy) and morning (ibkar) also covers the daylight period. So when will he be told about the night? We'll discover that in Surah Maryam, where the other half of this conversation is revealed, and the sequencing of these instructions becomes beautifully clear.

translation: And [mention] when the angels said, "O Mary, indeed Allah has chosen you and purified you and chosen you above the women of the worlds
commentary:
وَإِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰكِ وَطَهَّرَكِ وَٱصْطَفَىٰكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَآءِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

And [mention] when the angels said, "O Maryam, indeed Allah has chosen you and purified you and chosen you above the women of the worlds."

### The Return to Maryam's Story

The narrative now shifts back to Maryam herself. The angels had come to Prophet Zakariya (AS), and now they come to Maryam. Notice how Allah ﷻ uses angels as messengers for both of them, establishing a parallel between these two blessed individuals.

### Three Declarations of Honor

The angels make three powerful statements to Maryam:

**إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰكِ** - "Indeed Allah has chosen you." The word ٱصْطَفَىٰ comes from صَفَا, which means purity. اِصْطِفَاء means to choose someone based on their purity. Allah ﷻ selected Maryam because of the purity that was already within her. This choice was made based on her inherent spiritual quality.

**وَطَهَّرَكِ** - "And purified you." After choosing her based on her existing purity, Allah ﷻ then cleansed her even further. This is the result of being chosen for divine service - Allah ﷻ purifies you beyond your original state. She was already pure, but selection by Allah ﷻ elevated her purification to an even higher level.

**وَٱصْطَفَىٰكِ عَلَىٰ نِسَآءِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ** - "And chosen you above the women of the worlds." This is a third selection, different from the first. The first istifa was Allah ﷻ choosing her for Himself, for His special service. This second istifa is Allah ﷻ giving her preference and distinction over all other women in the world. She holds a unique status not just with Allah ﷻ, but among all of humanity. She is honored above all women of all times and places.

translation: O Mary, be devoutly obedient to your Lord and prostrate and bow with those who bow [in prayer]
commentary:
يَـٰمَرْيَمُ ٱقْنُتِى لِرَبِّكِ وَٱسْجُدِى وَٱرْكَعِى مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ

"O Maryam, be devoutly obedient to your Lord and prostrate and bow with those who bow [in prayer]."

### The Command to Worship

After telling Maryam about her elevated status, the angels immediately give her commands about worship. This teaches us something essential: no matter how honored you become in Allah's sight, you never graduate from the need to worship, to humble yourself before Him.

**يَـٰمَرْيَمُ ٱقْنُتِى لِرَبِّكِ** - "O Maryam, be devoutly obedient to your Lord." The word ٱقْنُتِى means to be ready, willing, and subservient in obedience. It implies standing in devotion and being constantly prepared to fulfill what your Master asks of you.

**وَٱسْجُدِى** - "And prostrate." Make sujud, which can be done both inside and outside of Salah.

**وَٱرْكَعِى مَعَ ٱلرَّٰكِعِينَ** - "And bow with those who bow." Now here's something interesting: why does Allah ﷻ say "with those who make ruku'" rather than just "make ruku'"?

Remember where Maryam lived - in the masjid, in the sanctuary. The رُكُوع (bowing) specifically happens within Salah, within congregational prayer. While sujud can be performed individually at any time, ruku' is specifically part of the formal prayer. So Allah ﷻ is highlighting the congregational aspect - make ruku' with those who are making ruku', join in the congregation, be part of the community worship in the masjid where you live.

translation: That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you, [O Muhammad]. And you were not with them when they cast their pens as to which of them should be responsible for Mary. Nor were you with them when they disputed
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ ۚ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَـٰمَهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ

That is from the news of the unseen which We reveal to you, [O Muhammad]. And you were not with them when they cast their pens as to which of them should be responsible for Maryam. Nor were you with them when they disputed.

### The Proof of Divine Revelation

Allah ﷻ now addresses the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ directly, shifting from the narrative to commentary about the narrative itself. ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ أَنۢبَآءِ ٱلْغَيْبِ - "That is from the news of the unseen." The word أَنۢبَآء means news, but specifically important news, significant reports. These aren't just stories - they're consequential historical accounts that carry weight and meaning.

نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ - "We reveal it to you." This is revelation - وَحْي. Allah ﷻ is making it clear that what the Prophet ﷺ just recited came directly from Him. This is Allah's power of hearing perfectly, recording perfectly, knowing perfectly, and then delivering and teaching perfectly through His messenger.

### The Historical Details You Couldn't Have Known

Then Allah ﷻ presents the evidence: وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَـٰمَهُمْ - "And you were not with them when they cast their pens." The Prophet ﷺ wasn't present when the religious scholars and custodians of the temple threw their pens into the water to determine who would take care of Maryam. This was their method of drawing lots - they would cast their pens into a river or pond, and whoever's pen floated would be given the responsibility.

أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ - "As to which of them should be responsible for Maryam." Notice the verb here: يَكْفُلُ (should take charge/be responsible). This is different from the verb used earlier: كَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا (Allah put her in the care of Zakariya). When the people were competing, they were asking who should "take charge". But when Allah ﷻ describes what actually happened, He uses the intensive form (kaffala) because it was ultimately Allah ﷻ who put Zakariya in charge of her. The people may have had their competition, but Allah ﷻ had already determined the outcome.

وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ - "Nor were you with them when they disputed." The verb يَخْتَصِمُونَ means they were arguing with each other, having a dispute. This was a very secretive, internal matter among the religious elite.

### The Significance for the People of the Book

This level of detail was not common knowledge, even among the Christians of Najran. The Christian tradition was different from the Jewish tradition. While Jews had their legal scholarship and Mosaic law that was studied by many, Christians were even more disconnected from their scripture. In the Christian tradition of that time, you needed almost classified access - high-level security clearance - before you could study the texts directly. The common masses of Christians did not know the book directly.

These kinds of historical details about the internal workings of the priesthood, about how they argued over Maryam's custodianship, about the casting of lots - this was done by the higher order of priests. This wasn't publicly known information.

So when learned Christian ministers and scholars from Najran heard from the Prophet's ﷺ mouth that there was an argument about the custodianship of Maryam, that the angels came to Zakariya (AS) and told him about Yahya (AS) and his mission to assist Isa (AS), they recognized these as details they themselves had learned in their advanced religious training. They didn't have easy access to this information even with their years of study, yet this man who never studied with their teachers, who had no access to their restricted texts, was giving them this news with perfect accuracy.

Allah ﷻ emphasizes this point: مَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ - "You were not there at all." Not even present. وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ - "You weren't there" when they were throwing their pens. These are minute details of historical events. And then Allah ﷻ adds the argument itself - that's an even smaller detail of what happened. The Prophet ﷺ is reciting information that could only come from divine revelation, information that serves as undeniable proof of the truth of his message.

translation: [And mention] when the angels said, "O Mary, indeed Allah gives you good tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Jesus, the son of Mary - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near [to Allah]
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ ٱسْمُهُ ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ وَجِيهًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ

When the angels said, "O Maryam, indeed Allah ﷻ gives you glad tidings of a word from Him, whose name will be the Messiah, Isa, the son of Maryam - distinguished in this world and the Hereafter and among those brought near."

The angels begin by delivering glad tidings to Maryam (AS), but they immediately clarify the source of this news. They say إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ (indeed Allah ﷻ gives you glad tidings). The angels are not congratulating her from themselves; they are delivering congratulations from Allah ﷻ Himself. This distinction is significant because it establishes that the message originates from the Divine.

The announcement centers on بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ (a word from Him). This phrasing connects directly to Maryam's earlier task when she was told to confirm a word from Allah ﷻ. Now she is being told that she will give birth to that very word. Isa (AS) is the manifestation of Allah's word - the miracle of his birth represents the power of divine speech becoming reality.

The name given is ٱلْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ (the Messiah, Isa, the son of Maryam). The title Al-Maseeh, often rendered as "Messiah" or "the Anointed One" in English, carries deep significance in Arabic that we will explore when examining the nature of his mission. What stands out immediately is the identification ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ - the son of Maryam. In Arab society, lineage was traced through the father. A person was always known as the son of their father, never the son of their mother. By identifying Isa (AS) as the son of Maryam, the Quran establishes from the very beginning that this child's birth will be extraordinary.

For Maryam (AS), the first concern upon hearing this news would naturally be her honor. When a child is born outside of marriage, both mother and child face lifelong humiliation in society. Such a child cannot "show his face" - an Arabic expression meaning to live with dignity and respect. The angels address this anxiety immediately with the word وَجِيهًا (distinguished). This word comes from the root wajh, meaning face. To have wajh in society is to have honor and respect. The angels assure Maryam (AS) that her son will be وَجِيهًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ (distinguished in this world and the Hereafter). Far from being humiliated, he will be honored in both realms.

The ayah concludes with وَمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (and among those brought near). The word muqarrabeen comes from qarraba, meaning to bring close. This describes those whom Allah ﷻ has brought close to Himself. The angels themselves are described in the Quran as المَلائِكَةُ المُقَرَّبُونَ (the angels brought near). This phrase serves multiple purposes: it indicates Isa's elevated spiritual status, and it also hints at what will happen to him physically - Allah ﷻ will raise him up, bringing him physically close, lifting him from the earth to the heavens.

translation: He will speak to the people in the cradle and in maturity and will be of the righteous
commentary:
وَيُكَلِّمُ ٱلنَّاسَ فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ وَكَهْلًا وَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

"And he will speak to the people in the cradle and in maturity and will be of the righteous."

This ayah contains a profound detail that addresses a critical belief about Isa (AS). Everything mentioned about him in these ayahs is miraculous: his birth without a father is a miracle, his honor in both worlds is miraculous, his being brought near to Allah ﷻ is miraculous. The pattern continues here, but with a subtle distinction that reveals something extraordinary.

The ayah states he will speak to people فِى ٱلْمَهْدِ (in the cradle). The word mahd refers to the cradle or the arms that hold an infant. For a baby to speak while still unable to even hold himself up, requiring the support of a cradle or his mother's arms, is clearly miraculous. No one questions this.

But then the ayah adds وَكَهْلًا (and in maturity). The word kahl refers to middle age, generally understood as ranging from the mid-thirties to around forty years old. Here's the crucial question: Is it miraculous for someone in their middle age to speak to people? Obviously not. There is nothing extraordinary about a man in his thirties or forties having conversations with others.

Why then would Allah ﷻ mention something ordinary in the midst of describing only miraculous events? The answer reveals a profound truth about Isa's fate. When we understand that Isa (AS) spoke as an infant, then the timeline of normal human life would continue until he reaches middle age. But what happened to Isa (AS)? Allah ﷻ raised him up while he was still a young man, before he reached middle age. 

The mention of speaking in middle age becomes miraculous when we realize there will be thousands of years between these two conversations - one as an infant, and one after his return to earth in the future. When Isa (AS) returns and speaks to people in his middle age, it will indeed be extraordinary because millennia will have passed between his infancy and his maturity.

The ayah concludes with وَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (and of the righteous). This phrase takes on special meaning when we consider the context of his return. When Isa (AS) comes back, whose company will he be in? The righteous believers. In his first coming, he was surrounded primarily by enemies and had only a small group of followers. Upon his return, he will lead the believers in prayer and be among the company of the righteous - those who have maintained true faith until the end of time.

translation: She said, "My Lord, how will I have a child when no man has touched me?" [The angel] said, "Such is Allah; He creates what He wills. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is
commentary:
قَالَتْ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌ وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

She said, "My Lord, how will I have a child when no man has touched me?" He said, "Such is Allah; He creates what He wills. When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, 'Be,' and it is."

When the angels spoke to Zakariya (AS) earlier, he responded by questioning how he could have a son. Now when the angels speak to Maryam (AS), notice what concerns her. She doesn't question the honors her son will receive, his ability to speak as an infant, or his elevated status. Her question is far more fundamental: أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى وَلَدٌ (how will I have a child?). The word walad literally means "one who is born" - she's asking about the very possibility of giving birth itself.

Her concern is expressed with complete purity: وَلَمْ يَمْسَسْنِى بَشَرٌ (no man has touched me). The word bashar refers to a mortal human being. She states the biological impossibility plainly - without a husband, how can birth occur?

The response she receives is profound, and Allah ﷻ carefully chooses His words. Notice that when Zakariya (AS) was told about his son, the response was كَذَٰلِكَ ٱللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ (Such is Allah; He does what He wills). The verb yaf'alu (He does) is general and could theoretically be misinterpreted. Any verb can be misconstrued, and the phrase "Allah does what He wills" could potentially be twisted to support false beliefs.

But here, with Maryam (AS), Allah ﷻ says كَذَٰلِكِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَآءُ (Such is Allah; He creates what He wills). The verb is specifically yakhluqu (He creates). This word choice is deliberate and cuts off any possibility of misinterpretation. There is no way to twist "He creates what He wills" into a claim that Allah ﷻ fathered a child. The verb "creates" makes it absolutely clear that what is being brought into existence is a creation, not offspring. 

This precision is especially important because this conversation is preserved in revelation and will be read by Christians who might try to find support for their beliefs about Jesus. By using the specific verb "creates" rather than the general "does," Allah ﷻ leaves no room for theological confusion. The response makes crystal clear that Isa (AS) is created by Allah ﷻ and therefore cannot have divine status.

The ayah then explains the mechanism of divine creation: إِذَا قَضَىٰٓ أَمْرًا فَإِنَّمَا يَقُولُ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (When He decrees a matter, He only says to it, "Be," and it is). This is the word that was mentioned at the beginning - بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ (a word from Him). Isa (AS) is the manifestation of Allah's creative word "kun" (be). When Allah ﷻ decrees something, He simply commands it to be, and it comes into existence. The birth of Isa (AS) is the ultimate demonstration of this divine power.

translation: And He will teach him writing and wisdom and the Torah and the Gospel
commentary:
وَيُعَلِّمُهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ

"And He will teach him the Book and wisdom and the Torah and the Injeel."

The miraculous nature of Isa's life continues with the knowledge Allah ﷻ will grant him. The ayah states that Allah ﷻ will teach him الْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحِكْمَةَ (the Book and wisdom). These two terms work together - Al-Kitab refers to revealed scripture, and Al-Hikmah (wisdom) refers to the deeper understanding and proper application of that scripture. In the specific context of Isa (AS), Al-Kitab refers to the Tawrah (Torah) and Al-Hikmah refers to the Injeel (Gospel).

What makes this remarkable is the depth of knowledge Isa (AS) will possess from a young age. The ayah specifies وَٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ (the Torah and the Injeel). The Torah had already been revealed to Musa (AS) centuries before. By the time of Isa (AS), had it been corrupted? Certainly. But Allah ﷻ made Isa (AS) a complete scholar of both the Torah and the Gospel from the very beginning of his mission.

This means that from his youth, Isa (AS) possessed comprehensive knowledge of what Musa (AS) had actually been given and what had been changed. Imagine the scene: Isa (AS), still young, correcting the learned rabbis of the Israelites, telling them, "That's not the correct ayah. You've changed this word here and this word there." 

Consider how humiliating this would be for a rabbi standing before his entire congregation, delivering a sermon, only to be corrected by Isa (AS) who knows both scriptures perfectly. The rabbi might be in the middle of his khutbah when Isa (AS) stops him: "That's not right. That's not what was revealed. That's a fabrication." 

This extraordinary knowledge made Isa (AS) a living witness to the corruption that had entered the previous scriptures. He could identify precisely what had been altered, what had been added, and what had been removed. This naturally made him deeply resented by the religious establishment of his time, who had built their authority on interpretations of texts they had modified.

translation: And [make him] a messenger to the Children of Israel, [who will say], 'Indeed I have come to you with a sign from your Lord in that I design for you from clay [that which is] like the form of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by permission of Allah. And I cure the blind and the leper, and I give life to the dead - by permission of Allah. And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers
commentary:
وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۖ أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

"And as a messenger to the Children of Israel, saying, 'I have come to you with a sign from your Lord in that I design for you from clay the form of a bird, then I breathe into it and it becomes a bird by permission of Allah. And I cure the blind and the leper, and I give life to the dead - by permission of Allah. And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses. Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers.'"

This ayah is crucial for multiple reasons, particularly in the conversation with Christians. Notice first the specific designation: وَرَسُولًا إِلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (And as a messenger to the Children of Israel). Allah ﷻ does not say Isa (AS) was sent to "his people" or "his nation." This is significant because to be considered part of a nation in that cultural context, one's father had to be from that nation. Lineage was traced through the paternal line.

Since Isa (AS) has no earthly father, he cannot claim Israel as his nation in the traditional sense. He doesn't say "my people" or "my nation" the way Musa (AS) and other prophets did. Instead, he refers to them as بَنِى إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (the Children of Israel) - identifying them by their ancestor Israel (Yaqub AS), but not claiming that lineage as his own. His only lineage is through his mother, which is why throughout the Quran he is consistently called Isa ibn Maryam (Isa, son of Maryam).

This directly refutes the Christian claim that Jesus came as a savior for all humanity. The Quran records his own words clearly stating his mission was specifically to the Children of Israel. There is even a similar statement preserved in some Christian texts where Jesus says he was sent for "the lost sheep of the house of Israel."

Isa (AS) then describes the signs he brings: أَنِّى قَدْ جِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ (I have come to you with a sign from your Lord). The miracles he lists are extraordinary:

First, أَنِّىٓ أَخْلُقُ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلطِّينِ كَهَيْـَٔةِ ٱلطَّيْرِ (I design for you from clay something like the form of a bird). Notice the precision: he doesn't say he makes a perfect bird, but كَهَيْـَٔةِ (something like the form of) - roughly the shape of a bird, as a sculptor might create. Then فَأَنفُخُ فِيهِ فَيَكُونُ طَيْرًۢا (I breathe into it and it becomes a bird). This clay form becomes a living, flying bird.

But immediately, the ayah adds the critical phrase: بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (by permission of Allah). This phrase appears repeatedly in this passage, and it's the phrase that was conveniently removed or de-emphasized in Christian theology. The miracles aren't performed by Isa's own divine power - they occur بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (by Allah's permission).

The miracles continue: وَأُبْرِئُ ٱلْأَكْمَهَ وَٱلْأَبْرَصَ (And I cure the blind and the leper). The word ubri' comes from the root barra', meaning to be free of something or to separate from something. Isa (AS) can heal those with al-akmah (blindness) and al-abrasa (leprosy). These were people who were often quarantined, forbidden from entering cities, isolated from society because their conditions were considered contagious and incurable.

Then the most dramatic miracle: وَأُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (and I give life to the dead - by permission of Allah). Again, the phrase بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ is repeated. These miracles parallel the creative power of Allah ﷻ, but they are explicitly performed through His permission, not through any independent divine authority of Isa (AS).

The final miracle mentioned is different in nature: وَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا تَأْكُلُونَ وَمَا تَدَّخِرُونَ فِى بُيُوتِكُمْ (And I inform you of what you eat and what you store in your houses). The verb unabi'ukum means to give thorough news or detailed information. Isa (AS) could tell people exactly what they had eaten - yesterday's meal, two days ago, what they had for breakfast that morning.

The word تَدَّخِرُونَ is particularly interesting. It comes from the root dhakhara, meaning to treasure or vault away. The word encompasses both valuable possessions and private information that people hide in their homes - money hidden under floorboards, items stashed behind panels, secrets kept in locked safes. Isa (AS) could reveal all of these hidden matters.

The ayah concludes: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لَّكُمْ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (Indeed in that is a sign for you, if you are believers). All of these miracles should be sufficient proof that he is genuinely a messenger from Allah ﷻ. These signs demand a response of faith.

Allah ﷻ Himself is witness to these words. The Quran emphasizes that Allah ﷻ is شَهِيدَ (a witness) to justice, and it is unjust to misquote what Isa (AS) actually said. Perhaps in the vaults and archives of those who possess true knowledge of the original scriptures, records of these actual statements remain - statements that clearly identify the miracles as being performed بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ (by Allah's permission), not through independent divine power.

translation: And [I have come] confirming what was before me of the Torah and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you. And I have come to you with a sign from your Lord, so fear Allah and obey me
commentary:
وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ ۚ وَجِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ

"And confirming what was before me of the Torah and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you. And I have come to you with a sign from your Lord, so fear Allah and obey me."

Isa (AS) clarifies the relationship between his message and what came before. وَمُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ مِنَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ (And confirming what was before me of the Torah). The phrase مَا بَيْنَ يَدَىَّ (what is between my hands) is an old Arabic expression meaning "what is directly in front of me" - what is immediately accessible and present. When something is between your hands, you cannot avoid it or go around it; it is right there before you.

Isa (AS) came to confirm the Torah that was directly accessible to him - meaning he came to affirm the original revelation given to Musa (AS), even as he corrected the corruptions that had entered it.

His second purpose: وَلِأُحِلَّ لَكُم بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى حُرِّمَ عَلَيْكُمْ (and to make lawful for you some of what was forbidden to you). The word حُرِّمَ is in the passive voice - "it was made forbidden." The ayah doesn't specify who did the forbidding, and this ambiguity is meaningful.

There are two possibilities: Some things were genuinely made haram by Allah ﷻ, and now through Isa (AS), Allah ﷻ was lifting those prohibitions for the Children of Israel. Other things were made haram by the people themselves - prohibitions they invented that had no divine origin. Isa (AS) came to clarify which was which and to make permissible again what they had wrongly forbidden.

The Quran gives us an example of the latter category. The Children of Israel are named after their patriarch Yaqub (AS), whose other name was Israel. He personally did not like camel meat - perhaps he found the taste unpleasant, or it disagreed with him, or he had some other personal reason for avoiding it. This was simply his individual preference. But the Children of Israel turned his personal dietary choice into a religious law. They declared camel meat haram for everyone, even though Allah ﷻ had made it halal.

Later in the Quran, Allah ﷻ addresses this directly: "All food was lawful for the Children of Israel except what Israel made haram upon himself before the Torah was revealed." The prohibition had no divine basis - it was based solely on Yaqub's personal preference. Isa (AS) came to correct such man-made restrictions and return people to what Allah ﷻ had actually made permissible.

This point is particularly important because this conversation is ultimately with Christians. What did Christian theology do with the concept of haram and halal? They essentially eliminated it entirely. The claim became that the blood of Jesus cleanses believers from all sins and all prohibitions - the Sabbath restrictions, the prohibition of pork, all dietary laws were declared void because Jesus had "paid for" all transgressions. Everything became permissible.

But Isa (AS) himself said بَعْضَ ٱلَّذِى حُرِّمَ (some of what was forbidden) - not all of it, only some. He came to lift certain prohibitions, not to abolish the entire concept of halal and haram. This is a fundamental difference between what the Quran records and what Christian theology teaches.

The ayah concludes with the essential message: وَجِئْتُكُم بِـَٔايَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (And I have come to you with a sign from your Lord, so fear Allah and obey me). After all the miracles and proofs, what does Isa (AS) ask for? Two things, and they are interconnected: فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (fear Allah - have taqwa of Allah, be conscious of Him, protect yourselves from His displeasure) and وَأَطِيعُونِ (and obey me).

These two commands are actually one and the same. To obey the messenger is to manifest taqwa. To have taqwa is expressed through obeying the messenger. This is the summary of what all messengers asked from their people. If you study Surah Ash-Shu'ara, you'll find this same request repeated by prophet after prophet: "Fear Allah and obey me." It's the fundamental call of every messenger - not worship of the messenger, not devotion to the messenger's person, but devotion to Allah ﷻ expressed through following the guidance the messenger brings.

This is the pure message of Isa (AS): awareness of Allah ﷻ and obedience to His commands as delivered through His messenger. It leaves no room for the later theological innovations that would transform a messenger into an object of worship.

translation: Indeed, Allah is my Lord and your Lord, so worship Him. That is the straight path
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُوهُ ۗ هَـٰذَا صِرَٰطٌ مُّسْتَقِيمٌ

Prophet Isa (AS) continues his declaration to the Children of Israel, making the core message of his mission absolutely clear. He proclaims: "No doubt, Allah ﷻ is my Master and your Master as well, so enslave yourselves to Him and worship Him. This is the straight path."

This ayah captures the essence of what Isa (AS) came to convey. He declares Allah ﷻ as both his own Rabb and the Rabb of his people. The word رَبّ signifies not just a lord, but a master who nurtures, sustains, and has authority. By declaring Allah ﷻ as his own Master first, Isa (AS) establishes his position as a servant before calling others to servitude.

The command فَٱعْبُدُوهُ (so worship Him) follows naturally from this declaration. True worship can only be directed to the One who is truly Rabb. The demonstrative هَـٰذَا (this) emphasizes that this message, this path of pure monotheism, is the الصِّرَٰطُ ٱلْمُسْتَقِيمُ (siratul mustaqim - the straight path). Not a straight path among many, but THE straight path, singular and unmistakable.

translation: But when Jesus felt [persistence in] disbelief from them, he said, "Who are my supporters for [the cause of] Allah?" The disciples said, "We are supporters for Allah. We have believed in Allah and testify that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]
commentary:
۞ فَلَمَّآ أَحَسَّ عِيسَىٰ مِنْهُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ قَالَ مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ ٱلْحَوَارِيُّونَ نَحْنُ أَنصَارُ ٱللَّهِ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ

The word أَحَسَّ carries a powerful meaning - to sense something intensely, to feel the weight of it. When Isa (AS) sensed the disbelief from among the Israelites, particularly from the rabbis of Bani Israel who opposed his message, he called out: "Who will be my helpers to Allah?"

This phrase مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ is profound. The نُصْرَة (aid) he seeks is not just general help, but specifically aid in times of conflict. Unlike other words for help in Arabic that can apply to peaceful situations, nusra implies support during opposition and struggle. The phrase "to Allah" indicates that this mission isn't merely important because it's Allah's path - it's a journey that leads to Allah ﷻ Himself. Every step taken in service of this cause brings one closer to the Divine.

The الْحَوَارِيُّونَ answered his call. This word has a beautiful origin - it comes from the root meaning to wash or cleanse. Some scholars note that hawariyyun referred to fishermen and clothes washers who worked by the riverbank in Jerusalem. Isa (AS) would frequent these areas, approaching these humble laborers at the lower end of the social and economic spectrum. His message to them was profound: "You've learned how to wash clothes - let me teach you how to wash your hearts."

These hawariyyun responded with conviction: "We are the helpers of Allah. We have believed in Allah." Then they made a crucial request: وَٱشْهَدْ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ ("Bear witness that we are Muslims"). The concept of testimony and bearing witness remains central in Christian tradition to this day, though its meaning has been altered over time. They asked Isa (AS) to witness their acceptance of Islam, their submission to Allah ﷻ.

translation: Our Lord, we have believed in what You revealed and have followed the messenger Jesus, so register us among the witnesses [to truth]
commentary:
رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا بِمَآ أَنزَلْتَ وَٱتَّبَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَ فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ

After asking Isa (AS) to bear witness to their faith, the hawariyyun immediately turned to Allah ﷻ directly. This pattern is significant and recurring in Surah Aal-E-Imran - first there's interaction with a messenger or angel, but then the believers turn directly to Allah ﷻ in conversation. This shows they understood that while Isa (AS) was their teacher and guide, he was not the only one they could converse with. Their direct access to Allah ﷻ remained.

They declared: "Our Master, we have believed in what You sent down." The word رَبَّنَآ (Our Lord) shows intimacy and recognition of Allah's nurturing authority. Then comes وَٱتَّبَعْنَا ٱلرَّسُولَ ("and we have followed the Messenger"). The verb اتَّبَعَ indicates more than mere following - it implies voluntary, loving, passionate pursuit. They didn't just acknowledge the messenger; they went out of their way to follow him completely.

Their final request was: فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ ("Write us among those who bore witness"). They asked Isa (AS) to witness their Islam, and now they ask Allah ﷻ to record their names among الشَّـٰهِدِينَ - those who testify to the truth. This is a request for their testimony to be permanently recorded, recognized by Allah ﷻ Himself.

translation: And the disbelievers planned, but Allah planned. And Allah is the best of planners
commentary:
وَمَكَرُوا۟ وَمَكَرَ ٱللَّهُ ۖ وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلْمَـٰكِرِينَ

The response to this call for helpers wasn't just spiritual opposition - it manifested in active plotting. The context is crucial: the Children of Israel at this time lived under a puppet Jewish government controlled by the Romans. When the verse states وَمَكَرُوا۟ ("and they plotted"), it refers to a calculated conspiracy.

The word مَكْر in Arabic describes a deceptive plan, a scheme designed to make the enemy think they're winning while actually leading them toward loss. It's never used in a positive sense ordinarily - it implies conniving and conspiracy. The plot against Isa (AS) involved fellow believers who had turned traitor, planning to make him say something that would sound like treason to the Roman authorities. The goal was to get him arrested and eventually executed for crimes against the state.

A contemporary parallel would be spreading accusations that a Muslim scholar is a traitor to his country, getting him arrested on false charges. This kind of internal betrayal isn't new - it happened to Isa (AS) as well.

The verse uses makr twice: وَمَكَرُوا۟ وَمَكَرَ ٱللَّهُ ("They plotted and Allah plotted"). This repetition emphasizes two things: first, that there were two different plots operating simultaneously, and second, that Allah's response would be of the same nature - a counter-plot. But then comes the decisive statement: وَٱللَّهُ خَيْرُ ٱلْمَـٰكِرِينَ ("And Allah is the best of planners"). Whatever deception they attempted, Allah ﷻ is superior in planning and execution.

translation: [Mention] when Allah said, "O Jesus, indeed I will take you and raise you to Myself and purify you from those who disbelieve and make those who follow you [in submission to Allah alone] superior to those who disbelieve until the Day of Resurrection. Then to Me is your return, and I will judge between you concerning that in which you used to differ
commentary:
إِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَىٰٓ إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۖ ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

We don't have explicit details in the Quran or authentic Sunnah describing exactly what happened at the end of Isa's (AS) earthly mission. The Quran provides a brief account, and the closest historical text that seems to align with the Islamic understanding is the Gospel of Barnabas, which was preserved in the Vatican library for centuries. According to that account, there was a traitor among the hawariyyun who agreed to identify Isa (AS) to the Romans. The plan was for this traitor to go inside where Isa (AS) was staying and then emerge calling him "my master" so the soldiers would know whom to arrest.

However, when he entered, four angels descended, broke through the roof, and raised Isa (AS) up while Allah ﷻ transformed the traitor's face to resemble Isa (AS). When he emerged in shock, some of the hawariyyun called out "our master" to him, and he was taken and crucified in place of Isa (AS). This account aligns most closely with what the Quran reveals.

Allah ﷻ declares: إِنِّى مُتَوَفِّيكَ ("I am taking you fully"). The word تَوَفِّي comes from the root وَفَى, which means to fulfill, to complete, to take in full. When used about payment, it means to pay someone their complete due. When used about taking, it means to take completely and entirely.

Some groups, like the Ahmadiyya, have argued that mutawaffika necessarily means "causing you to die." However, the linguistic evidence shows otherwise. The root wafa means to give in full or take in full. When Allah ﷻ takes souls at death, the Quran uses this terminology because the soul is taken. Here, Allah ﷻ says He is taking Isa (AS) entirely - body and soul - implying something beyond ordinary death.

The proof that tawaffi doesn't inherently mean death comes from the Quran itself. In Surah Al-An'am, the phrase حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّاهُ ٱلْمَوْتُ appears - "until death takes them." If tawaffi itself meant death, this phrase would be redundant, saying "until death deaths them." The fact that الْمَوْتُ (death) is mentioned as the subject doing the taking proves that tawaffi means "to take fully," not necessarily "to cause to die."

Then Allah ﷻ clarifies: وَرَافِعُكَ إِلَىَّ ("and raising you to Me"). The word رَفْع (raf') means to elevate, to raise up. Some commentators have tried to interpret this metaphorically, suggesting it means raising in rank or spiritual status. However, this interpretation is problematic. When the Quran speaks of raising in rank, it explicitly mentions rank: نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَاتٍ ("We raise in degrees"). When Allah ﷻ says the mountain was raised above the Children of Israel, it wasn't raised in rank - it was physically elevated. The straightforward meaning here is physical elevation toward Allah ﷻ.

Next comes وَمُطَهِّرُكَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ("and purifying you from those who disbelieved"). Allah ﷻ is removing Isa (AS) completely from the company of the disbelievers. The first time he had a group of followers, there was treachery within. When he returns, his company will be entirely pure believers.

Then comes a challenging phrase: وَجَاعِلُ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوكَ فَوْقَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ("and placing those who followed you above those who disbelieved until the Day of Resurrection").

Some scholars have interpreted this to mean Christians will be above Jews in worldly power and dominance. They cite historical events like the destruction of Jerusalem by Nebuchadnezzar seventy years after Isa (AS), the Spanish Inquisition's persecution of Jews, and the Holocaust as evidence of Christian superiority over Jewish communities.

However, this interpretation faces a significant problem. The verb اتَّبَعَ (to follow) in the Quran, when attributed to a prophet, refers to those who genuinely follow the prophet's message as it was meant to be followed. While people might follow desires, true ittiba' of a prophet means obedience to his actual teachings. 

The Christians, despite their love for Isa (AS), committed the very شِرْك (associating partners with Allah) that he warned against. Making Isa (AS) divine or part of a trinity is the opposite of what he taught. The last thing Isa (AS) wanted was to be worshipped, yet that became the central deviation. Calling Christians "followers of Isa" when they fundamentally rejected his core message of Tawheed is problematic.

The more convincing interpretation is that this refers to the true followers Isa (AS) will have when he returns near the end of time. When he descends again, he will lead actual believers - Muslims who follow his true message. Those followers will be placed above the disbelievers in the final battles before the Day of Judgment, in what Christians call Armageddon. Isa (AS) will lead the armies of faith against the armies of the Dajjal (the False Messiah), and Allah ﷻ will grant him decisive victory.

The phrase إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ can mean "until the Day of Resurrection" or "approaching the Day of Resurrection," since إِلَىٰ sometimes indicates proximity. This suggests these events occur close to the Day of Judgment.

ثُمَّ إِلَىَّ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ("Then to Me alone is your return"). The word مَرْجِع encompasses both the place and time of return. Allah ﷻ emphasizes that ultimately, everyone returns to Him alone.

فَأَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ ("Then I will judge between you in all that you used to differ about"). All the disputes about Isa (AS) - whether he was God, the son of God, a prophet, or whether he died or was raised - all of these disagreements will be settled by Allah's judgment.

translation: And as for those who disbelieved, I will punish them with a severe punishment in this world and the Hereafter, and they will have no helpers
commentary:
فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ declares the fate of those who rejected Isa (AS): فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ فَأُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذَابًا شَدِيدًا ("As for those who disbelieved, I will punish them with an intense punishment").

This punishment spans فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ("in this world and the Hereafter"). When we think of Allah's punishment, we often imagine nations being destroyed collectively - floods, earthquakes, armies being swallowed by the earth. But Allah's punishment isn't limited to dramatic collective destruction. He can punish intensely at the individual level as well.

Personal punishment can manifest as chronic illness, mental anguish, emotional torment, or deep grief. Modern psychology recognizes that emotional and psychological suffering can exceed physical pain in intensity. Those who immediately rejected Isa (AS) may not have witnessed a collective catastrophe like the destruction that came to Jerusalem seventy years later. But Allah ﷻ could have pursued each one individually with various forms of tribulation and torment.

وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ ("And they will have no helpers whatsoever"). The word نَاصِر (helper, supporter) echoes back to the question Isa (AS) asked: مَنْ أَنصَارِىٓ ("Who are my helpers?"). The hawariyyun answered that call. But those who rejected him will find themselves utterly without support when Allah's punishment comes.

translation: But as for those who believed and did righteous deeds, He will give them in full their rewards, and Allah does not like the wrongdoers
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

In contrast to the fate of the disbelievers, Allah ﷻ describes what awaits the believers: وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ("And as for those who believed and did righteous deeds").

The promise is فَيُوَفِّيهِمْ أُجُورَهُمْ ("He will pay them their rewards in full"). Notice the return of the root وَفَى - to fulfill completely. Just as Allah ﷻ took Isa (AS) completely (mutawaffika), He will pay the believers their compensation completely (yuwaffihim). There will be no shortchanging, no reduction. Every good deed, every struggle, every sacrifice will be accounted for and rewarded in full.

Then comes a profound statement: وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ ("And Allah does not love the wrongdoers"). This addresses a question that troubles many believers: why do wrongdoers seem to prosper? Why does evil appear to flourish in the world?

The answer is implicit here - when you see wrongdoers succeeding temporarily, it's not because Allah ﷻ loves them or approves of their actions. They're being given respite, an extension of time, but this should not be confused with divine pleasure or approval. The believers will be paid in full, and that's what matters. Don't be misled by the temporary success of those who do wrong. Allah ﷻ doesn't love wrongdoing or wrongdoers, and His justice will ultimately prevail.

translation: This is what We recite to you, [O Muhammad], of [Our] verses and the precise [and wise] message
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ مِنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ وَٱلذِّكْرِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ

The passage concludes with Allah ﷻ addressing the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ directly: ذَٰلِكَ نَتْلُوهُ عَلَيْكَ ("That is what We recite to you").

The demonstrative ذَٰلِكَ (that) refers to everything that came before - the story of Isa (AS), his mission, the response of the hawariyyun, the plots against him, his raising, and the ultimate fate of believers and disbelievers. All of this is being recited to the Prophet ﷺ.

مِنَ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ("from the signs") - this account is among the miraculous signs of the Quran. The Prophet ﷺ had no access to these historical details, no libraries to consult, no scholars of previous scriptures teaching him. Yet the Quran reveals these events with clarity and truth.

وَٱلذِّكْرِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ ("and the wise reminder"). This is one of the beautiful descriptions of the Quran - it is both ذِكْر (reminder, remembrance) and حَكِيم (wise, full of wisdom). It reminds us of truths we need to know and does so with profound wisdom.

The Quran doesn't just recount history for entertainment. Every story carries wisdom, every detail has purpose. The account of Isa (AS) reminds the believers of the pattern of prophetic struggle, the nature of true faith, the reality of divine protection, and the certainty of ultimate justice. This is the ذِكْر حَكِيم - a reminder that transforms hearts and minds with its wisdom.

translation: Indeed, the example of Jesus to Allah is like that of Adam. He created Him from dust; then He said to him, "Be," and he was
commentary:
إِنَّ مَثَلَ عِيسَىٰ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ كَمَثَلِ ءَادَمَ ۖ خَلَقَهُۥ مِن تُرَابٍ ثُمَّ قَالَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ

The example of Isa (AS) in the sight of Allah ﷻ is just like the example of Adam (AS). Allah ﷻ created Adam (AS) from dust, from dirt, and then said to him "Be," and he came into existence.

Allah ﷻ presents this powerful analogy to address the Christian claim of Isa's (AS) divinity. If the Christians believe Isa (AS) is divine because he was born without a father, then Adam (AS) has twice the right to be considered divine—he had neither mother nor father. This is an undeniable logical argument that exposes the flaw in attributing divinity based on miraculous birth.

When engaging with this argument in conversation, you may encounter what has become a standard response in Christian theology: "It's the mystery of faith." This phrase is often used to avoid rational examination of beliefs that cannot be defended logically. When faced with clear contradictions or questions that expose inconsistencies, the retreat to "mystery" becomes the final defense. The attitude becomes: "It doesn't make sense, but that's what makes it incredible. Don't you see? Can't you feel it?" This emotional appeal replaces intellectual engagement.

However, Allah ﷻ is calling people to use their intellect, not abandon it. Faith in Islam is built on a foundation that can withstand scrutiny, that welcomes questions, and that never asks believers to check their minds at the door.

translation: The truth is from your Lord, so do not be among the doubters
commentary:
ٱلْحَقُّ مِن رَّبِّكَ فَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْمُمْتَرِينَ

This is the truth from your Master, so do not be among those who fall into doubt.

The word الْمُمْتَرِينَ refers to a specific type of doubt. Arabic has several words for doubt — شك , ريب , لبس, and مرية. The term miryah implies that there's a concrete fact, something you're absolutely certain of, yet through someone's philosophical arguments, twists and turns, and convoluted suggestions, you start falling into doubt about something that should be clear.

Allah ﷻ warns against this because when you engage in discussion with those defending indefensible positions, they often employ circular reasoning and false comparisons. A common example is the Trinity being compared to an egg—the shell, the white, and the yolk are all one egg. Or water existing as liquid, ice, and vapor yet still being H2O. These comparisons might sound clever at first, but they fundamentally violate a core Islamic principle: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ—"There is nothing like Him."

We don't compare Allah ﷻ to creation. When we look at creation, we appreciate His creative power, what He's capable of doing, not who He is in His essence. Comparing God to created things was actually considered blasphemous in traditional Christian scholarship, though such arguments have become commonplace in contemporary religious discourse.

The message is clear: don't let convoluted arguments shake what is fundamentally true and clear.

translation: Then whoever argues with you about it after [this] knowledge has come to you - say, "Come, let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, ourselves and yourselves, then supplicate earnestly [together] and invoke the curse of Allah upon the liars [among us]
commentary:
فَمَنْ حَآجَّكَ فِيهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَكَ مِنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ فَقُلْ تَعَالَوْا۟ نَدْعُ أَبْنَآءَنَا وَأَبْنَآءَكُمْ وَنِسَآءَنَا وَنِسَآءَكُمْ وَأَنفُسَنَا وَأَنفُسَكُمْ ثُمَّ نَبْتَهِلْ فَنَجْعَل لَّعْنَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ

Whoever argues with you about this matter after knowledge has come to you, say: "Come, let us call our sons and your sons, our women and your women, ourselves and yourselves—then let us pray earnestly and invoke the curse of Allah ﷻ upon the liars."

This ayah addresses what happened after weeks of debate with the Christian delegation from Najran. These Christians stayed as honored guests inside Masjid an-Nabawi for an extended period, debating matters of faith with the Prophet ﷺ himself. Think about what this means: people openly declaring beliefs that Allah ﷻ describes elsewhere as so offensive that the heavens and earth are nearly torn apart—the claim that He has taken a son—yet they were honored guests inside the masjid. They debated respectfully for weeks.

Today, we struggle to have healthy disagreements between Muslims who all believe in the Oneness of Allah ﷻ and the Prophethood of Muhammad ﷺ without voices being raised or worse. We have much to learn about the ethics of discourse from the Seerah, about how to engage with those who fundamentally disagree with us.

After these extended discussions, Allah ﷻ commanded His Messenger ﷺ to issue this final challenge: a مباهلة, a mutual invocation of curse. The word ابتهل comes from the root meaning "to leave someone in their state"—when you've reached an impasse in argument and you say, "Fine, stay where you are." But ابتهال takes this to the ultimate level: "Let's call upon Allah ﷻ to curse the liars among us."

The Prophet ﷺ said, "Bring your children, your women, your entire community. We'll bring ours. Every man, woman, and child will put their lives at stake for this faith. We're ready to invoke Allah's wrath on ourselves if we're lying."

This wasn't something the Prophet ﷺ decided on his own—Allah ﷻ commanded him to do this. It's not something we're licensed to do; this was a specific divine intervention reserved for the Messenger ﷺ. The Christians agreed to meet the next day. They never showed up. Had they shown up, as reported in the hadith, there wouldn't have been any of them left on the face of the earth.

This was a final proof, a last decisive argument that closed the door on further debate.

translation: Indeed, this is the true narration. And there is no deity except Allah. And indeed, Allah is the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْقَصَصُ ٱلْحَقُّ ۚ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

Indeed, this is the true narrative, and there is no deity except Allah ﷻ, and indeed Allah ﷻ is the Almighty, the All-Wise.

Allah ﷻ declares: هَـٰذَا لَهُوَ ٱلْقَصَصُ ٱلْحَقُّ—this is undoubtedly the true narration. This is what really happened with Adam (AS), this is the reality of what happened with Isa (AS), and this is what's recorded in history accurately.

Then comes the fundamental declaration: وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ. Notice the phrasing—it uses مَا مِنْ, which indicates a response to a claim. When someone says there are other gods besides Allah ﷻ, the response is: No, there absolutely is not anyone worthy of worship except Allah ﷻ. You have the wrong position.

And certainly Allah ﷻ is العزيز —the Ultimate Authority, the One with all power and dominance—and الحكيم—the All-Wise in everything He decrees and creates.

translation: But if they turn away, then indeed - Allah is Knowing of the corrupters
commentary:
فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ

If they turn away, then indeed Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of those who cause corruption.

If they turn away—whether this refers to the Christian delegation specifically or anyone who turns away from this clear truth—Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge of المفسدين, those who cause corruption.

The term is significant. Allah ﷻ doesn't just call them corrupt; He calls them causers of corruption. Why? Because these are religious leaders. Deep inside, they know the truth, yet they refuse to accept it. And leaders don't just hold personal religious views—they're the ones who disseminate those views to entire societies. Their refusal becomes a source of corruption that spreads through communities and across generations. This is why their accountability is greater.

translation: Say, "O People of the Scripture, come to a word that is equitable between us and you - that we will not worship except Allah and not associate anything with Him and not take one another as lords instead of Allah." But if they turn away, then say, "Bear witness that we are Muslims [submitting to Him]
commentary:
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ تَعَالَوْا۟ إِلَىٰ كَلِمَةٍ سَوَآءٍۭ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ وَلَا نُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُولُوا۟ ٱشْهَدُوا۟ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ

Say: "O People of the Book, come to a word that is equitable between us and you—that we worship none but Allah ﷻ, that we associate no partners with Him, and that none of us shall take others as lords besides Allah ﷻ." And if they turn away, then say: "Bear witness that we are Muslims."

The address shifts to أهل الكتاب — People of the Book. This doesn't simply mean Jews and Christians in general. It specifically refers to those who are educated in their religion, those who have knowledge of the scriptures. Among both Jews and Christians are unlettered people, but here Allah ﷻ addresses those who have studied their texts, the learned ones.

The call is to كلمة سواء— a word, a statement, a principle that is equal between us. Come to common ground: أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ—that we worship none except Allah ﷻ.

A modern Christian might object, "Our book doesn't say we worship Allah. That's a Muslim thing." But the Arabic rendition of the Bible is ancient. Some of the earliest Christians were Arab Christians, and they used the word الله (Allah) for God long before any European Christian used the word "God"—which itself comes from the German "Gut." The word Allah is original to Christian tradition in the Arabic-speaking world. When they ask, "Who is this Allah?" the answer is: ask your own scholars, read your own Arabic scriptures. The Arabic Bible, still available today, is full of the word Allah.

The conditions continue: وَلَا نُشْرِكَ بِهِۦ شَيْـًٔا—that we associate no partners with Him whatsoever. And وَلَا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنَا بَعْضًا أَرْبَابًا مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ—that we don't take each other as lords besides Allah ﷻ.

What does it mean to take others as lords? When Adiyy ibn Hatim (RA), whose father was the legendary Hatim at-Ta'i known for his generosity, heard this ayah, he was confused. He had been Christian before accepting Islam and asked the Prophet ﷺ, "What does it mean that we took each other as lords?" The Prophet ﷺ explained: "Didn't you have rabbis and priests who, when they said something was forbidden, you considered it forbidden, and when they said something was permissible, you considered it permissible? They made halal and haram for you—that's what that is. You took legislators besides Allah ﷻ, lawgivers besides Allah ﷻ."

Another manifestation of this is the veneration of saints to the point where they're given divine attributes—this saint protects my house, that saint keeps my car safe, another brings wealth. This is taking others as lords besides Allah ﷻ.

If after all this they still turn away, then simply declare: اشْهَدُوا۟ بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ—"Bear witness that we are Muslims."

These words echo what the true followers of Isa (AS) said when they declared to Allah ﷻ: "Bear witness that we are Muslims" (3:52). By repeating these words, Muslims affirm they are the true followers of Isa (AS) because they say what his real followers said.

translation: O People of the Scripture, why do you argue about Abraham while the Torah and the Gospel were not revealed until after him? Then will you not reason
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تُحَآجُّونَ فِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَتِ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ وَٱلْإِنجِيلُ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِۦٓ ۚ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

O People of the Book, why do you argue about Ibrahim (AS) when the Torah and the Gospel were not revealed until after him? Do you not use reason?

Again, أهل الكتاب refers to people of knowledge, those with scholarly understanding of scripture. The question is direct: Why are you trying to win a debate about Ibrahim (AS)? Why are you arguing whether he was a Jew or a Christian?

The absurdity is obvious: the Torah and Gospel were revealed long after Ibrahim (AS). He's the great ancestor, the forefather in the lineage that eventually led to Musa (AS) and Isa (AS). How can he be categorized by religious labels that didn't exist until centuries after his time?

أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ—Why don't you use your intellect? Why don't you think about this logically?

translation: Here you are - those who have argued about that of which you have [some] knowledge, but why do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge? And Allah knows, while you know not
commentary:
هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ حَـٰجَجْتُمْ فِيمَا لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ فَلِمَ تُحَآجُّونَ فِيمَا لَيْسَ لَكُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

You are the very ones who argued about matters of which you have some knowledge, so why do you argue about that of which you have no knowledge? Allah ﷻ knows, and you do not know.

This ayah makes a crucial distinction. Look, you've been arguing about Isa (AS), about Zakariyya (AS)—at least you know something about them. At least there's some knowledge you possess about these matters, even if your conclusions are wrong.

But Ibrahim (AS)? You have no knowledge of him at all. Where do you get off arguing about something completely outside your knowledge base?

وَٱللَّهُ يَعْلَمُ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ—Allah ﷻ in fact knows, and you don't know at all. This is a humbling reminder that there are limits to human knowledge, and claiming authority over matters beyond one's understanding is intellectual dishonesty.

translation: Abraham was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was one inclining toward truth, a Muslim [submitting to Allah]. And he was not of the polytheists
commentary:
مَا كَانَ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ يَهُودِيًّا وَلَا نَصْرَانِيًّا وَلَـٰكِن كَانَ حَنِيفًا مُّسْلِمًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

Ibrahim (AS) was neither a Jew nor a Christian, but he was a Hanif, a Muslim, and he was not of the polytheists.

Allah ﷻ settles the matter definitively: Ibrahim (AS) was neither Jewish nor Christian. Rather, he was a حنيف — one who is solely dedicated, focused entirely on Allah ﷻ without distraction. He was a Muslim—one in complete submission to Allah ﷻ.

The term Hanif captures beautiful focus and sincerity. When someone makes an intent for something and remains undistracted by anything else, completely focused on their purpose, that's being Hanif. Ibrahim (AS) was that kind of Muslim—entirely devoted to Allah ﷻ with singular focus.

وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ—And he was not of the polytheists.

This is significant because at the time of revelation, there were three groups all claiming some allegiance to Ibrahim (AS): the Jews, the Christians, and the polytheistic Arabs. All three, in some way, claimed ownership or connection to him. Allah ﷻ takes him from all three groups. He wasn't Jewish, he wasn't Christian, and he certainly wasn't a polytheist. He was a Muslim.

translation: Indeed, the most worthy of Abraham among the people are those who followed him [in submission to Allah] and this prophet, and those who believe [in his message]. And Allah is the ally of the believers
commentary:
إِنَّ أَوْلَى ٱلنَّاسِ بِإِبْرَٰهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ ٱتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَـٰذَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Indeed, the people most worthy of Ibrahim (AS) are those who followed him, and this Prophet, and those who believe. And Allah ﷻ is the Protector of the believers.

أَوْلَى (awla) means to have the closest affiliation, the strongest connection, the most legitimate claim. The people who should have the closest friendship with Ibrahim (AS), the most loyalty to him, the truest claim to his legacy are those who actually followed him closely—those who followed his path.

Then Allah ﷻ highlights وَهَـٰذَا ٱلنَّبِىُّ—and this Prophet, Muhammad ﷺ—specifically and individually. Why is he singled out? Because he was commanded elsewhere in the Quran: فَاتَّبِعْ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَاهِيمَ—"Follow the way of Ibrahim" (16:123). He was told to take on that legacy, to carry forward that tradition.

More than that, his job was to cleanse and restore the House that Ibrahim (AS) built—the Ka'bah. For centuries it had been corrupted by idolatry. The Prophet Muhammad ﷺ fulfilled the mission of restoring pure monotheism to the place where Ibrahim (AS) and Ismail (AS) established it. That's why the one who should have the most affiliation with Ibrahim (AS) is the Prophet ﷺ, because he's fulfilling the very tradition that Ibrahim (AS) started.

And وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟—those who have come to believe, the followers of this Prophet ﷺ—they too share in this legacy.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ وَلِىُّ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—And Allah ﷻ is the Protector, the Guardian, the Friend of the believers. The true legacy of Ibrahim (AS) lives on in those who believe, and Allah ﷻ takes care of them, protects them, and supports them as He supported Ibrahim (AS) before them.

translation: A faction of the people of the Scripture wish they could mislead you. But they do not mislead except themselves, and they perceive [it] not
commentary:
وَدَّت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَوْ يُضِلُّونَكُمْ وَمَا يُضِلُّونَ إِلَّآ أَنفُسَهُمْ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ

A group from among the People of the Book desperately wish they could mislead you. The intensity of their desire is captured in the word وَدَّت, which expresses a deep longing. They want very badly to lead the believers astray from the truth. But Allah ﷻ reveals the reality of their efforts: they are only misleading themselves. When you try to extinguish the light of truth, when you try to cover it up and hide it from others, the only person who ultimately suffers from that darkness is you. They don't even realize what they're doing to themselves. They think they're protecting their position, their authority, their congregation, but in reality they're destroying their own souls.

translation: O People of the Scripture, why do you disbelieve in the verses of Allah while you witness [to their truth]
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَأَنتُمْ تَشْهَدُونَ

This ayah shifts to a direct address: "O People of the Book, why do you disbelieve in the signs of Allah ﷻ?" The previous passage in this surah was more specifically directed at the Jewish community, telling them to believe in what has been sent down as a confirmation of what they already have. Now the address becomes more comprehensive, speaking to both Jews and Christians. The question is profound: why would you engage in kufr—in covering up the truth—when you yourselves are witnesses to it?

Their status as witnesses (تَشْهَدُونَ) comes from their access to the Scripture. If they genuinely read their own books with honesty, they would see the truth confirmed there. They have the evidence right in their hands, yet they choose to reject what Allah ﷻ has revealed. This is the essence of their kufr in this context—they come into contact with the Quran, they have no real response to refute it, and yet they still refuse to accept it.

translation: O People of the Scripture, why do you confuse the truth with falsehood and conceal the truth while you know [it]
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَلْبِسُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ بِٱلْبَـٰطِلِ وَتَكْتُمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ

"O People of the Book, why do you disguise truth with falsehood?" The word تَلْبِسُونَ literally means to dress something up or clothe it, to mix and confuse. They were taking what is true and wrapping it in layers of falsehood, making it difficult for people to distinguish one from the other. And beyond just mixing truth with falsehood, they were actively hiding the truth (تَكْتُمُونَ).

The final phrase is critical: وَأَنتُمْ تَعْلَمُونَ—"while you know." This isn't innocent confusion or honest misunderstanding. This is deliberate. The masses of Jews and Christians weren't necessarily doing this consciously, but those who had knowledge and access to the Scripture—the rabbis, the scholars, the religious authorities—they knew exactly what they were doing. They were deliberately obscuring the truth to maintain their position and influence.

translation: And a faction of the People of the Scripture say [to each other], "Believe in that which was revealed to the believers at the beginning of the day and reject it at its end that perhaps they will abandon their religion
commentary:
وَقَالَت طَّآئِفَةٌ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ءَامِنُوا۟ بِٱلَّذِىٓ أُنزِلَ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَجْهَ ٱلنَّهَارِ وَٱكْفُرُوٓا۟ ءَاخِرَهُۥ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ

This ayah reveals an actual strategy employed by some among the People of the Book. A particular group said to each other: "Believe in what has been revealed to those who believe at the beginning of the day, and reject it at the end of it, so that they might return."

The historical context helps us understand this scheme. Among the Jewish community in Madinah, there were rabbis who were genuinely worried. Jews in neighboring towns were becoming Muslim, and they were literally losing their congregation. Even worse, those who hadn't left yet were becoming curious. It's like when a student suddenly runs out of a classroom—immediately, others start wondering what they're missing. One person takes that leap, and it creates curiosity in everyone else. The religious authorities saw this as a serious problem that could turn from a leak into a flood.

So they devised a plan. They would send some of their bright young rabbis to spend a day with the Prophet ﷺ. The strategy was simple but calculated: these leaders would publicly announce they were becoming Muslim at the beginning of the day. Everyone would see them accept Islam. Then at the end of the day, they would come out and say, "Look, you were all so impressed by this message, so we decided to give it a fair shot. We spent an entire day with him. Honestly? There's nothing much there. It's not really a confirmation of what we have in our books. We don't understand what you're all so worked up about. We need to come back to our faith."

The psychology was clear. These were rabbis who had given lectures, provided religious instruction, led prayers for years. That respect and influence doesn't disappear overnight. They hoped that when these same religious authorities—people their community had trusted for decades—came back and said there was nothing to Islam, it would pull some of the lost congregation back in. لَعَلَّهُمْ يَرْجِعُونَ—"so that they might return."

translation: And do not trust except those who follow your religion." Say, "Indeed, the [true] guidance is the guidance of Allah. [Do you fear] lest someone be given [knowledge] like you were given or that they would [thereby] argue with you before your Lord?" Say, "Indeed, [all] bounty is in the hand of Allah - He grants it to whom He wills. And Allah is all-Encompassing and Wise
commentary:
وَلَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِمَن تَبِعَ دِينَكُمْ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْهُدَىٰ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ أَن يُؤْتَىٰٓ أَحَدٌ مِّثْلَ مَآ أُوتِيتُمْ أَوْ يُحَآجُّوكُمْ عِندَ رَبِّكُمْ ۗ قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ ٱللَّهِ يُؤْتِيهِ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ

As these rabbis were being sent on their mission, their leaders gave them parting instructions. Remember, at that time, once someone tasted Islam, there was no letting go. You could put boulders on their chest, you could tear them apart, but they would let go of their body before they'd let go of this faith. So the instruction was serious: "Don't believe in anyone, don't listen to anyone, don't give into anyone except the one who follows your religion, your way of doing things. Make sure you hold onto your loyalties. Don't get impressed."

The construction here is significant: وَلَا تُؤْمِنُوٓا۟ إِلَّا لِمَن تَبِعَ دِينَكُمْ. The word لِ indicates they're being told not to accept, not to listen to, not to cave into anyone who isn't following their way. It's about maintaining absolute loyalty to their own system.

Allah ﷻ then commands the Messenger ﷺ to respond: قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْهُدَىٰ هُدَى ٱللَّهِ—"Say: Indeed, guidance is the guidance of Allah." This is different from a similar statement in Surah Al-Baqarah. There, the Messenger ﷺ was being told that if he were to follow their desires and compromise even slightly, he would have no helper—that Allah's guidance is the only guidance he should seek. Here, the People of the Book are being told something else: guidance itself is not your property to own or withhold. You can't say, "It was with us, and now it's with them, so if we can just get rid of it somehow..." No. Guidance belongs to Allah ﷻ. It's His property, not yours or mine.

The passage continues addressing their concerns: are they worried that someone else has been given something like what they were given? Are they afraid others will make a case against them before their Master? These things shouldn't frighten them into holding onto falsehood. قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْفَضْلَ بِيَدِ ٱللَّهِ—"Say: Indeed, all favor is in the Hand of Allah." 

This directly addresses a core issue. Allah ﷻ had previously told the Children of Israel: "I gave you preference over all nations" (2:47). That preference manifested in the continuous sending of messengers, books, and revelation. Now that favor has gone to another nation—the Muslims, who have been given the Quran. The People of the Book struggled with this: "This was our favor. Why is it going to someone else?" 

Allah ﷻ makes it clear: favor isn't yours to control. He gives it to whomever He wills. وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ—Allah is All-Encompassing and All-Knowing. He encompasses all of humanity, and His knowledge is perfect.

translation: He selects for His mercy whom He wills. And Allah is the possessor of great bounty
commentary:
يَخْتَصُّ بِرَحْمَتِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو ٱلْفَضْلِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

He specifically selects by His mercy whomever He wills. The word يَخْتَصُّ means to single out, to pick exclusively. And Allah ﷻ is the Owner of the ultimate favor. The ultimate favor referenced here is the Quran itself—the greatest gift given to humanity.

translation: And among the People of the Scripture is he who, if you entrust him with a great amount [of wealth], he will return it to you. And among them is he who, if you entrust him with a [single] silver coin, he will not return it to you unless you are constantly standing over him [demanding it]. That is because they say, "There is no blame upon us concerning the unlearned." And they speak untruth about Allah while they know [it]
commentary:
۞ وَمِنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ مَنْ إِن تَأْمَنْهُ بِقِنطَارٍ يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ إِلَيْكَ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ إِن تَأْمَنْهُ بِدِينَارٍ لَّا يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ إِلَيْكَ إِلَّا مَا دُمْتَ عَلَيْهِ قَآئِمًا ۗ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِى ٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ سَبِيلٌ وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

After these harsh words about certain groups among the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ teaches us a critical lesson about fairness and avoiding stereotypes. Even if you disagree with someone very strongly—even if there's a fundamental disagreement in faith—you cannot allow that to make you judge everyone's character the same way. A disagreement in belief is one thing; making sweeping judgments about people's character is something else entirely.

Allah ﷻ says: "Among the People of the Book is one who, if you trust him with a qintar (a huge pile of wealth—like a wheelbarrow full of gold coins), he will return it to you completely." The word يُؤَدِّهِۦٓ emphasizes returning something in full. You could leave massive wealth with this person, and he wouldn't notice if you took a few coins out—but he would still give everything back to you with complete honesty.

"And among them is one who, if you trust him with a dinar (a single coin), he will not return it to you unless you remain standing over him." You'd have to stay on him constantly, filing legal claims, persistently demanding your money back. Even for something as small as a dollar, you'd never see it again unless you pursued it relentlessly.

Why would someone be like this? ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَيْسَ عَلَيْنَا فِى ٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ سَبِيلٌ—"That is because they say: 'There is no case against us concerning the unlettered people.'" Some among them claimed that their Scripture gave them permission to cheat, lie to, or steal from gentiles—the "unlettered" ones, the pagans, those outside their faith community. They justified their unethical behavior by saying these people were going to hell anyway, so it didn't matter how they treated them.

The reality is that neither the Torah nor the Gospel contain such teachings. However, some interpretations and later texts did include troubling statements about how to treat those outside the faith community—that you don't have to pay them their wages, that you can lie to them, and so on. These people used religious justification for fundamentally unethical behavior.

This disease isn't limited to one community. Muslims have fallen into the same trap. I've seen Muslim business owners justify stealing from their non-Muslim business partners by saying, "He's a kafir. He's going to burn in hell anyway—might as well take some money before he goes." Some even convince themselves they'll use it for "good causes," buying halal chicken or supporting Islamic work. It's an insane attitude.

When I was in college, some MSA students would inflate receipts when ordering food from street vendors who didn't give proper receipts.
