Muslim refers to someone who submits through action. In other words, death will come and you'd better be in obedience when it arrives. You'd better be in submission. Don't be caught doing something in disobedience when death comes, because death won't say, "Oh, I'm sorry, you're watching a movie. I'll come back at a more convenient time." It won't do that. It will catch you whether you're obeying or disobeying.

The one who truly wants to protect themselves knows death can come at any moment. You'd better not get caught red-handed in the middle of sin. This is called سوء الخاتمة —the worst kind of death, the evil death, meaning you died in the middle of a sin. The state in which you die is a strong indication of what Allah ﷻ has in store for you. There are people who die making sujud—they simply didn't get up from prostration. There are people praying Fajr at the masjid who made one salam but didn't get to the second before they were gone.

Then there are the tragic stories—young people headed to parties who never arrived because of car accidents. These are terrifying realities. No person should feel safe. This isn't just about some historical figure or some evil person who died—this is about you and me. Every one of us has a public face and a private face: how we are at home, among friends, when nobody else is around. The Angel of Death doesn't announce himself. He doesn't say, "I'm sorry, you're in a meeting. You need some private time. You're on break. I shouldn't come right now." It won't be like that.

وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ—"Don't you dare die except that you are in a state of submission." This ayah calls for personal transformation. One must start living a life of caution with Allah ﷻ, and what keeps them constantly cautious is the reminder of death. That reminder will maintain their vigilance.

The most valuable asset we have is time, and the biggest loss we're all suffering simultaneously is also time. Whether we like it or not, we're losing it at every moment. All your life amounts to is a series of inhales and exhales. Every breath you take, every time you inhale and exhale, a piece of your life in this world has been taken away that can never return. Every single breath is a step closer to meeting your Master.

إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا—"You are marching forward toward your Lord." You've been put on a conveyor belt heading toward your Master whether you like it or not, whether you accept it or not, whether you're oblivious to it or not. None of that matters—you're still heading there. You could stand on a conveyor belt facing backward or forward; you're still heading forward. Whether you close your eyes or open them, you're still heading forward.

The one who refuses to see reality and the one who opens their eyes to it—it doesn't matter in terms of the journey's direction. They're both still heading forward. But the one who opens their eyes and looks at the danger around them can protect themselves on this journey. They'll be ready to be taken in a state where they are Muslim—in submission.

لَا تَمُوتُنَّ uses the forbidding form of the verb. This is said for someone who's about to die, not someone who's already dead. It's the strongest possible warning. Don't you dare die except while you're in Islam, in a state of submission.

May Allah ﷻ grant us that state of Islam throughout our lives and at the moment when our souls are taken from us.

translation: And hold firmly to the rope of Allah all together and do not become divided. And remember the favor of Allah upon you - when you were enemies and He brought your hearts together and you became, by His favor, brothers. And you were on the edge of a pit of the Fire, and He saved you from it. Thus does Allah make clear to you His verses that you may be guided
commentary:
وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِحَبْلِ ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا۟ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنتُمْ أَعْدَآءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا وَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَفَا حُفْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ فَأَنقَذَكُم مِّنْهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

## From Individual Reform to Collective Unity

The previous ayah (102) focused on individual reform—each person making a personal decision to protect themselves spiritually and to ensure that death does not reach them except in a state of Islam. Death, after all, is an intensely individual matter, not a collective one. Each person's journey to Taqwa unfolds differently. Allah ﷻ opens unique pathways for each soul, guiding them according to their circumstances and capabilities.

However, once individuals have achieved this awakening—once they've recognized the urgency of protecting themselves and committing to Islam—a crucial question emerges: How do you create harmony and synchronization among these people of Taqwa? How do you get hearts and minds on the same page? Individual goodness is essential, but for meaningful change to happen at a societal level, there must be collective unity.

Think of every person who has come to Iman as a lamp or a source of light. For that light to increase, the lamps must come together—two, then three, then four, then five. There needs to be some form of unity for this good to carry forward and multiply. The Prophet ﷺ himself highlighted the importance of collective goodness in many ways. The simplest example is the difference between praying individually at home versus praying in congregation. Individual prayer carries reward, but congregational prayer multiplies that reward twenty-seven times—even with just one other person. These are the kinds of incentives that help us understand that while individual goodness is important, goodness in a collective multiplies exponentially.

## Holding Fast to the Rope of Allah

The previous ayah taught us the concept of i'tisam—holding on for dear life. It spoke about those who held onto Allah ﷻ for protection and guidance. Now, this ayah provides the practical application: How exactly do you hold onto Allah ﷻ? The answer: وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِحَبْلِ ٱللَّهِ (hold fast to the rope of Allah).

There are beautiful narrations about what this "rope" represents. Ali ibn Abi Talib (RA) and Abdullah ibn Mas'ud (RA) both described the Quran as the extended rope of Allah—one end reaching from the heavens to the earth. In other words, the Quran connects you directly to Allah ﷻ. Another narration beautifully captures this: one end of the rope is literally in your hand, while the other end is in the hands of Allah ﷻ.

Previously, the instruction was abstract—hold onto Allah ﷻ. But how do you hold onto God in a tangible way? This ayah makes it concrete: hold onto the Quran. The Quran becomes the means, the rope that keeps you connected to your Creator.

## The Meaning of "Altogether"

The command continues with جَمِيعًا (altogether, collectively). This word operates on two levels. First, it means hold onto the entire Book—all of it, not selectively. Don't pick and choose which parts appeal to you. Second, it means all of you together—as a unified community.

Now picture this image: a group of people all holding onto the same rope. What does that necessarily create? It brings them closer together. Their physical proximity increases. But more than that, their direction becomes unified. Whether they're pulling the rope or climbing it, they're all moving in the same direction. They've been synchronized in purpose.

This is what the Quran does when people unify around it. Their thinking begins to align. Their attitudes shift toward a common direction. It creates one synchronized trajectory. Yes, there are thousands of Tafsir works, hundreds of thousands of scholars and thinkers who have reflected on the Quran, and countless variations in understanding and interpretation. But overall, the central message of the Quran creates synchronization. It doesn't matter whether you're reading Ibn Kathir or Qurtubi, whether you're reading a translation or listening to a Tafsir lecture—in the end, the Quran becomes a source of unifying people. And this remains true to this day.

## The Quran as the Great Unifier

Consider the various fields of Islamic studies: Islamic history, Fiqh, Hadith scholarship, Aqeedah, Kalam (theology). Each of these fields carries tremendous blessings and importance. However, none of them serves as a unifier quite like the Quran. When you go deeper into these specialized fields, you often encounter more and more differences of opinion, more divisions, sometimes even staunch disagreements that cause entire groups to break away over this issue versus that issue.

The Quran, by contrast, is different. When the study and spreading of the Quran takes priority—along with the Seerah of the Prophet ﷺ, which also serves as a unifier—something remarkable happens. It doesn't matter what background you come from or what school of thought you were raised in. When the Quran speaks about the importance of Taqwa, it doesn't matter which madhab you follow. When it warns about dying in any state other than Islam, those distinctions become irrelevant. You begin to feel a sense of cohesion with others, unified through the very word of Allah ﷻ.

Holding onto this rope for dear life creates a likeness of thought and a likeness of minds across the Ummah.

## The Foundation of Islamic Education

This brings us to consider what constitutes foundational Islamic education. We will always need scholars deeply trained in traditional Islamic sciences—that need is permanent. But as a major component of reform in Islamic society, we need something additional, not instead of, but in addition to specialized scholarship.

For the average educated Muslim, I would propose this framework: The first area of focus should be personal Adab—mannerisms and dealings with people. How do you interact with teachers? How do you treat your parents? How do you handle family relationships? How do you conduct yourself with coworkers? How do you approach Allah ﷻ in prayer? Your character, your akhlaq—this should be the first area of concern and study.

The second area is personal Fiqh—acts of worship as they should be performed correctly. What are the rulings on the clothes you wear? How should you conduct business? How do you perform Hajj or Umrah? What should you do if you miss a fast? These are not things you study to teach others or to issue fatwas, but to become a better Muslim yourself in your personal life. For this, rely on your local resources—your imams and scholars—and please, avoid simply Googling Islamic rulings.

Beyond these two foundations, every educated Muslim should pursue serious, lifelong education in Quran and Seerah. Read one Seerah of the Prophet ﷺ every year—don't ask which one, read all of them eventually. Pick one each year and go through it completely. When you're done, start over with another. This practice creates a profound sense of affiliation, love and appreciation for Allah's Messenger ﷺ. Constantly reading about his struggles helps you identify them with your own. You begin to see a closeness to him that you cannot experience unless you study his life.

As for the Quran, make it a lifelong study. Whether that means attending lectures, doing personal research, grammatical breakdown, or deeper reflection—maintain at least some familiarity with the entire Quran through an ongoing process of study and contemplation.

## Understanding Our Contemporary World

But Islamic education alone is only half the picture. The other half involves understanding contemporary thought: sociology, anthropology, psychology, political science. We should be aware of the major ideas prevalent in our society and understand how our religion responds to those ideas. We need to know the age in which we live. That's part of being truly educated.

Only when we understand both our sacred texts and these contemporary fields of knowledge does our religion become alive and relevant—not just to us personally, but to all of society. This understanding will guide whatever career path you pursue, giving you a clear vision of how your Deen can bring genuine goodness to the world around you.

You cannot isolate these two areas—human thought and Islamic knowledge. You really do need them together. When students ask me personally what they should study, I often say: Go to your local college and get a degree in sociology, psychology, anthropology, political science, or history. Study these subjects—they're important. Build a solid foundational education, and then study Islam alongside it. Study the Quran and Seerah deeply. This combination creates a wholesome, well-rounded human being.

Now, I recognize that going too deep into academic studies can sometimes take people off track, so it's valuable to have mentors who have already navigated these fields. They can guide you: "Here are the articles you should read. Here's what you should be aware of regarding contemporary issues and how Muslims have responded to them."

This kind of well-rounded education would create a natural agreement among educated Muslims that wouldn't need to be debated. We would simply be thinking along similar lines, operating on the same wavelength. This would manifest itself in creative projects and wonderful initiatives—not all the same, but all inspired by the same source: the same revelation, the same example of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

## The Command Against Division

The ayah continues: وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا۟ (and do not become divided). Notice the precision in Allah's choice of words. He doesn't say *la tafarraku* (from *firaq*, simple separation), but rather *la tafarraqu* (from *tafarruq*, breaking into factions). There's a crucial distinction here.

People will naturally disagree—that's part of human nature and how learning happens. Among the Sahabah (RA), disagreement existed, but *tafarruq* did not. Some of our greatest schools of Islamic thought were developed by scholars who respectfully disagreed with their teachers and went on to establish their own methodologies. These schools were born out of disagreement, yes, but not out of *tafarruq*—not from breaking into hostile factions.

The command *la tafarraqu* means don't divide yourselves even a little bit. Don't become factions that cut themselves off from the rest of the community.

## Practical Applications of Unity

How does this manifest in our immediate reality? Don't make your primary allegiance to an organization, a label, a logo, or a particular group. Yes, these things can help you serve Islam. There are charity organizations doing tremendous work, youth groups creating positive change, masajid serving their communities beautifully, dawah organizations spreading beneficial knowledge, educational institutions training the next generation. They all do important work.

But our allegiance should not be to these labels. Our allegiance is to a greater cause—to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ, to the Deen itself.

You can check where your true allegiance lies when you start viewing the progress of another Muslim organization as competition. If you think, "Those guys are expanding—that's not good. They're coming to our area. They had a bigger program than us. Hmm, that can't be good"—then you know your allegiance has shifted away from the Deen itself and you're viewing other groups as completely separate from your own. This is *tafarruq*. This is the very division Allah ﷻ forbids.

We're supposed to welcome other efforts, even if they're happening in the same field. We should say, Alhamdulillah, and see it as Allah's means of creating more unity among us. The more the merrier. We shouldn't look at the landscape of Muslim organizations and ask, "Why do we have so many different organizations?" Actually, organizations are not the problem—they're a stepping stone to eventual unity.

Think of it this way: if all Muslims on a map were represented by green dots, and then some Muslims came together to form an association—whether it's a masjid, a Sunday school, an Islamic school, or a charity—those many individual dots become one larger dot. If all Muslims were somehow affiliated with some kind of organization, you would see fewer dots overall, not more. Organizations and communities are not creating more disunity; they're actually decreasing the fragmentation. They're a stepping stone toward unity, provided those organizations have the right intention.

These institutions need to understand the spirit of mutual cooperation. They must recognize that they're part of a very large picture and that they don't hold all the solutions in their hands. There are other institutions, other individuals, other organizations doing equally important work in different areas, and they need to work together. That understanding will be there naturally if you hold onto the rope of Allah ﷻ. When you hold onto the Quran, you'll recognize that the mission is grand, and you're just trying to accomplish one small piece within that larger mission.

Without that connection to the rope of Allah ﷻ, everyone becomes territorial and competitive. They advertise against one another, speak badly about each other's efforts, and engage in destructive criticism. This is unfortunate because it goes against the very spirit of i'tisam—holding fast together.

## The Ethics of Criticism

Even if you wholeheartedly disagree with some effort, ask yourself: Are they doing anything good? You might think, "Yeah, but it could be better." Fine—but leave them alone, because you could be better too. Learn to acknowledge the good and remain silent about what you perceive as shortcomings. Make dua for other efforts instead of tearing them down.

We've become exceptionally skilled at pointing out flaws in other people's work. We're experts at criticism. But we're often the last to recognize and celebrate the goodness in those same efforts. If there's even an ounce of good coming from an initiative, you should appreciate it. You don't know what that good is worth to Allah ﷻ. You simply don't know. So appreciate the good and leave the rest. You don't even have to mention the parts you disagree with.

My favorite is when people say, "I'm not saying anything, but..." and then proceed to say something. If you're not saying anything, then actually don't say anything. Don't even say "I'm not saying anything" because that's also saying something. If you don't have anything good to say, just remain silent. Just leave it. Allah ﷻ will create good from it.

This is the meaning of وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا۟—don't break into factions. Don't let division take root in your heart or in your actions. Hold fast to the rope of Allah ﷻ together, and let that unity be your strength.

translation: And let there be [arising] from you a nation inviting to [all that is] good, enjoining what is right and forbidding what is wrong, and those will be the successful
commentary:
وَلْتَكُن مِّنكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

### The Rise of a Nation Within the Nation

وَلْتَكُن مِّنكُمْ أُمَّةٌ - a nation should rise from among you. Ummah means a nation unified by cause. The passage already began with يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا (O you who believe), so the address is already to the Muslims. Yet Allah ﷻ says a group should rise from among you.

What does this mean? People are holding onto the Quran as described in the previous ayah, some more strongly than others. Those who hold more strongly will naturally gravitate toward each other even more, and they will naturally form an alliance. That alliance will lead them to do great things. This is the natural progression: from being brothers (ikhwan) to being unified through the Quran to now becoming an ummah within the ummah - not an ummah separate from the ummah, but an ummah within it.

Some scholars understand مِّنكُمْ (from among you) to mean a proportion, like a subgroup should rise from within you. Others say this من is for transformation - that you all should be transformed into this ummah, like a flower comes from a stem. The flower is not a fraction of the stem; it is a transformation of the stem. Similarly, all of you should become this ummah.

### Inviting to Goodness (Ad-Da'wah ilal-Khayr)

يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ - they invite to the good. The word دَعْوَة itself is an act of invitation, and inviting is inherently an act of goodness. We often think of da'wah as a transaction, like a cashier handling business. That's not what da'wah is. Da'wah is: "Come over to my house, let's have dinner together." It's the beautiful thing, the good thing.

Consider the example of the Messenger ﷺ returning from Taif, bloodied and hurt. A Christian boy with a crucifix hanging from his neck helps the Messenger ﷺ, and he starts making small talk with him. "Where are you from? Oh, you're from there? My brother Yunus (AS) is from there." This is all khayr - finding something good to say, inviting people to goodness itself. When people come close to Muslims and all they notice is goodness, that's inviting to khayr.

### Commanding What is Recognized as Good (Al-Ma'roof)

وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ - and they command what is known to be decent. المَعْرُوف comes from the root ع-ر-ف, which relates to knowing and recognition. Ma'roof is goodness that is known by everybody. You don't have to teach it because it's already recognized.

There are aspects of our religion you have to teach - the procedure for wudu, the specifics of prayer - but you don't have to teach goodness to parents. You don't have to teach humility, honesty, truthfulness, or kindness. These things are ma'roof, recognized by all people. Do you need to teach that there's one God, that we were created for a purpose? These are recognizable truths. Even practical matters: you should be kind to your neighbor, you should be honest with your boss, you shouldn't speak harshly to your wife. You can share this advice even with non-Muslim friends because it's something they can benefit from. This is ma'roof.

### The Degrees of Commanding Good (Amr)

The word أَمْر has degrees. Classical Arabic poetry helps us understand this. A poet once wrote about his girlfriend who left him because she listened to people telling her he was worthless: أَطَعْتِ آمِرًا (you obeyed those who were continuously suggesting to you that I'm a loser). Notice: people weren't commanding her to leave; they were suggesting. So amr has degrees.

Amr can be suggesting to someone, advising someone, encouraging someone. A step above that is scolding somebody a little, pushing them harder. An even higher degree is telling someone firmly, "Wake up now!" All of these fall under amr, but the intensity depends on the context.

Consider Prophet Yaqub (AS). When his sons brought him the bloodied shirt of Yusuf (AS), he said nothing harsh. By definition, as a father, he had authority over his sons and could have commanded them strongly. But he doesn't. His silence was itself a form of amr - suggestion and advice. He simply says, بَلْ سَوَّلَتْ لَكُمْ أَنفُسُكُمْ أَمْرًا (rather, your souls have enticed you to something). He suggests, he advises them gently, and that's the end of his response. Anything beyond that would have backfired. This proves that amr has degrees and is contextual.

How you do amr bil-ma'roof with your friends will be different from how you do it with your parents. Sometimes you're in no position to command your parents, but you still have to do amr bil-ma'roof. So what do you do? You advise, you suggest, in a very humble fashion. You find creative ways.

Imagine your father is getting into a clearly haram business - opening a liquor store, for example - and you're very uncomfortable with it. You can't just go in and quote: إِنَّمَا ٱلْخَمْرُ وَٱلْمَيْسِرُ (intoxicants and gambling are an abomination from the work of the devil). That approach won't work. Instead, you might say, "I was wondering, should we really do this? Are you personally really comfortable with that?" He might respond, "Shut up, go to your room." Okay, leave. Find another time. You have to find ways of doing amr bil-ma'roof depending on who you're dealing with.

### Forbidding What is Unrecognized (Al-Munkar)

وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ - and they forbid that which is alien, unrecognized. المُنكَر comes from the root ن-ك-ر, the opposite of ma'roof. It means to deny something, something unknown, something completely alien that you don't recognize at all. When people do things that nobody knows to be good, this is munkar.

Beautiful modern manifestations of munkar are sociologists and philosophers who come up with new theories of ethics that nobody's ever heard of, completely out of balance with human nature. The first thing you say is, "This is unheard of," and the Quran's term for that is munkar. Often, these unheard-of things are just new manifestations of evil.

I once took a sociology class where the professor said, "Marriage is just an institution put together by society. Naturally, we're not supposed to have any restrictions. Marriage is just a man-made construct. In the natural world, we should be able to interact freely." What he meant was: like animals in the natural world, we should behave without rules. But Allah ﷻ created us above that. The assumption underlying such thinking is that we're not above animals - we're just animals too, so there's no need for rules.

When you deny that there is something special inside the human being that gives us supremacy, honor, and nobility, then why act any different from animals? This is munkar - unknown, alien, unidentified as good. Nobody recognizes it as good.

### The Degrees of Forbidding Evil (Nahy 'anil-Munkar)

Like amr bil-ma'roof, forbidding evil also has degrees. The Prophet ﷺ taught us: مَنْ رَأَىٰ مِنْكُمْ مُنْكَرًا فَلْيُغَيِّرْهُ بِيَدِهِ (whoever among you sees an evil, let him change it with his hand). فَإِنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَبِلِسَانِهِ (and if he is not able, then with his tongue). فَإِنْ لَمْ يَسْتَطِعْ فَبِقَلْبِهِ (and if he cannot do so, then with his heart). وَذَٰلِكَ أَضْعَفُ ٱلْإِيمَانِ (and that is the weakest state of Iman).

By the way, this is not just the weakest state of Iman for an individual - it is also the weakest state of Iman for a society. This passage began with people of taqwa trying to protect their Iman. Then they fortify their Iman by coming closer to the Quran. Then they become able to command good and forbid evil. If they're not able to do so, that means something is missing in this process.

If this progression is in place, we'll start seeing manifestations of amr bil-ma'roof and nahy 'anil-munkar in many walks of life. We'll start seeing Muslims address matters of injustice that everybody knows to be unjust, plaguing societies - both in Muslim societies and here in the United States - all in the spirit of bringing ma'roof back and calling out munkar for what it is.

### The Wisdom of Calling to Al-Khayr

Notice that Allah ﷻ didn't say يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْإِسْلَامِ (they call to Islam). He could have said that - Islam is the perfect word of Allah ﷻ. But Allah ﷻ said يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ (they call to goodness). Why? Because Islam may not be appreciated by everybody initially, but khayr is appreciated by Muslim and non-Muslim alike. They're going to call to something that everybody will see is good, commanding what everybody will recognize as decent, and forbidding what everybody will recognize as wrong.

### The Ultimate Success

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ - and those are, in fact, the ones who are truly successful, the ones who attain success after long, sincere effort.

This is the completion of the progression: from protecting your own faith, to unifying through the Quran, to becoming a force for good in the world that invites to goodness, commands what is recognized as decent, and forbids what is alien to human decency. This is the path to true success, to falah.

translation: And do not be like the ones who became divided and differed after the clear proofs had come to them. And those will have a great punishment
commentary:
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ تَفَرَّقُوا۟ وَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

This ayah warns against a specific sequence of division. The verse uses تَفَرَّقُوا۟ first, then وَٱخْتَلَفُوا۟. They fell apart into groups first, and then disagreed. This order is significant. When people separate into factions, their disagreements that follow serve only to justify their division. They're not seeking truth anymore - they're protecting their group, their school of thought, their particular view.

This is why the ayah says "don't be like those" who did this after clear proofs came to them. These proofs - the ayat, the stories from the lives of the Prophets - were meant to unify and guide. Instead, they became tools to justify sectarianism. The knowledge of revelation was twisted to progress a particular faction's agenda rather than to protect the religion itself.

This is the tragedy: they had البينات - the clearest proofs - but used them for the wrong purpose. For such people, Allah ﷻ promises عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ (a great punishment).

translation: On the Day [some] faces will turn white and [some] faces will turn black. As for those whose faces turn black, [to them it will be said], "Did you disbelieve after your belief? Then taste the punishment for what you used to reject
commentary:
يَوْمَ تَبْيَضُّ وُجُوهٌ وَتَسْوَدُّ وُجُوهٌ ۚ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْوَدَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ أَكَفَرْتُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ فَذُوقُوا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْفُرُونَ

On the Day of Judgment, the consequences of these actions manifest visibly. Some faces تَبْيَضُّ - become completely white, and some وُجُوهٌ تَسْوَدُّ - become completely black. These colors represent light and darkness respectively. Some faces will have so much light on them that they turn white from that illumination, while others will be engulfed in such darkness that their faces turn black.

The ayah then focuses on those whose faces have been darkened. The angels address them with a devastating question: "You disbelieved after you had faith?" How could this happen? They were given that which could unify humanity - the message of Islam - and instead used it to create disunity within the Ummah itself. What a crime to take such a gift and use it for destruction rather than unity.

The command follows: "Taste the punishment because of the disbelief you had always been engaged in." The first act of sectarianism itself was an act of disbelief. When someone breaks away, creating their own faction and justifying it - this is where it begins. It's reminiscent of the first rebel, Iblis, who separated himself and justified his disobedience.

translation: But as for those whose faces will turn white, [they will be] within the mercy of Allah. They will abide therein eternally
commentary:
وَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ٱبْيَضَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فَفِى رَحْمَةِ ٱللَّهِ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

As for those whose faces have been illuminated - their description is remarkably brief. Allah ﷻ doesn't even complete a full sentence about them. He simply says: they are in the mercy of Allah, هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ - in which they will remain permanently.

Fill in the blanks yourself. How many blessings are confirmed in these few words? They enjoy His closeness in His mercy. They're in Paradise, living forever in the mercy of Allah ﷻ. The brevity itself speaks to the overwhelming nature of their reward - it cannot be fully captured in words.

translation: These are the verses of Allah. We recite them to you, [O Muhammad], in truth; and Allah wants no injustice to the worlds
commentary:
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ نَتْلُوهَا عَلَيْكَ بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

These are the miraculous signs of Allah ﷻ, revealed with truth and purpose. The word بِٱلْحَقِّ doesn't just mean truth - it also means with purpose, with good reason, with justification. Allah ﷻ is telling us these things because division will be a living problem, and these ayahs provide living solutions. There's a reason we need to hear this.

Then comes a powerful negation: وَمَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ ظُلْمًا لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - "Allah does not intend wrongdoing for any of the nations." Notice the structure: مَا ٱللَّهُ يُرِيدُ - it's not Allah at all who intends this. The emphasis makes it timeless and absolute.

People argue to this day: why does God create Hell? Why would He want to put people there? What does He get out of it? This ayah answers definitively: He doesn't want oppression for anyone. Allah ﷻ certainly doesn't want it. But someone does want to lead people to Hell - Shaytan does. Yes, there is a plan to put people in Hell, but it's not Allah's plan. It's your enemy's plan. You want to follow it? Go ahead. But don't blame Allah ﷻ.

translation: To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. And to Allah will [all] matters be returned
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ

There's a pattern in the Quran you need to recognize. Sometimes in the middle of a conversation, the subject changes and suddenly we're talking about how great Allah ﷻ is - how much He owns, how much He knows. Here's the general rule: the more Allah ﷻ describes His greatness, the more the servant is supposed to feel humbled. The more He describes His grandeur, the more it should drive us to feel our own insignificance.

If you read ayahs about Allah's greatness and they don't make you feel humbled, you've missed their point entirely. These ayahs are meant to put us in our place.

The previous ayah talked about people having knowledge of revelation yet still falling into disagreement. They had knowledge, but that knowledge didn't lead to unity as it should have. If they had held onto the rope of Allah ﷻ, they would not have been divided. They violated what they were given, which means they weren't able to humble themselves.

So we need to be reminded. We need to be put in our place. Who are you dealing with? Whose word is it that you're manipulating for your own purposes?

وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - To Allah ﷻ alone belongs whatever is in the skies and whatever is in the earth. وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ - And to Allah ﷻ alone, all affairs will be returned. The word تُرْجَعُ (turja'u) indicates they will be returned - not that they are returning themselves. Whatever you have done, all of it will come back to Allah ﷻ for processing, for judgment.

translation: You are the best nation produced [as an example] for mankind. You enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and believe in Allah. If only the People of the Scripture had believed, it would have been better for them. Among them are believers, but most of them are defiantly disobedient
commentary:
كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ أُخْرِجَتْ لِلنَّاسِ تَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَتَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ ۗ وَلَوْ ءَامَنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم ۚ مِّنْهُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

You are the best of nations - that part sounds wonderful until you examine what Allah ﷻ actually says. He doesn't say كُنتُمْ خَيْرَ أُمَّةٍ خَرَجَتْ (you are the best nation that came out), which would give credit to the nation itself. He says أُخْرِجَتْ- you have been brought out, extracted. The passive voice is critical. You get no credit for being formed as a nation. Zero credit. The doer is implied but unstated - obviously Allah ﷻ. You were brought out by Him.

For what purpose? Notice: لِلنَّاسِ - for the people. Not مِنَ النَّاسِ which would mean you're the best nation as opposed to other people, better than everybody else. No, لِلنَّاسِ - for the people. To serve people, to help people, to bring goodness to people.

Imagine a tree planted only to give shade. It exists for that purpose. Allah ﷻ is saying you were brought out for the people - to give to them, not to take from them. There was a nation before you given preference over all others for the same reason. They didn't fulfill their task. Now it's your turn.

Don't think of yourself as superior to people. You're here for them.

Then comes the description: تَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَتَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ - you command the recognized good and forbid the recognized evil. Notice these verbs are in the present tense. In the previous passage, Allah ﷻ said there should be a nation that does this - that was conditional, something that might happen. Here He says it as fact: you do command the good, and you do forbid the evil.

This is powerful. He's not describing something that might happen - He's stating it as though it's already occurring. Put this in perspective: imagine I know my daughter didn't do her homework, but I say to her, "You work on your homework every day, and the way you did it today was very good." If she knows she didn't do it, she'll be embarrassed. "Actually Dad, I didn't do it." "What do you mean you didn't do it? You're a student, right? You're supposed to do it. I assume you did it. Of course you would. Why wouldn't you?"

That's what's happening here. Allah ﷻ mentions it as a matter of fact: you're the best nation brought out for people, and of course you command good and forbid evil, and of course you believe in Allah ﷻ. We sit there listening and think, "Yeah... I guess... not really." It's humiliating. If we're doing it, it should be an honor. If we're not, it should make us feel deeply uncomfortable. 

Allah ﷻ didn't frame this as a command because even commands imply we're not already doing something. Instead, He states it as though the previous ayahs created an expectation, and He expects it should already be done. So He says: of course you're doing it, aren't you? Well... you believe in Allah ﷻ, don't you? Why wouldn't you do these things?

The belief in Allah ﷻ is mentioned at the end to suggest that faith is the reason you do all of this. That's why commanding good and forbidding evil flows naturally - because of your Iman.

وَلَوْ ءَامَنَ أَهْلُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم - Had the People of the Book only believed, it would have been so much better for them. If they came to Iman, how good it would be for them.

مِّنْهُمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ - Among them are believers. There are still people from the People of the Book who have real potential for faith, or who have already embraced Islam. Allah ﷻ knows this, so He calls to them.

وَأَكْثَرُهُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ - While most of them are corrupt. What is their corruption? They're commanded to do something and they don't do it. 

This becomes a mirror for us. Are we like the People of the Book who were given a mission and abandoned it? Or are we fulfilling what we've been brought out to do?

translation: They will not harm you except for [some] annoyance. And if they fight you, they will show you their backs; then they will not be aided
commentary:
لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّآ أَذًى ۖ وَإِن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ

Before exploring this ayah, we must understand a foundational concept that precedes it: commanding good and forbidding evil (الأمر بالمعروف والنهي عن المنكر). This is a profound and multifaceted principle in Islamic practice that carries implications at every level—individual, family, community and even geopolitical. A shallow understanding of this concept can lead to drastic and misguided conclusions about how to apply it in society.

This sacred duty begins with what is known as المعروف and المنكر —concepts that require deep study in themselves. The first and most essential application starts with the individual and family. Each of us lives in our own worldly realm, our دنيا. Your worldly life differs from mine. My dunya includes my children, my wife, my family, my friends and their unique challenges. Your dunya contains your own set of blessings and trials. Yet we all share the same آخرة, the same Hereafter.

The primary arena for commanding good and forbidding evil is within yourself—a struggle with your own nafs—and within your immediate family and close friends. When we learn to implement this principle properly at this intimate level, the broader applications will naturally follow. However, there's a delicate balance required. When you offer someone advice, it can almost always be perceived as self-righteousness. The person may think, "Who are you to tell me?" This is why one must develop tremendous wisdom in engaging in this blessed act without appearing arrogant, and more importantly, without actually becoming arrogant.

When you grow accustomed to guiding people or offering religious and ethical counsel, it becomes dangerously easy to slip into self-righteousness without even realizing it. This reminds us of the profound advice Luqman (AS) gave to his son. The sequence itself reveals deep wisdom. He told his son, "Establish salah, command good and forbid evil." Notice what comes first—establishing the prayer, the direct spiritual connection to Allah ﷻ. This was advice given even before the Prophet ﷺ, in a time when we don't know the exact form of salah, but its essence remains: establish worship that connects you directly to your Creator.

What does establishing salah accomplish? It helps a person genuinely taste and appreciate divine guidance. When you've truly experienced something wonderful for yourself, you naturally want to share it with those you love. It's like discovering an amazing piece of chocolate—you immediately want to give a piece to your children. When you appreciate something deeply beneficial, sharing it becomes instinctive. Similarly, when salah genuinely connects you to Allah ﷻ, the natural consequence is wanting others to experience that same light and guidance.

Luqman's (AS) next piece of advice is equally wise: "Command good and forbid evil, and be patient over what befalls you" (واصبر على ما أصابك). Then he warns, "Do not turn your cheek away from people in contempt, and do not walk upon the earth exultantly." What does commanding good and forbidding evil have to do with arrogance? When you consistently advise people and don't see results, you might begin to think, "These people are useless." That very thought is a manifestation of arrogance and pride.

When you give someone sincere advice and they don't listen, what happens? You become angry. Extremely angry people sometimes storm off mid-conversation. This is why Luqman (AS) follows with "be patient." When you engage in commanding good and forbidding evil, people will respond in ways that feel disrespectful. They may ridicule what you're saying or dismiss it entirely. No human being—Muslim or otherwise—enjoys having their words belittled. It's natural to feel angry in such situations. Your pride is wounded.

But it's precisely at that moment when Allah ﷻ reminds you not to swell your cheeks in pride and not to walk on the earth with arrogance. When people truly lose their temper, they pace aggressively, they storm out. This is why the next instruction comes: "Be moderate in your pace, and lower your voice." What do people do when they lose control? They raise their voices. Allah ﷻ then compares the person who raises their voice in anger to a donkey: "Indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of donkeys" (إِنَّ أَنكَرَ ٱلْأَصْوَٰتِ لَصَوْتُ ٱلْحَمِيرِ).

Why specifically a donkey? In ancient times, a donkey was a beast of burden. You would load it with supplies for travel—piles upon piles until it could carry no more. The Quran even describes such a donkey: "like a donkey carrying volumes of books" (كَمَثَلِ ٱلْحِمَارِ يَحْمِلُ أَسْفَارًۢا). When you've loaded so much onto the animal that it simply cannot bear anymore, it makes that harsh, unpleasant sound. Allah ﷻ compares this to a human being who says, "You've said this to me, you've tested my patience, I've taken it and taken it and taken it, and now I can't take anymore!"

Allah's response? This is exactly when sabr truly begins. Everything before this moment, you could handle—your patience wasn't genuinely tested yet. The real test arrives when things spiral out of control. It's an incredible parable. The first manifestation of anger is a kind of temporary insanity, and at its end, you feel ashamed of your behavior.

All of this should serve as a reminder for myself and for you, especially when we engage in commanding good and forbidding evil within our families, among friends and even on public platforms. We must never become self-righteous. We cannot forget that these principles apply to us just as much as they apply to others. Not every minor issue requires intervention. You don't need to lean over to the person praying next to you and say, "By the way, the way your finger was moving in tashahhud was totally wrong and you're distracting me." Leave people alone. That's not truly commanding good and forbidding evil—you're not doing anyone a favor with that approach.

There's a proper process, a wise methodology for dealing with human beings. We cannot forget that each person carries their own background, their own baggage, their own unique circumstances and struggles.

Now we come to ayah 111. After discussing the People of the Book and their reluctance to embrace Islam—noting that it would have been better for them if they had believed, but that most of them are corrupt—Allah ﷻ describes their opposition to the Messenger ﷺ and to the Muslims. They engaged in numerous conspiracies, much like the Quraysh of Makkah, providing various arguments to use against the Prophet ﷺ. Significant behind-the-scenes maneuvering was taking place, which naturally worried the Muslims.

Allah ﷻ provides relief to the believers: "They will not harm you except for some annoyance" (لَن يَضُرُّوكُمْ إِلَّآ أَذًى). The word أذى means minor pain or irritation. In other words, they won't be able to inflict real damage upon you—only cause some pain here and there. They might say hurtful things. They might implement policies that wound your dignity or cause temporary difficulty. But beyond that, they cannot truly harm you.

"And if they fight you, they will turn their backs to you, then they will not be aided" (وَإِن يُقَـٰتِلُوكُمْ يُوَلُّوكُمُ ٱلْأَدْبَارَ ثُمَّ لَا يُنصَرُونَ). If actual conflict breaks out, they will flee in fear, turning their backs, and no help will come to them. Allah ﷻ was preparing the Muslims, because some of these ayahs were revealed before the Battle of Uhud and some after. The believers needed to understand that eventually, they would face conflict not only with the polytheists of Makkah but also with some from among the People of the Book. When that time came, they would be assured of victory—their enemies would retreat and receive no divine aid.

translation: They have been put under humiliation [by Allah] wherever they are overtaken, except for a covenant from Allah and a rope from the Muslims. And they have drawn upon themselves anger from Allah and have been put under destitution. That is because they disbelieved in the verses of Allah and killed the prophets without right. That is because they disobeyed and [habitually] transgressed
commentary:
ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ أَيْنَ مَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟ إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ

"Humiliation has been struck upon them" (ضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلذِّلَّةُ)—the word ضُرِبَتْ means struck or slapped onto them. It wasn't something they chose; it was imposed upon them forcefully. "Wherever they are found" (أَيْنَ مَا ثُقِفُوٓا۟)—meaning throughout the diaspora, as they wandered from nation to nation. Wherever they went, humiliation followed them like a shadow.

"Except when protected by a rope from Allah and a rope from the people" (إِلَّا بِحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَحَبْلٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ). Even in the midst of their humiliated state, Allah ﷻ would sometimes extend them relief, or another powerful nation would take them in and grant them protection or limited autonomy. During the time of Isa (AS), for example, the Jewish community had two kingdoms, but both existed under Roman authority. They had states, but not true sovereignty. This is why the religious leaders tried to get Isa (AS) in trouble not with their own governance but with the actual Roman rulers, accusing him of being a traitor to Rome.

"And they drew upon themselves the wrath of Allah" (وَبَآءُو بِغَضَبٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ). They returned laden with divine anger. "And poverty was struck upon them" (وَضُرِبَتْ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْمَسْكَنَةُ). The word مَسْكَنَة is deeply meaningful—it comes from the same root as سكون, meaning stillness or being stationary. It conveys being stuck, unable to move forward, trapped in a position you cannot escape. When someone is in a financial situation they cannot improve no matter what they try, they're called مسكين. This poverty and powerlessness were slapped upon them as well.

"That is because they used to disbelieve in the signs of Allah and kill the prophets without right" (ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ). Here Allah ﷻ uses the word أَنۢبِيَآءَ rather than رسل (messengers). In Surah Al-Baqarah, يَقْتُلُونَ (they kill) was used, while here يَقْتُلُونَ appears with the broken plural أَنۢبِيَآءَ. The broken plural in Arabic indicates a greater number than the sound plural. The reason for this difference is significant: Surah Al-Baqarah's discussion focused primarily on the Jewish community up to a certain historical point. Here in Surah Aal-E-Imran, the conversation includes both Jews and Christians—a more comprehensive discussion of the People of the Book. Therefore, reference is made to a greater number of prophets.

The phrase بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ (without right, without justification) emphasizes the complete absence of any legitimate reason. They had no revealed justification, nor could they even concoct one themselves. This appears for the second time in this surah, reinforcing the absolute injustice of their actions.

"That is because they disobeyed and used to transgress" (ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ). The word يَعْتَدُونَ comes from the root meaning to cross boundaries, to transgress limits. The one who crosses (يَعْتَدِي) does so often out of animosity and hostility. They continuously crossed every restriction placed before them. All the humiliation and powerlessness mentioned were direct consequences of their persistent disobedience and transgression.

translation: They are not [all] the same; among the People of the Scripture is a community standing [in obedience], reciting the verses of Allah during periods of the night and prostrating [in prayer]
commentary:
۞ لَيْسُوا۟ سَوَآءً ۗ مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أُمَّةٌ قَآئِمَةٌ يَتْلُونَ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ

After describing the humiliation struck upon them and their wandering from place to place, Allah ﷻ immediately clarifies: "They are not all the same" (لَيْسُوا۟ سَوَآءً). Don't paint all of them with the same brush. This is a crucial divine instruction in fairness and justice.

"Among the People of the Book is an upright community" (مِّنْ أَهْلِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ أُمَّةٌ قَآئِمَةٌ). The word قَآئِمَةٌ means standing upright, established. We've encountered this concept before in أُمَّةٌ قَآئِمَةٌ بِٱلْقِسْطِ—a nation that stands by justice. Here it describes an upright nation, one that stands firm in truth. What makes them stand upright?

"They recite the verses of Allah during the hours of the night while they prostrate" (يَتْلُونَ ءَايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ ءَانَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ). Can there be People of the Book who have not yet encountered the message of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, who haven't committed shirk regarding Adam (AS) or any other prophet, who are still sincerely waiting for a prophet to come, and who remain true to the original scripture? Absolutely. Such people exist or existed.

One example is Salman Al-Farsi (RA), who confirmed the Prophet's ﷺ messengership based on the Torah and previous revelations he had already studied. He recognized the truth because he knew the authentic descriptions preserved in earlier scriptures. There may be others like him among the People of the Book—those who genuinely recite the ayat of Allah ﷻ.

The phrase آنَآءَ ٱلَّيْلِ means "in the hours of the night"—the moments of the night when most people sleep, when darkness envelops the world and distractions fade away. These sincere believers dedicate those precious hours to reciting Allah's revelations.

"While they prostrate" (وَهُمْ يَسْجُدُونَ)—here's another crucial qualifier. Allah ﷻ specifically describes People of the Book who engage in the act of سجدة (prostration). This practice has virtually disappeared from most Christian and Jewish denominations, but a very small number still engage in prostration as an act of worship. According to some scholarly understanding derived from the Quran, in the prayer revealed to Musa (AS), sajdah came first and ruku' (bowing) came second—the opposite sequence from our salah. This is why when Maryam (AS) received divine instruction, she was told, "Prostrate and bow" (ٱسْجُدِى وَٱرْكَعِى)—the sequencing was inverted from what we practice.

The mention of their prostration is significant because it shows their sincere submission to Allah ﷻ in a form of worship that has been largely abandoned by others among the People of the Book. These are the ones who truly stand upright—not through arrogance or claims of exclusive salvation, but through humble worship in the depths of the night, reciting the words of their Lord while their foreheads touch the ground in complete submission.

translation: They believe in Allah and the Last Day, and they enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong and hasten to good deeds. And those are among the righteous
commentary:
**يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ وَيُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ**

*They believe in Allah and the Last Day, and they enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong, and they hasten to good deeds. And those are among the righteous.*

This ayah continues describing the exceptional group among the People of the Book mentioned in the previous passage. Allah ﷻ says **يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ** - they believe in Allah. This statement carries profound weight when we consider what the Quran has already established elsewhere about proper belief in Allah ﷻ. In other passages, Allah ﷻ has made it clear that whoever says something inappropriate about Him has committed disbelief. Those who claim that Allah ﷻ is a third of three have disbelieved. 

So when Allah ﷻ says here that there are members of the People of the Book who believe in Him, He is speaking about those who have not uttered such blasphemies. They have not declared the divinity of Isa (AS). They have not made any of the statements that some tribes of Bani Israel were known to make. In other words, the integrity of their faith in Allah ﷻ is validated by Allah ﷻ Himself in this ayah. According to what He defines as true faith - what we consider proper aqeedah - there may be groups among them that still maintain the correct understanding about Allah ﷻ.

The ayah then mentions **وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ** - belief in the Last Day. Are there not variations in belief regarding the afterlife among different groups of Christians and Jews? Absolutely. Among many Christian groups, there exists a confused concept of the hereafter. Popular culture reflects this confusion - cartoons depict heaven as a cloudy place where everyone wears white and flies around with wings. These images exist because many have lost clarity about what the afterlife actually looks like. However, the correct belief in the Akhirah was revealed to every messenger. It is possible that some groups among the People of the Book have maintained that intact, particularly if they never came into contact with later innovations or the teachings of our Messenger ﷺ.

This raises an important point: we should not judge all of them uniformly. This is a matter whose full reality we may not comprehend, but Allah ﷻ certainly does. He is speaking about those who have preserved the essence of divine guidance.

The ayah continues: **وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ** - they enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong. These are the very actions that Allah ﷻ commands all believers to perform, and this group fulfills them. Then Allah ﷻ adds something remarkable: **وَيُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ** - they compete with one another in doing good deeds. They don't just do good; they race toward it with eagerness and enthusiasm.

Some scholars say this ayah refers to those from the People of the Book who have already accepted Islam. Others say it refers to those who will eventually become Muslim. Yet another view holds that it includes those who may never be exposed to Islam but who never abandoned the revelation they had and lived by it faithfully. Whatever the case, Allah's verdict is clear: **وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ** - those are among the righteous. This is a powerful declaration, an exaltation of their rank before Allah ﷻ.

translation: And whatever good they do - never will it be removed from them. And Allah is Knowing of the righteous
commentary:
**وَمَا يَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلَن يُكْفَرُوهُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ**

*And whatever good they do, they will never be denied its reward. And Allah is Knowing of the righteous.*

Allah ﷻ continues by assuring us: **وَمَا يَفْعَلُوا۟ مِنْ خَيْرٍ فَلَن يُكْفَرُوهُ** - whatever they do of good will not be buried away from them, will not be denied to them. The word كَفَرَ here carries the meaning of covering or burying, as if their good deeds might be hidden or dismissed. Allah ﷻ emphatically declares this will not happen.

This addresses a critical theological question that arises in many circles: What happens to people who never knew about Islam and died as non-Muslims? Will they go to Hell? Our first response must be: don't worry about them - Allah ﷻ is fair, and you should really be concerned about whose salvation? Your own. If you've already secured your own salvation, then perhaps you have time to worry about others. But this question often stems from an assumption that we ourselves are already saved, when in reality, none of us knows whether our deeds are good enough, whether our iman was sincere enough, whether our intentions were pure enough.

Regardless, Allah ﷻ drops hints about this matter here and there in the Quran. The very fact that it's mentioned sparingly indicates it shouldn't be a primary concern. When Allah ﷻ makes extensive mention of something, it's because it's important; when He barely mentions something, it's because it shouldn't be central to our thinking.

There is a thought-provoking hadith that addresses this question. It describes three types of people on the Day of Judgment: a very old man who was too senile to leave his house when a prophet came; a child who died too young to understand; and a man who lived at a time when there were no prophets around. Each pleads that they had no proper exposure to the message. Allah ﷻ commands all three to jump into the Hellfire. Some hesitate and return to plead their case, claiming it wasn't their fault. Allah ﷻ responds that they claimed they would have believed without seeing Him, yet now that they see Him, they still don't trust Him. Those who jumped without question were actually entering Paradise. The point: if you're not tested here, you will be tested there. Nobody escapes the test. If there is goodness in your heart, it will manifest there; if there isn't, that too will manifest.

Allah ﷻ concludes the ayah: **وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِٱلْمُتَّقِينَ** - Allah is fully knowing, completely knowledgeable regarding people who possess taqwa. He even calls them people of taqwa, acknowledging their consciousness of Him.

translation: Indeed, those who disbelieve - never will their wealth or their children avail them against Allah at all, and those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ**

*Indeed, those who disbelieve - never will their wealth or their children avail them against Allah at all. And those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally.*

After describing the righteous among the People of the Book, Allah ﷻ now turns to their opposites: **إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟** - no doubt, those who have disbelieved. **لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا** - neither their wealth nor their children will benefit them against Allah at all, will not make them free of need before Allah.

This ayah has immediate historical context. Those whose wealth and children will not benefit them in the Hereafter - what are they doing with that wealth and those children now? They are using them to oppose the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. The reference here is to the Quraysh who were compiling their wealth to relaunch their assault against the Muslims after their defeat at Badr.

Consider the situation: at Badr, a thousand warriors from Makkah came out to fight. Seventy of their top leaders died. When they returned, you can imagine the scene. The Arabs had a tradition where women would come out, beat their chests and wail loudly. The entire city was filled with the sounds of mourning and humiliation. 

A year passes, and they are preparing themselves again. But this time, three thousand will march out - three times the army that came to Badr. They were so determined to humiliate the Muslims that they even brought their women and children with them to the battlefield. They reasoned: if fear makes us want to flee, we'll remember that our women are here, and if we run, the women will be taken by the Muslims. We cannot stand that humiliation, so we're taking them as motivation to keep fighting. This shows how driven they were.

Before engaging in such a military campaign, massive resources must be allocated. A huge portion of the budget must go toward this military endeavor. Allah ﷻ acknowledges this reality and then delivers His verdict: **وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ ۚ هُمْ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ** - those are the dwellers, the companions of the Fire, in which they will remain exclusively. May Allah ﷻ not make us from among them.

translation: The example of what they spend in this worldly life is like that of a wind containing frost which strikes the harvest of a people who have wronged themselves and destroys it. And Allah has not wronged them, but they wrong themselves
commentary:
**مَثَلُ مَا يُنفِقُونَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيهَا صِرٌّ أَصَابَتْ حَرْثَ قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَأَهْلَكَتْهُ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ**

*The example of what they spend in this worldly life is like that of a wind containing frost which strikes the harvest of a people who have wronged themselves, and it destroys it. And Allah has not wronged them, but they wrong themselves.*

Now Allah ﷻ addresses their spending directly. **مَثَلُ مَا يُنفِقُونَ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا** - the example of what they spend in this worldly life. Allah ﷻ presents a powerful parable: **كَمَثَلِ رِيحٍ فِيهَا صِرٌّ**- it is like a wind in which there is صِرّ. The word صِرّ refers to an intense, freezing coldness. This wind has such a freezing quality that when it blows over something, that thing turns to ice.

**أَصَابَتْ حَرْثَ قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ** - it struck, it targeted the حَرْث (crop) of a people who had wronged themselves. The word حَرْث specifically refers to a crop that has reached complete maturity, ready to be harvested. Imagine: you've worked the entire year, your crop has reached its peak, and tomorrow morning you plan to harvest it. But that night, a freezing wind comes, and the entire crop freezes. All of it dies and goes to waste.

Notice the precise description: **قَوْمٍ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ** - a people who wronged themselves. Here, the wrongdoers are the Quraysh, and the crop represents all their assets. Literally, their assets are frozen.

**فَأَهْلَكَتْهُ** - then that wind destroyed it violently. The word هَلاك in Arabic refers to a violent death, a merciless destruction. This wind ruthlessly annihilated that entire crop.

Allah ﷻ then clarifies: **وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِنْ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** - Allah did not wrong them at all; rather, they were wronging themselves. Notice the verb form here: **يَظْلِمُونَ** - they are wronging, present tense. This is different from past stories in the Quran where Allah ﷻ uses **كَانُوا يَظْلِمُونَ** - they used to wrong. Here there's no need for that past construction because this parable describes what they are doing now, in the present moment. The investments they are making right now to oppose the Messenger of Allah ﷺ will go to waste.

This is pre-Uhud commentary. The Battle of Uhud hasn't happened yet. Allah ﷻ is informing the believers in preparation: look, the enemy is gearing up for war, but all their investment will be wasted. Don't be intimidated.

translation: O you who have believed, do not take as intimates those other than yourselves, for they will not spare you [any] ruin. They wish you would have hardship. Hatred has already appeared from their mouths, and what their breasts conceal is greater. We have certainly made clear to you the signs, if you will use reason
commentary:
**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ بِطَانَةً مِّن دُونِكُمْ لَا يَأْلُونَكُمْ خَبَالًا وَدُّوا۟ مَا عَنِتُّمْ قَدْ بَدَتِ ٱلْبَغْضَآءُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ وَمَا تُخْفِى صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ ۚ قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ**

*O you who have believed, do not take as intimates those other than yourselves, for they will not spare you [any] ruin. They wish you would have hardship. Hatred has already appeared from their mouths, and what their breasts conceal is greater. We have certainly made clear to you the signs, if you will use reason.*

Having made us aware of the outside enemy - both certain elements among the People of the Book and the Quraysh who are conspiring and gearing up for war - Allah ﷻ now gives powerful advice about internal security.

**يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟** - O you who have iman. **لَا تَتَّخِذُوا۟ بِطَانَةً مِّن دُونِكُمْ** - do not take بِطَانَة from other than yourselves, anyone other than yourselves. Let's examine this critical word بِطَانَة.

The word بِطَانَة is one of many Arabic words for friend, but it has a very specific meaning. It comes from بَطْن, which means stomach or belly. What does friendship have to do with the stomach? There's an expression: "he has a light stomach," meaning he cannot hold a secret. A بِطَانَة is a friend who holds your secrets, who swallows them and keeps them in his belly without regurgitating them. These are intimate confidants with whom you've had such a long-standing relationship that you can talk about anything personal, and you trust completely that it won't go anywhere. They won't spill their guts to anyone else.

The historical situation in Medina made this warning necessary. Many Muslims had old friends from childhood who were Christians or Jews, because they themselves had been Christians or Jews before accepting Islam. Your old buddy is still your old buddy. But now you sit in meetings with the Messenger of Allah ﷺ discussing plans and preparations - intelligence about what's happening in Makkah, strategies for the upcoming conflict. Then later, this same person goes and casually meets his old friend. They're having coffee together, and the friend asks, "So what's going on?" The Muslim doesn't think anything of it - this is his بِطَانَة, someone who keeps his secrets - so he shares what was discussed.

But Allah ﷻ says: you haven't realized that although he's your friend, you are now in a state of war. When you're in a state of war, these friendships transform. Other loyalties will surface, and the very same people who used to be best friends will become completely different toward you.

A contemporary story illustrates this painfully well. A teacher once lived in Palestine before the occupation. Some of his neighbors were Christians, some were Jews, and they all lived together peacefully. They would sit together at night, pray together, share jokes - a very casual, warm atmosphere with no religious debate or tension. Then the invasion happened. Muslims and Christians remained friends, but overnight, the Jewish friend was no longer a friend. Doors shut. Petitions were filed to remove Muslim families from the neighborhood. Yards were vandalized. Greetings went unanswered. Parents told their children not to play with Muslim children. Overnight, everything changed as other allegiances won out. This is what war does - it can transform lifelong relationships and reveal sides of people you never expected.

This is what Allah ﷻ is cautioning the Muslims of Medina about. **لَا يَأْلُونَكُمْ خَبَالًا** - they will leave no stone unturned. The word أَلَى means to leave something deficient or unfinished. Allah ﷻ says: when it comes to you, they will spare no effort in causing you خَبَال.

What is خَبَال? It refers to harm that comes to the physical body, particularly chronic pain that doesn't go away. The Arabs use the expression "love has ruined his intellect" - when someone's obsessive love or extreme sadness has taken away their mind. When one part of the body goes through extreme pain and becomes almost wasted, and that pain persists, that is خَبَال.

Allah ﷻ is saying: don't hold onto بِطَانَة from other than yourselves, because they will spare no effort in trying to cause you constant, persistent pain - the kind that won't go away. If they find an opportunity to hurt you, to get you where it hurts most, they will.

**وَدُّوا۟ مَا عَنِتُّمْ** - they would love whatever causes you harm. The word عَنَت actually refers to pain that causes death, an even more severe form. Notice the progression in the ayah: first, persistent pain (خَبَال), then they wish for you the kind of pain that leads to your destruction (عَنَت).

Now, it's important to note what Allah ﷻ is not saying. He is not saying: don't be nice to them anymore, don't maintain relations, don't be courteous, don't be friends, don't be the best neighbor to them. Keep all of that as it was. The only thing Allah ﷻ is cautioning about is: don't spill your secrets. Don't share private affairs. Don't reveal classified information thinking it's no big deal. It is a big deal.

**قَدْ بَدَتِ ٱلْبَغْضَآءُ مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ** - the words of animosity, the بَغْضَاء, have already come out from their mouths. The word بَغْضَاء refers to a specific type of hatred - the desire to humiliate someone because you hate them. It's the opposite of love. This sentiment of wanting to humiliate and despise you has already become apparent, has already been exposed, though by definition such feelings usually stay hidden.

How have they been exposed? **مِنْ أَفْوَٰهِهِمْ** - from their mouths. There are instances where they've said things about the Messenger ﷺ, where they've said things about the Quran, about Islam. Certain statements have been made, so you know the kind of hatred that sits there.

Consider our contemporary situation. How many nasty, horrific things are being said about Islam in the media? Deeply offensive, hateful rhetoric that reveals the animosity beneath.

But then Allah ﷻ says something chilling: **وَمَا تُخْفِى صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ** - but what their chests are hiding is even greater, even bigger. You think what they have said is offensive? Wait until you discover what they're thinking. What Allah ﷻ sees inside their chests, what they say publicly on television is what they know won't get them banned or in trouble. But what's going on inside their minds is far, far worse.

This manifests in small, personal ways as well. I once knew a pharmacist who would always be friendly when I came in - "How's it going? You're from Pakistan, right? I have a lot of Muslim customers." Just casual small talk. One day I walked into the store, but he didn't see me yet. The pharmacy is always in the back with aisles in front, so I could hear him talking to a friend from Serbia. He was saying, "You guys should really kill more Muslims, you should get rid of all of them." His friend seemed uncomfortable, but the pharmacist was insistent. Then I came around the aisle, and immediately: "Hey! How's it going? How are you doing?" Friendly on one side, vicious hatred on the other. This is what comes out in their private circles. **وَمَا تُخْفِى صُدُورُهُمْ أَكْبَرُ** - what they hide in their chests is far greater.

Allah ﷻ concludes: **قَدْ بَيَّنَّا لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ** - We have clarified, We have made extremely clear to you all these miraculous signs, these ayat. **إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** - if in fact you are willing to apply your intellect, if you are willing to think about it. The guidance is there, crystal clear. The question is whether we will reflect upon it and act accordingly.

translation: Here you are loving them but they are not loving you, while you believe in the Scripture - all of it. And when they meet you, they say, "We believe." But when they are alone, they bite their fingertips at you in rage. Say, "Die in your rage. Indeed, Allah is Knowing of that within the breasts
commentary:
**هَـٰٓأَنتُمْ أُو۟لَآءِ تُحِبُّونَهُمْ وَلَا يُحِبُّونَكُمْ وَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْكِتَـٰبِ كُلِّهِۦ وَإِذَا لَقُوكُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا وَإِذَا خَلَوْا۟ عَضُّوا۟ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱلْأَنَامِلَ مِنَ ٱلْغَيْظِ ۚ قُلْ مُوتُوا۟ بِغَيْظِكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ**

This ayah addresses a painful reality for the believers: "Here you are, loving them, but they do not love you." Allah ﷻ confronts the believers with their misplaced affection toward those who harbor deep animosity against them. You extend your hand in friendship, you show them kindness, yet they refuse to reciprocate that love.

What makes this even more striking is the contrast Allah ﷻ highlights: "And you believe in the Book, all of it." The believers are the ones who should be loved because they have faith in all of the revealed scriptures. They honor the Torah, the Gospel and the Quran. Yet despite this comprehensive belief, despite being worthy of respect and love, here they are loving people who don't deserve that kind of affection—people who actually reject parts of their own scripture.

This complete belief in all of Allah's ﷻ revelation is actually part of the reason these enemies don't love the believers. When you believe in the complete Book, it exposes what they choose to ignore, what they refuse to accept.

Then Allah ﷻ reveals their two-faced behavior: "And when they meet you, they say, 'We have faith too.'" When the Muslim community spreads, when they see the masjid being built, when they encounter believers in public spaces, they present themselves as part of the same faith community. They say all the right things—yes, we believe just like you, we have so much in common.

But the reality is starkly different: "And when they are alone, they bite their fingertips in rage at you." The image Allah ﷻ uses is visceral—they're literally chewing on their fingers out of fury. They can't stand you. They can't stand your progress, your success, your very existence. That rage burns inside them when no one else is watching.

This pattern continues today. You might hear a preacher talk beautifully about mercy and kindness, but then turn around and declare Islam to be evil and Muslims to be followers of the devil. The pleasant face in public, the seething hatred in private—it's the same pattern Allah ﷻ exposed fourteen centuries ago.

Allah ﷻ commands the Prophet ﷺ to respond to this rage: "Tell them: Die in your rage." Let that fury consume you. You will not succeed in harming what Allah ﷻ has protected. "Certainly, Allah is fully aware of what is in the hearts." He knows the distinction between those who harbor this rage and those who don't. Nothing is hidden from Him ﷻ.

translation: If good touches you, it distresses them; but if harm strikes you, they rejoice at it. And if you are patient and fear Allah, their plot will not harm you at all. Indeed, Allah is encompassing of what they do
commentary:
**إِن تَمْسَسْكُمْ حَسَنَةٌ تَسُؤْهُمْ وَإِن تُصِبْكُمْ سَيِّئَةٌ يَفْرَحُوا۟ بِهَا ۖ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ لَا يَضُرُّكُمْ كَيْدُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ مُحِيطٌ**

Allah ﷻ continues to expose the mindset of these enemies: "If any good touches you, it grieves them." Modern examples abound. When stores decide to carry halal products—a simple business decision to serve a customer base—there are boycotts and outrage. "We can't support those stores because they're helping Muslims." It's not even about the progression of Islam; it's just merchants trying to make an honest living. Yet even this kind of good, even this kind of success in the most mundane aspects of life, becomes offensive to them. Any blessing that comes to you, they hate it.

"And if any misfortune befalls you, they rejoice at it." When calamity strikes the Muslim community, they are overwhelmed with joy. This is the depth of their enmity.

But then Allah ﷻ provides the solution: "And if you continue to be patient and maintain your taqwa, their plot will not harm you in the least." The path forward isn't to obsess over their hatred or to be consumed by their schemes. The path is sabr—perseverance in the face of difficulty—and taqwa—protecting yourself from displeasing Allah ﷻ by maintaining your values.

"Certainly, Allah encompasses all that they do." The word مُحِيطٌ means to completely encircle something. Imagine animals running wild, thinking they're free, not realizing there's an invisible fence ahead. The owner sits calmly because he knows they cannot escape. That's the reality of the enemies' plotting. Allah ﷻ has encircled what they're doing. They're not going anywhere. They cannot escape His ﷻ knowledge and His ﷻ power.

This is an essential teaching in our religion. Allah ﷻ makes us aware of what the enemy is thinking, but He ﷻ tells us this to let us know that He'll deal with it, not us. We see this pattern with the Prophet ﷺ himself. When his own uncle, Abu Lahab, celebrated the death of the Prophet's ﷺ infant son—his own grandnephew—and went around the neighborhood yelling that Muhammad's ﷺ name would be discontinued, that must have hurt deeply. Your own blood, your next-door neighbor, celebrating such a personal tragedy.

What did Allah ﷻ do? He didn't tell the Prophet ﷺ to focus on that pain. He revealed Surah Al-Kawthar and changed the entire thought process: "I have given you Al-Kawthar. So pray to your Lord and sacrifice." Don't worry about the enemy—I'll deal with him. "Indeed, it is your enemy who is cut off." That's the essence of our religion captured in those brief ayahs.
