You have a problem? Don't be overwhelmed by it. Be grateful to Allah ﷻ and do what comes with gratitude. Pray and sacrifice. The enemy who thinks he's won? He's the one who will be discontinued.

This same message appears throughout Surah Aal-E-Imran. At the beginning, Allah ﷻ said to those who disbelieved: "Enjoy yourselves a little, then you'll be driven to the Fire." At the end of the surah, He ﷻ will say: "Don't let their activities in the land deceive you." The message is consistent—don't be overwhelmed by what they're doing. You have your work to focus on.

translation: And [remember] when you, [O Muhammad], left your family in the morning to post the believers at their stations for the battle [of Uhud] - and Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
**وَإِذْ غَدَوْتَ مِنْ أَهْلِكَ تُبَوِّئُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ مَقَـٰعِدَ لِلْقِتَالِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ**

Now we enter the commentary on the Battle of Uhud. But first, Allah ﷻ will remind us of what happened at Badr—that landmark victory in Islam—to prepare us for what's about to be discussed here. There will be a comparison between these two battles.

"And when you left your household in the morning to position the believers at their battle stations." The word غَدَوْتَ comes from the root that means "tomorrow" or the later part of the next morning. The Muslims had set up camp, and in the morning, the Prophet ﷺ came out from his family to organize the forces.

تُبَوِّئُ means to settle someone exactly where you want them. The root بَوَّأَ means to move somewhere precisely, with clear intention—this neighborhood because of these schools, because of this highway, because of this specific advantage. It's placement with purpose. This same word is used in the Quran when describing Paradise: "They will settle therein wherever they wish." The believer will enter Jannah and choose exactly where they want to be—this is the perfect place, I've always wanted to be on top of a mountain, and there it is.

Here, the Messenger ﷺ is physically positioning the believers مَقَـٰعِدَ لِلْقِتَالِ—at their stations for fighting. مَقَـٰعِدَ means the places to sit, to be stationed. He's organizing the army: you three over here, you archers on that mountain, you with the spears here, the horsemen there. Each group is settled into their tactical position with clear instruction to remain there and not move, because they're getting ready for battle.

"And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing." This combination of attributes is mentioned here because when soldiers are getting ready for battle, there's a lot of talking happening. The nervous energy, the anxiety—what's going to happen? Are you scared? Maybe a little. This conversation fills the air among the troops. It's hard to remain calm. Allah ﷻ hears all of it, knows all of it—every word spoken, every thought racing through their minds.

translation: When two parties among you were about to lose courage, but Allah was their ally; and upon Allah the believers should rely
commentary:
**إِذْ هَمَّت طَّآئِفَتَانِ مِنكُمْ أَن تَفْشَلَا وَٱللَّهُ وَلِيُّهُمَا ۗ وَعَلَى ٱللَّهِ فَلْيَتَوَكَّلِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ**

"When two groups among you were about to lose heart." The word هَمَّت means to think about something for a long time, to want something but have been contemplating it for a while. تَفْشَلَا means to lose heart, to become disheartened. The root فَشَل originally meant to lose motivation, to have the wind taken out of your sails. In modern Arabic, it's used for failure, but in classical usage, it referred to losing the zeal, the energy to continue.

These two groups were Banu Haritha and Banu Salama. They were reluctant about going into battle. When the fighting started, they actually retreated for a while, gripped by fear. But then they recovered, turned back and fought again. Allah ﷻ mentions that back-and-forth struggle they experienced.

But immediately after mentioning this moment of weakness, Allah ﷻ provides the reconciliation: "While Allah was the protective friend of them both." This is both a gentle condemnation and a note of honor. Yes, they wavered, but they were still under Allah's ﷻ protection and guardianship. They recovered and fulfilled their duty. Both of these groups actually took pride in being mentioned in the Quran—mentioned first in a moment of difficulty, but then immediately affirmed with Allah's ﷻ care for them.

"And upon Allah let the believers place their trust." This is the lesson extracted from their experience. Exclusively upon Allah ﷻ should believers rely, not on their own strength or numbers or strategies. When you place your trust in Him ﷻ alone, even moments of weakness can be transformed into recovery and steadfastness.

### Historical Context: The Battle of Uhud

To understand these ayahs fully, we need to grasp the circumstances of the Battle of Uhud. The Quraysh had prepared a massive force—3,000 warriors, including women and children brought along for psychological motivation. This was a stark difference from Badr.

The battle dynamics were entirely different from Badr. At Badr, the Quraysh didn't have a strong personal motivation—a skirmish had broken out, and things escalated. They even underestimated the Muslims initially. But by Uhud, they had been humiliated at Badr. Revenge became a powerful motivator. The energy and zeal they brought to Uhud was incomparable to their previous encounter. They didn't underestimate the Muslims this time. They came heavily armed, with women and children to motivate their warriors not to retreat.

Their great military leader, Abu Jahl, had been killed at Badr. Now the battalion was under Abu Sufyan's leadership, with Khalid ibn al-Walid—before his acceptance of Islam—playing a major strategic role.

The positioning of forces became critical to the battle's events. The Prophet ﷺ stationed about fifty archers on top of Mount Uhud with explicit orders: even if you see birds eating from our corpses, do not come down. In plain terms, under no circumstances were they to leave their positions.

Initially, the Muslims were annihilating the enemy. The 3,000-strong Quraysh force was being decimated. Seeing this unexpected victory, many of the archers on the mountain had a difference of opinion. The Prophet ﷺ told them not to come down unless birds were eating from Muslim corpses, but it was the enemy's corpses being scattered across the battlefield. Some argued they could come down; others insisted that the command meant they shouldn't move until receiving new instructions. A significant number descended from their post.

Khalid ibn al-Walid, with his sharp military mind, saw the Muslims coming down from the mountain. The Quraysh were in full retreat—even abandoning their women and children—but Khalid called a group of soldiers, made a massive U-turn, came back from behind, climbed the mountain, and attacked the Muslims from their rear. The archers had left the very post they were supposed to guard.

Chaos erupted. Dust and sand flew everywhere, visibility dropped to almost nothing. In the confusion, the Prophet ﷺ was struck on his jaw so severely that one of his teeth fell out, and he lost consciousness. When people saw him bloodied on the ground, rumors spread that he had been killed.

The psychological impact was devastating. When some Muslims heard that the Prophet ﷺ had been killed, they lost the will to fight. Some literally stuck their swords in the ground and sat down, thinking: What's the point now?

But then the Prophet ﷺ regained consciousness. In his pain and with his face covered in blood, he said, "How will a nation prosper—a nation that has covered their Prophet's face with blood?" At that moment, revelation came down, correcting even the Messenger ﷺ: you have no right to say that. We'll see this correction later in the surah.

The Muslims formed a protective formation around the Prophet ﷺ and retreated up the mountain. Khalid ibn al-Walid wanted to pursue them and finish the battle, but Abu Sufyan disagreed. The Muslims now had the strategic military advantage on higher ground—they could simply throw rocks and boulders down on anyone trying to climb up. Abu Sufyan made the calculated decision: we've gotten our revenge, we should leave. They exchanged some words, praised their false gods and departed.

But there's a significant event that Allah ﷻ doesn't even dignify with mention in these ayahs. The Muslim army initially numbered around 1,000. About 300 of them were aligned with Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the leader of the hypocrites. Halfway to the battlefield—before fighting even began—he walked away with his followers, claiming the whole thing was orchestrated, that both sides were working together and the Muslims were just being led to slaughter.

When he left, he took 300 warriors with him. The Muslim army already had two tribes uncertain about the battle—Banu Haritha and Banu Salama. Now a third of the entire force just disappeared. The Quraysh had a 3-to-1 advantage initially. Now it became nearly 4-to-1, perhaps even greater. The scales of battle had been completely altered. Muslim morale had been injured.

Yet Allah ﷻ makes a point to mention those two wavering tribes who still held out and fought, but doesn't even dignify the 300 hypocrites with a mention in these ayahs. That's how insignificant they are in His ﷻ sight. They will be mentioned only in passing much later in the surah—a profound expression of their worthlessness.

This is the context in which these ayahs were revealed, reminding the believers of the Battle of Badr's lessons as they processed the difficult trials of Uhud.

translation: And already had Allah given you victory at [the battle of] Badr while you were few in number. Then fear Allah; perhaps you will be grateful
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ نَصَرَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِبَدْرٍ وَأَنتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ ۖ فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ

Allah ﷻ opens this passage with a powerful reminder to the believers: He had already aided them at the Battle of Badr when they were in a position of complete weakness. The word نَصَرَ is significant here—it refers specifically to grand, substantial forms of aid, particularly military assistance. This wasn't minor help; this was decisive, overwhelming support from Allah ﷻ.

The phrase وَأَنتُمْ أَذِلَّةٌ describes their state with striking clarity. The word أَذِلَّةٌ comes from ذَلِيل, referring to something completely powerless and subdued—like a domesticated animal that has been beaten into submission and exists entirely at someone else's mercy. The believers at Badr were, in worldly terms, defeated before the battle even began. They had no material advantages, no superior numbers, no military strength to speak of.

Yet it was precisely in this state of weakness that Allah ﷻ granted them victory. The command that follows is therefore deeply connected to this reality: فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ —be conscious of Allah, protect yourselves from His displeasure, maintain awareness of Him. Why? لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ —so that you may be grateful.

Gratitude here operates on multiple levels. The believers should be grateful for the victory itself, certainly. But there's a deeper gratitude being called to: the privilege of fighting in Allah's path alongside the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Not every generation receives such an honor. The very fact that they were chosen to stand with the Prophet ﷺ in establishing Islam is itself a blessing worthy of lifelong gratitude.

translation: [Remember] when you said to the believers, "Is it not sufficient for you that your Lord should reinforce you with three thousand angels sent down
commentary:
إِذْ تَقُولُ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ أَلَن يَكْفِيَكُمْ أَن يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِثَلَـٰثَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُنزَلِينَ

This ayah takes us to a specific moment during the Battle of Uhud. News reached the Muslims in the morning that a massive enemy army—three thousand strong—was advancing toward them. The situation was dire. Morale had already been damaged when Abdullah ibn Ubayy withdrew a third of the army. The remaining believers were outnumbered, unprepared, and facing a formidable force seeking revenge for their defeat at Badr.

In this critical moment, the Messenger of Allah ﷺ delivered a motivational address to strengthen the believers' resolve. He asked them: أَلَن يَكْفِيَكُمْ —wouldn't it be enough for you? The question itself is rhetorical but powerful, designed to shift their focus from the material odds to divine support.

The phrase أَن يُمِدَّكُمْ رَبُّكُم contains beautiful linguistic layers. The root م-د-د means to extend or stretch. When you extend yourself, that's one thing—but when you extend something for someone else, that's إمداد, provision and support. Allah ﷻ uses this same root elsewhere in the Quran: نُمِدُّهُ بِهِ (We extend for him) in terms of wealth and children. Here, the extension is of a different kind: بِثَلَـٰثَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ (three thousand angels).

Notice the precise number—three thousand angels to match three thousand enemy soldiers. But these aren't ordinary reinforcements. They are مُنزَلِينَ —sent down, dispatched specifically by Allah ﷻ. The passive form emphasizes that the angels don't descend on their own authority; they are sent with divine purpose and command.

At this point in the narrative, the Prophet ﷺ is speaking from hope and faith, offering encouragement to his companions. He doesn't yet have revelation confirming this angelic support—he's expressing trust in his Lord. The next ayah will confirm and expand upon this promise.

translation: Yes, if you remain patient and conscious of Allah and the enemy come upon you [attacking] in rage, your Lord will reinforce you with five thousand angels having marks [of distinction]
commentary:
بَلَىٰٓ ۚ إِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ وَيَأْتُوكُم مِّن فَوْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُم بِخَمْسَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ مِّنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ مُسَوِّمِينَ

Allah ﷻ responds to His Messenger's ﷺ statement with emphatic confirmation: بَلَىٰٓ —yes, absolutely, without question. But this confirmation comes with conditions that reveal the spiritual prerequisites for divine intervention.

The first condition is إِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ —if you remain patient, if you persevere. Patience here encompasses staying steadfast in battle, maintaining discipline despite fear, and not breaking rank regardless of the circumstances. The second condition repeats a theme we've seen twice already in this passage: وَتَتَّقُوا۟ —and if you maintain taqwa of Allah. This repetition is deliberate and significant. Taqwa isn't mentioned once in passing; it's the foundation upon which everything else rests.

Then comes a fascinating tactical detail: وَيَأْتُوكُم مِّن فَوْرِهِمْ هَـٰذَا. The word فَوْر means an instant, a sudden moment—we get the English word "forthwith" from a similar concept. Here it describes the enemy attacking at this very instant, catching the Muslims by surprise even as they're still getting into position. The enemy might strike before the believers are fully prepared, in that vulnerable moment of transition.

Allah's promise extends even to this worst-case scenario: يُمْدِدْكُمْ رَبُّكُم —your Lord will extend for you, your Master will support you. Notice the shift from the initial promise. First, the Prophet ﷺ mentioned three thousand angels. Now Allah ﷻ raises the offer: بِخَمْسَةِ ءَالَـٰفٍ (five thousand angels).

But there's more. These aren't just angels; they're مُسَوِّمِينَ —equipped with distinctive markings, riding branded horses prepared for battle. The root س-و-م relates to branding or marking. Some scholars interpret this to mean the angels themselves are marked and identifiable. Others suggest they're riding specially branded war horses. Still others argue it means each angel has specific targets marked for destruction—they know exactly who they're coming for.

The image is overwhelming: five thousand battle-ready angels, fully equipped, dispatched with precision and purpose.

translation: And Allah made it not except as [a sign of] good tidings for you and to reassure your hearts thereby. And victory is not except from Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Wise
commentary:
وَمَا جَعَلَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ لَكُمْ وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُم بِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ

After painting this vivid picture of divine military support, Allah ﷻ clarifies the true purpose of these promises. The material aid isn't the point—the spiritual effect is.

وَمَا جَعَلَهُ ٱللَّهُ إِلَّا بُشْرَىٰ لَكُمْ (And Allah did not make it except as good news for you). The promise of angels serves primarily as بُشْرَىٰ —glad tidings, a source of hope and encouragement. Think about the psychological state of the believers at that moment: they'd just watched a third of their army abandon them; they were facing an enemy three times their size; they were positioned hastily on difficult terrain. Fear and doubt would naturally creep in. The promise of angelic reinforcements wasn't just about military strategy—it was about morale, about replacing anxiety with confidence.

وَلِتَطْمَئِنَّ قُلُوبُكُم بِهِۦ (And so that your hearts may find tranquility through it). The verb تَطْمَئِنَّ speaks to a deep, settled peace—not just surface-level calm but genuine tranquility that reaches the core of one's being. In battle, when arrows are flying and swords are clashing, inner peace becomes the difference between courage and panic. Your heart either trusts completely in Allah's plan, or it wavers. This promise was designed to anchor their hearts in certainty.

Then comes the crucial lesson: وَمَا ٱلنَّصْرُ إِلَّا مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ (And victory is not except from Allah). This statement reframes everything that came before. Yes, Allah ﷻ promised angels. Yes, they would be equipped and ready. But the ultimate point isn't the angels—it's the source of all victory. Whether angels descend or not, whether you're outnumbered or not, whether you have superior weapons or not, victory belongs exclusively to Allah ﷻ. It comes specially from Him (مِنْ عِندِ), from His divine treasury of support.

The ayah concludes with two of Allah's names: ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَكِيمِ —the All-Mighty, the All-Wise. Al-Azeez tells us He has absolute authority to grant victory whenever He wills, to whomever He wills, in whatever measure He wills. Al-Hakeem tells us His decisions about when to send aid, how much to send, and in what form are all products of perfect wisdom. He knows things we don't. His timing is perfect even when we can't understand it.

translation: That He might cut down a section of the disbelievers or suppress them so that they turn back disappointed
commentary:
لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ فَيَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَآئِبِينَ

This ayah explains Allah's purposes in granting victory—what He intends to accomplish through His support. Notice it begins with لِيَقْطَعَ —"so that He may cut." This connects back to the promise of aid; Allah ﷻ supports the believers in order to accomplish specific outcomes against the disbelievers.

طَرَفًا literally means a side, an extremity, or an arm. In the context of battle, this is profound imagery. A soldier uses both arms—one for defense (holding a shield) and one for offense (wielding a weapon). If you cut off one arm, the soldier becomes defenseless or unable to attack. Either way, he's finished. So لِيَقْطَعَ طَرَفًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ means Allah ﷻ will sever a portion of those who disbelieve, effectively crippling their ability to fight.

But it's not just physical defeat. أَوْ يَكْبِتَهُمْ —or He may humiliate them. The verb كَبَتَ is a specific type of humiliation in Arabic. It means to insult someone thoroughly and then dismiss them, to shut the door in their face after degrading them. It's the humiliation that comes with total defeat and rejection. The disbelievers came to Uhud seeking revenge for Badr, hungry for victory and honor. Instead, Allah ﷻ promises they'll return humiliated and shamed.

فَيَنقَلِبُوا۟ خَآئِبِينَ (They will return back as losers). The word خَائِبِينَ comes from خَابَ, meaning to be deprived of what one hoped for, to miss out completely. There's an Arabic expression: مَن خَابَ خَابَ—whoever didn't show up missed out entirely. These disbelievers came with grand ambitions—to crush the Muslims, to kill the Prophet ﷺ, to end Islam once and for all. They will return to Makkah having accomplished none of it, having been deprived of everything they sought, bearing only humiliation and loss.

translation: Not for you, [O Muhammad, but for Allah], is the decision whether He should [cut them down] or forgive them or punish them, for indeed, they are wrongdoers
commentary:
لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ شَىْءٌ أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ أَوْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ

This ayah addresses a specific moment during the Battle of Uhud when the Prophet ﷺ himself was struck and wounded. This is crucial context. Consider what he had endured: the Muslims had established superiority in battle through Allah's help; then some archers disobeyed orders, breaking rank out of greed for spoils of war; this allowed the enemy to regroup and counterattack; the tide turned disastrously; rumors spread that the Messenger ﷺ had been killed; and in the chaos, even the Prophet ﷺ was injured, his blessed face bloodied.

The Messenger of Allah ﷺ—who had shown patience through the persecution in Makkah, who made dua for his enemies at Ta'if even after being stoned—in this moment said: "O Allah, how will You guide a nation that made the face of their prophet bleed?"

It's important to understand that it takes an extraordinary amount of pressure to bring the Prophet ﷺ to such a statement. This wasn't the first hardship he'd faced. He had endured years of mockery, boycott, and violence. But the Battle of Uhud represented something uniquely painful: disobedience from within the Muslim ranks leading to catastrophe, combined with the physical assault on the Prophet ﷺ himself after he'd delivered them such clear instructions.

Allah's response comes immediately and firmly: لَيْسَ لَكَ مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ شَىْءٌ —"You have no authority in this matter whatsoever." Not some authority, not limited authority—absolutely no authority at all. The phrasing is emphatic through repetition and structure: لَيْسَ (there is not), لَكَ (for you), مِنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ (from the matter), شَىْءٌ (anything). Four layers of negation removing any possibility of the Prophet ﷺ having say in who receives guidance or punishment.

Then Allah ﷻ asserts His Own prerogatives: أَوْ يَتُوبَ عَلَيْهِمْ (or He may accept their repentance). Look at the profound wisdom here. The very architects of this battle—Abu Sufyan (RA) who led the Quraysh army, and Khalid ibn al-Waleed (RA) whose brilliant military maneuver turned the battle—both of them eventually became Muslim. Khalid (RA), whose sword caused so much bloodshed among the believers that day, was later given the title سَيْفُ اللَّٰهِ —the Sword of Allah—by the Prophet ﷺ himself. How complete the transformation! How unexpected the turns of Allah's plan!

أَوْ يُعَذِّبَهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ ظَـٰلِمُونَ (Or He may punish them, for indeed they are wrongdoers). Allah ﷻ acknowledges they are currently wrongdoers. If He chooses to punish them, it wouldn't be unjust—they've earned it through their aggression and disbelief. But even wrongdoers can make tawbah, and when they do, their slate is completely clean. This is the hope Allah ﷻ keeps open, even in the midst of battle.

A crucial note about this ayah: it might appear that Allah ﷻ is correcting His Messenger ﷺ harshly. We must understand something fundamental about revelation and prophethood. We are here, at the lowest level of spiritual rank. The Messenger of Allah ﷺ is incomparably higher—but above him is Allah ﷻ. The Master has the right to address His slave as a Master addresses a slave, no matter how honored that slave may be.

There's a beautiful hadith where Allah ﷻ says: "I remain the Lord (الرَّبُّ يَبْقَىٰ رَبًّا) and the slave remains a slave (وَالْعَبْدُ يَبْقَىٰ عَبْدًا)." Even when the Master descends to the lowest heaven in the last third of the night, listening to the duas of His slaves, He remains the Master. And even when the slave ascends as high as the Prophet ﷺ did during Isra and Mi'raj—going beyond the heavens—he remains a slave.

This dynamic doesn't diminish the Prophet ﷺ. In fact, it establishes his validity as a true messenger. Part of what makes him ﷺ the perfect example is his complete submission to Allah ﷻ, his acceptance of divine correction, his recognition that he is a created being receiving guidance from the Creator. No prophet wants to be corrected later; they want immediate knowledge. But these moments of divine instruction humble even the highest of creation and teach us the proper relationship between Creator and created.

translation: And to Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. He forgives whom He wills and punishes whom He wills. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

After correcting His Messenger ﷺ, Allah ﷻ now establishes the cosmic reality that explains why no created being has authority over matters of guidance and punishment. Whenever Allah ﷻ describes His Own grandeur in the Quran, what should it do to us? It should humble us profoundly. Every mention of His majesty is an invitation to recognize our smallness and His greatness.

وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth). Ownership is the foundation of authority. If everything in existence belongs to Him—every atom, every galaxy, every soul, every outcome—then only He has the right to decree what happens to His possessions. We don't own ourselves, let alone others. We don't own the outcomes of battles or the trajectory of hearts.

يَغْفِرُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ (He forgives whom He wills) وَيُعَذِّبُ مَن يَشَآءُ (and He punishes whom He wills). These two statements establish Allah's absolute sovereignty over the two outcomes the Prophet ﷺ had just invoked—forgiveness and punishment. But notice: only Allah ﷻ has the wisdom to know who deserves what, when, and how. Our vision is limited to the present moment, to apparent actions. His vision encompasses past, present, future, seen and unseen, the depths of hearts and the trajectories of souls.

The ayah concludes with two divine names: وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (And Allah is Ghafoor-ur-Raheem). We must understand the morphological significance of these two words. غَفُورٌ is صِيغَةُ مُبَالَغَة —a hyperbolic form meaning extremely forgiving, intensely forgiving. رَحِيمٌ is اسْمُ فَاعِل—an active participle meaning continuously merciful, always merciful.

Why this difference? Allah's mercy is a constant—it never stops flowing, it never diminishes, it's always available. But His forgiveness is not constant; it's extremely abundant but not automatic. If it were constant, someone might say: "I don't need to worry about sin because Allah is always forgiving." No—He is exceedingly forgiving, but that forgiveness must be sought through repentance and righteousness. His mercy manifests in many forms, and forgiveness is one of them, but His mercy might also manifest as consequences that lead someone back to Him.

Typically, when Allah ﷻ concludes an ayah with غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ, it follows mention of human error, opening the door to forgiveness. Here, it's a subtle indication that whatever the Messenger ﷺ said in that moment of intense pressure—even though it was corrected—he should not think himself unforgiven. The door remains open, the mercy remains available.

translation: O you who have believed, do not consume usury, doubled and multiplied, but fear Allah that you may be successful
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ أَضْعَـٰفًا مُّضَـٰعَفَةً ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ

At first glance, this ayah seems to interrupt the narrative about Uhud. In the middle of discussing battle, sacrifice, and divine support, suddenly we encounter a prohibition against riba (interest/usury). This apparent discontinuity has puzzled many readers, and the coherence of this placement reveals profound wisdom.

The connection begins with understanding sacrifice. Believers entering battle must be ready to give up their lives for Allah's cause. But before someone can give up their life, they must first be able to give up a portion of life—and that portion is one's wealth. Money represents an extension of yourself because you sacrifice your time, your energy, your freedom to earn it. You work eight hours, ten hours, twelve hours, doing what an employer requires rather than what you might prefer. Your money is, in a sense, crystallized life.

If someone cannot let go of their wealth for Allah's sake, how can they possibly be expected to let go of their life? If they cling to financial comfort, they'll certainly cling to life itself. The progression is natural: first comes financial sacrifice (spending in Allah's path), then comes physical sacrifice (putting your life on the line in battle).

Now consider the hypocrite's fundamental problem: excessive love of dunya (worldly life). Someone attached to dunya will have difficulty spending their money, and therefore will definitely have difficulty fighting. They won't just want to preserve their wealth—they'll want it to multiply, whether through permissible means or forbidden ones. What brings such a person pleasure? Watching their assets grow exponentially, multiplying upon themselves, without having to touch or reduce them.

This is precisely what riba accomplishes. In the old system, it worked like loan sharking: someone desperate needs twenty dollars for food this week. You lend it but demand thirty back next week. If they can't pay, it becomes forty the week after, then fifty, then sixty—compounding penalties until a small debt becomes an insurmountable burden. This is أَضْعَـٰفًا مُّضَـٰعَفَةً —multiplied multiples, accumulations piled upon accumulations.

The modern banking system operates on the same principle with more sophistication. Lending institutions offer loans to those in need and demand exorbitant returns—20%, 30% interest rates that compound over time. A house worth $80,000 ends up costing $230,000 because of this artificial, compounding debt. Even if the borrower sells everything, they remain in debt because of imaginary value created by the lending system.

This practice represents the ultimate form of materialism and greed—it's the opposite of what believers should do with their wealth. The best use of money is spending it in Allah's path, behind the Messenger ﷺ, supporting the cause of truth. The worst use is أَضْعَـٰفًا مُّضَـٰعَفَةً—exploiting others' desperation to multiply your own wealth through oppression.

لَا تَأْكُلُوا۟ ٱلرِّبَوٰٓا۟ (Do not consume riba). Notice the verb أَكَلَ (to eat/consume) rather than "take" or "collect." This emphasizes that such wealth becomes part of you, integrated into your life and sustenance. It's not just a transaction—it's what you're feeding yourself and your family.

وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (And have taqwa of Allah). This is now the third time taqwa has been mentioned in this passage, and it's the baseline correction to materialism. Fear Allah more than you love wealth. Be conscious of Him more than you're conscious of profit. Protect yourself from His displeasure more than you protect your assets.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ (So that you may achieve success). Real success—فَلَاح —isn't measured in accumulated wealth. It's measured in Allah's pleasure, in earning Jannah, in living and dying on truth.

Interestingly, this same contrast appears near the end of Surah Al-Baqarah: spending in charity versus consuming riba, jihad versus materialism. It's almost a review of lessons already taught, but placed here specifically to address the root cause of failure at Uhud. Some archers left their positions because of greed for war spoils—a lesser form of the same disease that manifests fully in riba.

translation: And fear the Fire, which has been prepared for the disbelievers
commentary:
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ ٱلَّتِىٓ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

The command continues, but now with a more vivid warning: وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلنَّارَ (And protect yourselves from the Fire). This is significant because typically, Allah ﷻ doesn't command taqwa of Himself to those deeply attached to materialism. Why? Because the materialist doesn't fear an unseen God—they respond more to tangible, visual threats.

In Surah Al-Baqarah, when riba was discussed, the command was: "Protect yourselves from a Day in which you will be returned to Allah" (2:281). That's more abstract—a future day, a return to judgment. Here, it's more immediate and visceral: protect yourselves from the Fire itself.

ٱلَّتِىٓ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (Which has been prepared for the disbelievers). The verb أُعِدَّتْ comes from الإِعْدَاد —to completely prepare something so it's ready to be presented. It's not just preparation; it's finishing touches, final readiness. Imagine cleaning your house entirely for guests, everything in place, ready to show off—that's i'dad.

The passive form here is powerful: the Fire has been prepared. By whom? By Allah ﷻ. For whom? للْكَـٰفِرِينَ —for the disbelievers. The Fire isn't still being constructed; it exists now, fully ready, being heated and maintained, waiting for its designated inhabitants.

But notice the warning is addressed to يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ (O you who believe). How can believers be warned about a Fire prepared for disbelievers? Because engaging in riba is associated with kufr. In Surah Al-Baqarah, those who persist in riba after being warned are told they're in a state of war with Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ (2:279). That's the language of disbelief, not just sin.

The message is stark: you claim faith, but if you practice riba, you're approaching the Fire that awaits disbelievers. This is your warning to pull back, to correct course, before you cross a line from which there's no return.

translation: And obey Allah and the Messenger that you may obtain mercy
commentary:
وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

The passage concludes with a direct, encompassing command: وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ (And obey Allah and the Messenger). The verb أَطِيعُوا۟ carries a sense of voluntary submission—not forced compliance but chosen obedience. This is crucial: taqwa, avoiding riba, entering battle, spending wealth—all of it must come from voluntary submission, not mere external pressure.

Notice the pairing: Allah and the Messenger. Throughout the Quran, obedience to the Prophet ﷺ is joined with obedience to Allah ﷻ, establishing that you cannot have one without the other. You cannot claim to follow Allah ﷻ while disregarding His Messenger ﷺ. The Prophet's ﷺ commands, prohibitions, and example are divinely mandated pathways to Allah's pleasure.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (So that you may receive mercy). After mentioning the Fire and its preparation, Allah ﷻ concludes with mercy. This is His way—always leaving the door open, always providing a path back. No matter how far someone has strayed into materialism, into disobedience, into the practices of disbelievers, the door of mercy remains available through repentance and obedience.

The structure of these final ayahs creates a complete spiritual framework:
- Don't consume riba (eliminate the disease of materialism)
- Have taqwa of Allah (establish the foundation of consciousness)
- Fear the Fire (understand the consequences of persisting in wrong)
- Obey Allah and His Messenger (follow the path of safety and success)
- So that you may receive mercy (the ultimate goal and hope)

This conclusion ties back to the opening of the passage. Allah ﷻ aided the believers at Badr when they were weak, asking for their gratitude and taqwa. At Uhud, some failed in taqwa by disobeying orders out of greed. Now the prescription is given: root out that greed through avoiding riba, maintain taqwa, fear the consequences, and commit to complete obedience. Through this path lies divine mercy and ultimate success.

translation: And hasten to forgiveness from your Lord and a garden as wide as the heavens and earth, prepared for the righteous
commentary:
۞ وَسَارِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ

*And rush to forgiveness from your Master and a garden whose expanse is the heavens and the earth, prepared for the people of taqwa.*

This passage represents one of the most beautiful and encouraging sequences in the Quran. It begins with an invitation to Paradise and concludes with Paradise, while acknowledging the reality of human mistakes in between. The structure itself carries a profound message: our journey to Allah ﷻ is framed by His mercy and His promise of Jannah, even when we stumble along the way.

The opening command—وَسَارِعُوٓا۟, "rush" or "race"—is striking. Allah ﷻ doesn't simply tell us to walk toward forgiveness or to eventually make our way there. He commands us to rush, to compete, to move with urgency toward His forgiveness. This urgency reflects the limited time we have in this world and the reality that Shaytan is constantly working to delay our return to Allah ﷻ.

We're racing toward مَغْفِرَةٍ مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ —forgiveness that comes specifically from your Master, your Rabb. This isn't just any forgiveness; it's forgiveness from the One who created you, sustains you, and knows every detail of your struggles. The word "Rabb" emphasizes His care and His intimate knowledge of what you need.

The destination of this race is also وَجَنَّةٍ عَرْضُهَا ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ وَٱلْأَرْضُ (a garden whose width is the heavens and the earth). Think about this description—just the width of Paradise equals the combined expanse of the heavens and the earth. This isn't describing its length, just its width. The image overwhelms the imagination, and that's precisely the point. What Allah ﷻ has prepared transcends anything we can fully conceptualize.

This Paradise has been أُعِدَّتْ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ —thoroughly and completely prepared for the people of taqwa. The word أُعِدَّتْ suggests careful, complete preparation. Every detail has been attended to. But who are these people of taqwa? The following ayahs will paint a picture of their characteristics.

translation: Who spend [in the cause of Allah] during ease and hardship and who restrain anger and who pardon the people - and Allah loves the doers of good
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ فِى ٱلسَّرَّآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ وَٱلْكَـٰظِمِينَ ٱلْغَيْظَ وَٱلْعَافِينَ عَنِ ٱلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ

*Those who spend in ease and in hardship, and who restrain their rage and pardon people—and Allah loves those who excel.*

The characteristics of the people of taqwa now unfold. First, ٱلَّذِينَ يُنفِقُونَ فِى ٱلسَّرَّآءِ وَٱلضَّرَّآءِ (those who spend in ease and hardship). This quality stands in direct opposition to riba (usury), where the mentality is to hold onto money, to collect and multiply it endlessly. The people of taqwa have the opposite mentality—they're constantly looking for opportunities to give, whether times are easy or difficult. They don't wait for financial comfort to be generous. Even when things are tight, when money is scarce, they find a way to spend in the path of Allah ﷻ.

The second quality is وَٱلْكَـٰظِمِينَ ٱلْغَيْظَ (those who restrain their rage). The word الْغَيْظَ refers to a specific type of anger—the kind that fills you completely, that rises from deep within. The root of كَاظِمِ relates to something filled to the very top, like a container of water filled to its brim and sealed so tightly that nothing can escape.

The Arabs used this imagery for the nasal passages. When someone is كَاظِمِ, they're filled with rage all the way up to the nose—they're breathing anger. Their blood is rushing, their fists are clenched, they're shaking with the intensity of it. But it hasn't come out yet. This is the anger that comes right before retaliation, that moment when you're on the verge of doing or saying something you'll regret.

Most people think they've controlled their anger if they don't act on it. But Allah ﷻ is describing people who don't even let it show on their faces. They swallow it completely. They exercise such profound self-control that others don't even detect their internal struggle. This is the level of emotional discipline that characterizes the people of taqwa.

The third quality is وَٱلْعَافِينَ عَنِ ٱلنَّاسِ (those who pardon people). The word الْعَافِينَ comes from عَفْو, which means to have the power to punish someone but choosing instead to forgive them completely, to wipe the slate clean. This isn't a grudging forgiveness where you say the words but hold onto resentment. This is genuine pardon, where you could justifiably take action against someone who wronged you, but you choose mercy instead.

Notice the progression here: controlling your spending leads to controlling your anger, which leads to choosing forgiveness. These qualities build upon each other, each one requiring more refinement of character than the last.

Then comes the pinnacle: وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (and Allah loves those who excel). The ayah began by describing المُتَّقِينَ (the people of taqwa), but it concludes with المُحْسِنِينَ (the people of ihsan, excellence). This progression is crucial. Taqwa is the foundation, but consistently practicing these qualities—giving generously, swallowing anger, pardoning others—elevates a person from taqwa to the highest spiritual state: ihsan. Allah ﷻ declares His love for such people. This same progression from taqwa to ihsan will appear again in Surah Al-A'raf.

translation: And those who, when they commit an immorality or wrong themselves [by transgression], remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins - and who can forgive sins except Allah? - and [who] do not persist in what they have done while they know
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً أَوْ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ ذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ لِذُنُوبِهِمْ وَمَن يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلُوا۟ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ

*And those who, when they commit an obscenity or wrong themselves, remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins—and who can forgive sins except Allah?—and they do not persist in what they did while they know.*

Now comes a profound shift. After describing these elevated qualities, Allah ﷻ acknowledges reality: إِذَا فَعَلُوا۟ فَـٰحِشَةً (when they commit an obscenity). Notice it's إِذَا (when), not إِن (if). This isn't hypothetical—it's inevitable. Even people of taqwa will make mistakes. We're human beings, not angels. The question isn't whether we'll sin, but what we do after we sin.

The word فَـٰحِشَةً refers to anything shameless, indecent, or lewd—actions that lead toward zinā (fornication or adultery). In our context, this could be an inappropriate glance, watching something impure online, listening to vulgar music, or engaging in immodest conversation. The word suggests something done mindlessly, impulsively, without thinking through the consequences.

أَوْ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ (or wrong themselves) covers everything else. Maybe they didn't manage to swallow their anger and said something hurtful. Maybe jealousy got the better of them. Maybe they lied to avoid trouble or told a "small" lie to their parents. Any form of wrongdoing, major or minor, falls under this category.

What distinguishes the people of taqwa isn't that they never sin—it's their immediate response when they do: ذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ (they remember Allah). There's no فَ ("then") before ذَكَرُوا۟—no delay, no gap. The remembrance of Allah ﷻ comes immediately, instantaneously. They don't wait, they don't procrastinate, they don't make excuses. Right in that moment, even in the middle of the sin if possible, they turn to Allah ﷻ.

This is critical to understand because Shaytan has a specific strategy for people who are trying to be close to Allah ﷻ. He works hard to get them to make a mistake, and the moment they do, he attacks with a different weapon: shame and hopelessness. He whispers, "Look what you've done. You've been lowered in the sight of Allah. How can you turn back to Him after this? You hypocrite—why are you even wearing hijab when you just watched that movie? Why go to the masjid when you just spent 12 hours gaming? Stop pretending. You're just doing it for show anyway."

Shaytan wants to repeat his own experience with you. Just as he was expelled from Allah's presence, he wants you to feel expelled, to believe you have no way back. He uses your mistake to distance you from the very acts of worship that would bring you closer to Allah ﷻ.

Many people fall into this trap. They say, "I want to start praying, but I want to have the right reasons first. I want to wear hijab, but not until I'm doing it for the right reasons." But this is often just Shaytan playing word games in your head. Be honest with yourself—if you know something is right, do it. Don't let one mistake become an excuse to abandon everything else.

The people of taqwa understand this. They know there's nowhere to run except to Allah ﷻ. It's like a child who misbehaves and gets disciplined by their mother in a mall. The child might be upset, might even run a few steps away, but where are they going to go? To strangers? No—their whole world is their mother. They have to come back to her. Similarly, when we make mistakes, where else can we turn? Who else can we run to? Allah ﷻ is the only refuge.

So they immediately فَٱسْتَغْفَرُوا۟ لِذُنُوبِهِمْ (seek forgiveness for their sins). Then comes the powerful rhetorical question: وَمَن يَغْفِرُ ٱلذُّنُوبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ (and who can forgive sins except Allah?). This question has two dimensions. First, it reminds the believer that only Allah ﷻ has the authority to forgive—there's no intermediary needed, no impossible hurdle to overcome. You can turn directly to Him. Second, it reminds us that trying to hide from Allah ﷻ is futile. He already knows. Where are you going to run?

But there's a crucial condition: وَلَمْ يُصِرُّوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلُوا۟ (and they do not persist in what they did). The word يُصِرُّوا۟ comes from the root meaning to tie something up tightly in a bag, to hold onto it firmly and not let go. In this context, it means to insist on continuing in sin, to become addicted to it, to schedule it into your life.

This is the real problem—not the single mistake, but the attitude that says, "I know I'll probably do this again in two weeks anyway. I can't help myself. I might as well accept that this is who I am." The people of taqwa haven't given up on themselves like that. They haven't made peace with their sins. Each time they fall, they genuinely want it to be the last time.

This directly connects to a lesson from Surah Al-Baqarah: "Whoever earns a sin and his sin encircles him" (2:81). How does one sin encircle a person? Through addiction, through giving up the fight, through making the sin a permanent part of their life rather than something they're constantly struggling against.

وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (while they know)—they're fully aware of what they're doing. They're not operating in ignorance. They know this is wrong, they know they need to stop, and that awareness drives them back to Allah ﷻ again and again.

translation: Those - their reward is forgiveness from their Lord and gardens beneath which rivers flow [in Paradise], wherein they will abide eternally; and excellent is the reward of the [righteous] workers
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ جَزَآؤُهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ وَجَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ

*Those—their reward is forgiveness from their Master and gardens beneath which rivers flow, wherein they abide eternally. And excellent is the reward of those who work.*

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ (those)—this emphatic pronoun indicates we've reached the conclusion. These people, with all their qualities and all their struggles, have a specific reward: مَّغْفِرَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِمْ (forgiveness from their Master). Again, it's forgiveness specifically from their Rabb, their Lord who knows them intimately and cares for them deeply.

وَجَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ (and gardens beneath which rivers flow). Notice the ayah began with a singular جَنَّةٍ (garden) in ayah 133, but now it's جَنَّـٰتٌ (gardens, plural). What started as one becomes many—the reward multiplies. These gardens have rivers flowing from beneath them, a detail that emphasizes constant freshness, life, and abundance.

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا (abiding eternally therein)—there's no end to this blessing. After all the struggle, after all the falling and getting back up, after all the internal battles with anger and desire, there's permanent peace.

Then comes an expression of divine amazement: وَنِعْمَ أَجْرُ ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (and excellent is the reward of the workers). The verb نِعْمَ is used in Arabic to express amazement and praise. Allah ﷻ Himself is expressing amazement at the quality of reward He's prepared. This is Allah ﷻ declaring, "What an excellent compensation for those who put themselves to work!"

The word ٱلْعَـٰمِلِينَ (the workers) is significant. Allah ﷻ didn't say "believers" (مُؤْمِنِينَ) or "good people" (صَالِحِينَ). He said "workers"—those who actively strive, who put in effort, who don't just believe but act on that belief. This choice of word is a call to action: Get to work. Do something. Change yourself.

There's also a powerful contrast embedded here between فَعَلُوا۟ (they did) in the previous ayah and عَامِلِينَ (workers) in this one. فَعَلُوا۟ suggests impulsive, mindless action—they just did it without thinking. But عَامِلِينَ implies conscious, intentional effort. The lesson? Even your good deeds should be done with full consciousness and intention. Don't let your prayer become autopilot. Remind yourself why you're praying, why you're fasting, why you're giving charity. Put conscious effort into your worship so it doesn't become just another routine you go through without thought.

translation: Similar situations [as yours] have passed on before you, so proceed throughout the earth and observe how was the end of those who denied
commentary:
قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ

*Many examples have already passed before you. So travel through the earth and observe how was the end of those who denied.*

This ayah shifts perspective. After discussing personal spiritual transformation—repentance, controlling anger, spending in Allah's path—now comes a historical reminder. قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ سُنَنٌ (many examples have already passed before you). The word سُنَنٌ refers to patterns, precedents, established ways of life. Many nations, many communities, many peoples have come and gone before us.

فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ (so travel through the earth)—this is both literal and metaphorical. Literally, go see the ruins of past civilizations. Visit historical sites. Read about ancient peoples. But metaphorically, it means to reflect on history, to study what happened to communities before you.

فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (and observe how was the end of those who denied). What was the ultimate outcome for those who had truth but rejected it? Who had guidance but threw it aside? Who were given the same message of repentance, restraint and righteousness, but treated it as a joke?

This connects directly to the previous ayahs. Some people hear these beautiful descriptions of taqwa and Paradise, but they don't act. The message becomes empty words to them. They say, "That's nice," and move on with their lives unchanged. When an entire nation adopts this attitude—when large numbers of people are given clear guidance but refuse to implement it—what happens? History provides the answer.

This ayah serves as both warning and motivation. It's a warning that indifference to divine guidance has consequences, not just individually but collectively. And it's motivation to be among those who actually respond, who become عَامِلِينَ (workers) rather than passive listeners.

translation: This [Qur'an] is a clear statement to [all] the people and a guidance and instruction for those conscious of Allah
commentary:
هَـٰذَا بَيَانٌ لِّلنَّاسِ وَهُدًى وَمَوْعِظَةٌ لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

*This is a clear exposition for humanity, and guidance and instruction for those of taqwa.*

هَـٰذَا —referring to everything that has just been mentioned, from the command to race toward Paradise to the historical lessons about past nations. It's all بَيَانٌ لِّلنَّاسِ (a clear exposition for humanity). The word بَيَانٌ comes from the root meaning to distinguish, to make absolutely clear, to leave no ambiguity. This isn't vague spiritual poetry—it's a crystal-clear message that anyone can understand.

It's for الْنَّاسِ (all people, humanity in general). The guidance isn't hidden or restricted. Everyone has access to this message. But then Allah ﷻ adds two more descriptions that apply specifically to the people of taqwa: وَهُدًى (and guidance) and مَوْعِظَةٌ (and instruction).

The word مَوْعِظَةٌ is particularly significant. It refers to advice that penetrates deep into the heart, that actually affects you internally. You can hear a lot of advice in life that goes in one ear and out the other. You nod politely, say "Thanks," and forget it immediately. But مَوْعِظَةٌ is different—it reaches inside and moves something in you. It changes you.

For the people of taqwa, the Quran isn't just information. It's هُدًى (guidance that shows them the way forward) and مَوْعِظَةٌ (advice that penetrates their hearts and transforms them). They don't just read these ayahs and admire the eloquence. They feel the urgency to race toward Allah's forgiveness. They recognize themselves in the description of people who spend, who control their anger, who genuinely repent. They hear the warning about past nations and resolve not to be among those who hear truth but fail to act on it.

This final ayah brings the passage full circle. It began with a call to action—race toward forgiveness. It described the qualities of those who answer that call. It acknowledged that they'll stumble and sin, but showed how they respond with immediate repentance. It promised them Paradise. And it concluded by reminding us that all of this is بَيَانٌ—perfectly clear. There's no excuse for confusion. The question is simply whether we'll be among the workers (عَامِلِينَ) who take this clarity and act on it, or among those who let it remain just beautiful words that never change their lives.

translation: So do not weaken and do not grieve, and you will be superior if you are [true] believers
commentary:
وَلَا تَهِنُوا۟ وَلَا تَحْزَنُوا۟ وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

After the difficult day at Uhud, Allah ﷻ addresses the believers with two powerful commands: don't be weakened, and don't grieve. The Prophet ﷺ explained that weakness would manifest when the Ummah stops remembering death, develops love for the worldly life, and harbors hatred of death itself.

The word وَلَا تَهِنُوا carries profound meaning. Wahn describes something meant to be hard and strong becoming soft and weak—like a bone that decays, metal that rusts, or wood that gets moisture and becomes fragile. By using this particular term, Allah ﷻ implies that believers are meant to be strong. Their natural state is strength, and weakness is a departure from what they're supposed to be.

This command comes at a critical moment. The Muslim community has just returned from battle where seventy companions achieved martyrdom. Medina is filled with grief. There isn't a family that hasn't lost someone. The Prophet ﷺ himself is deeply saddened by the loss of his uncle Hamza (RA), whose body was mutilated by Hind in an act of revenge for her father, brother and uncle who were killed at Badr. When the Prophet ﷺ saw Hamza's (RA) remains, he said with deep sorrow that there wasn't even a woman to cry for him, since as a Muhajir, he had left his family behind in Makkah. The regulations prohibiting excessive wailing hadn't yet been revealed, so Medina mourned openly.

Yet in this atmosphere of grief, Allah ﷻ says: وَأَنتُمُ ٱلْأَعْلَوْنَ —you are going to be the supreme ones. A'lawoon is the plural of a'la, meaning the highest, the greatest. You will be on top. You will be the winners. But there's a condition: إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ —if you are true believers.

This isn't restricted to that historical context. This is a living ayah for every generation. It tells us that we have nothing to grieve about, nothing to be weakened by. The only ingredient needed for true supremacy in this world is iman. When we look at the humiliation and weakness suffered by Muslims across the world today, the one-word answer—though it requires deep discussion and understanding—is that something is missing in our iman. Allah's ﷻ guarantee is clear: if you have iman, you will have supremacy.

translation: If a wound should touch you - there has already touched the [opposing] people a wound similar to it. And these days [of varying conditions] We alternate among the people so that Allah may make evident those who believe and [may] take to Himself from among you martyrs - and Allah does not like the wrongdoers
commentary:
إِن يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ فَقَدْ مَسَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَآءَ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ puts the Muslims' pain in perspective. إِن يَمْسَسْكُمْ قَرْحٌ —if an injury has barely touched you. The word mass describes the least amount of contact between two things, something that barely touched. And notice that Allah ﷻ doesn't use the word jurh, which refers to a deep wound that penetrates the skin—a gash from a sword or spear. Instead, He uses qarh, which is a swelling, like a mosquito bite or a rash. Something that manifests on the skin but hasn't penetrated deeply. Which is worse? Jurh could kill you. Qarh is annoying, it hurts, it's uncomfortable, but it's not as severe.

So Allah ﷻ is saying: if a swelling has barely touched you—in other words, things could have been much worse. Yes, this is difficult, but it's nothing compared to what it could have been. This is actually better than what might have happened. And if it has touched you, فَقَدْ مَسَّ ٱلْقَوْمَ قَرْحٌ مِّثْلُهُۥ —then it has touched them too, just like this one. They felt the pinch as well. Seventy martyrs from the Muslims at Uhud, but seventy leaders of Quraysh had fallen at Badr. It has happened to them as well.

وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَيَّامُ نُدَاوِلُهَا بَيْنَ ٱلنَّاسِ —And those are the days We alternate between people. The word mudawalah comes from dawaala, which means to create change. When we say "times have changed," we don't mean time itself has changed—time keeps moving—but situations have changed. This is when time itself causes change. But when Allah ﷻ uses the form mudawalah, He's taking credit for it. We are the ones who create days of victory for believers. We create days of victory for disbelievers. We flip those days between people, shifting scenarios as time goes by. The alternation isn't just temporal either—it can be in terms of wealth and power. Sometimes certain people are rich and powerful, then circumstances shift. This prevents wealth from circulating only among the wealthy, which would be social injustice.

Why does Allah ﷻ allow difficult days? وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ —so that Allah may expose those who truly have iman. When knowledge is attributed to Allah ﷻ, the more appropriate understanding is "so that Allah may expose." Like a teacher saying "I give this test so I may know who's really doing well"—meaning, to expose and reveal their performance. This is a means of testing.

Think of it like sports fans. Some are flip-flop fans who support the winning team and abandon them when they lose. Then there are the die-hard fans—like Spike Lee with the Knicks—who stay loyal even when the team is down by forty-five points with ten seconds left, still believing. These are called "true believers." Loyalty is tested not during victory but during loss. These days We alternate between people so We can see where people's loyalties stand, who really has iman.

وَيَتَّخِذَ مِنكُمْ شُهَدَآءَ —and so that He may take from among you martyrs. Allah ﷻ chose to give some people immediate access to Jannah through martyrdom. Imagine one second they're in battle with a sword about to strike, the next moment they're in Jannah. This teleportation, this immediate transition, is part of what makes martyrdom so special.

Two critical reasons have been given so far for why Muslims suffered on the battlefield: first, so Allah ﷻ can expose who truly has iman, because victory doesn't reveal true belief; and second, so He can grant some immediate entry to Paradise through martyrdom.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ —And Allah does not love the wrongdoers. This clarification is crucial. Don't be confused when the disbelievers gain temporary victory. It doesn't mean Allah ﷻ likes them or that they have His support. The Muslims shouldn't think, "They won, so God must be on their side." No—He's making it absolutely clear that He does not love wrongdoers.

translation: And that Allah may purify the believers [through trials] and destroy the disbelievers
commentary:
وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَيَمْحَقَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

This ayah contains one of the most beautiful expressions describing Allah's ﷻ strategy throughout both the Makkan and Madinan periods: وَلِيُمَحِّصَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ —and so that Allah may purify those who believe.

The word mahasa means to purify gold. When gold is purified, you must subject it to extreme temperatures. You heat it, melt it, so all the impurities rise to the top as froth and disappear. This purification process is painful and demanding—it's not like washing something with water. This kind of cleansing requires putting the material through intense heat.

Allah ﷻ compares the Muslim community to gold being purified. He puts the community through extreme temperatures, extreme circumstances, to eliminate the filth—the hypocrites, the weak, the unworthy. These individuals will walk away on their own because they can't handle the difficulty. They might stay when things are easy, but they start leaving when circumstances get tough. And that's exactly what Allah ﷻ wants—the purest band of believers.

By using this metaphor, Allah ﷻ accomplishes several things at once: He explains that going through hardship is part of purification. He compares hypocrites to filth that must be removed. And He compares true believers to precious gold. The parable contains layers of meaning in just a few words.

وَيَمْحَقَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ —and He may gradually destroy the disbelievers. Mahq means to weaken something to the point of death, choking the life out of it little by little. It's the opposite of growth—it's letting something shrink and die slowly. So on one hand, believers are being purified through these trials. On the other hand, every one of these apparent victories for the disbelievers is actually inching them toward their end. Even though they see a battle as their victory, the purification of the believers is the greater victory. Each trial brings the disbelievers closer to their ultimate defeat.

translation: Or do you think that you will enter Paradise while Allah has not yet made evident those of you who fight in His cause and made evident those who are steadfast
commentary:
أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

أَمْ حَسِبْتُمْ أَن تَدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ —Or did you think you would enter Paradise? This isn't the first time this question appears in the Quran. Similar phrasing occurred earlier in Surah Al-Baqarah. Did you just assume you would walk into Jannah?

وَلَمَّا يَعْلَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ جَـٰهَدُوا۟ مِنكُمْ —when Allah has not yet exposed those among you who struggle? Lam means "didn't," but lammaa means "not yet." Allah ﷻ hasn't yet brought out and exposed who really does struggle, who truly strives in His path.

وَيَعْلَمَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ —and He hasn't yet exposed those who truly show perseverance. These qualities can only be revealed when difficulty arrives.

This ayah addresses a specific situation before the Battle of Uhud. Some young companions who hadn't experienced the trials of Badr or the persecution in Makkah were enthusiastic about fighting. When the Prophet ﷺ consulted the Muslims about whether to defend from within Madinah or go out to meet the enemy, Abu Bakr (RA) and others—including the Prophet ﷺ himself—believed they should stay within the city. Interestingly, the hypocrites also wanted to stay in the city, though for different reasons. They wanted an excuse to avoid real combat while claiming they were fighting "in another part of the city."

But these young companions were eager: "We haven't seen the glory of Badr! What are we afraid of? We should go out to the battlefield! We want martyrdom in the path of Allah!" Out of respect for their enthusiasm, the Prophet ﷺ decided to go out and meet the enemy, even though his own judgment was to stay. Later, the Quran would reveal that if people follow their own opinions in most matters, harm will result—but that lesson came afterward.

Who was unhappy with this decision? The hypocrites were unhappy because now they actually had to fight. And this ayah comments on those young, zealous companions.

translation: And you had certainly wished for martyrdom before you encountered it, and you have [now] seen it [before you] while you were looking on
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ كُنتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ ٱلْمَوْتَ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَلْقَوْهُ فَقَدْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ

وَلَقَدْ كُنتُمْ تَمَنَّوْنَ ٱلْمَوْتَ —You were certainly wishing for death. The word tamannaw comes from umniyah (plural: amaani), which describes a specific kind of wish—one where you don't really know what you're asking for, where you don't understand the consequences. You wish for something without grasping its reality. This was their state.

مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَلْقَوْهُ —before you came into contact with it. Before death came to meet you, you were hoping for it. You were eager for martyrdom.

فَقَدْ رَأَيْتُمُوهُ وَأَنتُمْ تَنظُرُونَ —Then you saw it while you were just staring at it. When these companions actually entered battle and witnessed what martyrdom looks like in reality, they were petrified. It's one thing to say "I want to give my life in the path of Allah" in theory. We all make that supplication. But when you see it—when death is actually before you—it's something completely different.

The Companions were human beings. This is a very human phenomenon: to be excited about sacrificing yourself for Allah ﷻ when it's theoretical, but to experience something else entirely when it's real. The Arabs have a saying: "What you hear about is not the same as what you see." When they saw it, they were stunned, just staring into empty space. This is reality hitting them.

This teaches us about the danger of over-zealousness and overreaching. The energy of youth is incredible and needs to be harnessed for the Ummah. These were magnificent companions who fought bravely even though they were initially petrified. But their youthful enthusiasm, if not properly directed, could have led to chaos for everyone. The energy of young people is powerful—it can either build or destroy. It must be channeled with wisdom and guidance, not just raw emotion.

This is a reminder that asking for certain things requires understanding what we're truly asking for. We should be sincere in our aspirations, but also humble about our human limitations and the reality of what sacrifice actually means.

translation: Muhammad is not but a messenger. [Other] messengers have passed on before him. So if he was to die or be killed, would you turn back on your heels [to unbelief]? And he who turns back on his heels will never harm Allah at all; but Allah will reward the grateful
commentary:
وَمَا مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ ۚ أَفَإِي۟ن مَّاتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ ۚ وَمَن يَنقَلِبْ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَن يَضُرَّ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا ۗ وَسَيَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

The previous ayah described how the Companions witnessed death approaching them on the battlefield. There's an important linguistic distinction here: نَظَر means to look at something, perhaps even glance at it, while نَظَار means to stare at it intensely. The Companions saw death coming, then looked at it again in disbelief, and then found themselves staring at it, completely stunned by what they were witnessing.

This ayah addresses the critical moment when rumors spread during the Battle of Uhud that the Messenger ﷺ had passed away. Allah ﷻ responds by declaring: وَمَا مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ - "Muhammad is nothing but a messenger." The name Muhammad appears only four times in the Quran by name, and remarkably, every single time it's mentioned, the word رَسُول (messenger) appears alongside it. This pairing emphasizes his role and mission above all else.

قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ - "Messengers have come much before him and they've gone." This establishes the reality that prophets are mortal, they come and they pass on. Then comes the profound question: أَفَإِي۟ن مَّاتَ أَوْ قُتِلَ ٱنقَلَبْتُمْ عَلَىٰٓ أَعْقَـٰبِكُمْ - "If he died or was killed, would you turn back on your heels?"

This phrase "turning back on your heels" appears throughout this passage and carries powerful imagery. It means to retrace your steps, to retreat, to fall back, to return to the way you were before. The question challenges: are you going to go back to your old ways? Allah ﷻ says in Surah Al-A'raf about those who turned back: إِنَّهُ ٱرْتَدَّ عَلَىٰٓ أَدْبَارِهِۦ - "He turned back on his heels."

وَمَن يَنقَلِبْ عَلَىٰ عَقِبَيْهِ فَلَن يَضُرَّ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا - "And whoever would turn back on his heels, then he won't be harming Allah in any way, shape or form." Your retreat doesn't diminish Allah ﷻ in the slightest.

وَسَيَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ - "And Allah will soon compensate the grateful." Those who had a true commitment to Allah ﷻ will remain committed to Him. The word شَاكِرِين (grateful ones) appears repeatedly in this passage, highlighting that true gratitude manifests as steadfastness in faith.

Interestingly, this exact scenario played out twice in Islamic history. At Uhud, when rumors spread that the Prophet ﷺ had been killed, Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) was among those completely disheartened by the news. Then, when the Messenger ﷺ actually passed away years later, Umar (RA) drew his sword and declared that whoever says the Prophet ﷺ has died, he would take their head off. It was Abu Bakr (RA) who came out and recited this very ayah: وَمَا مُحَمَّدٌ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ. Umar (RA) later said it was as though he heard this ayah for the first time, as if it had just been revealed. Abu Bakr (RA) then made his famous statement: "Whoever used to worship Muhammad should know that Muhammad has died. And whoever worshiped Allah should know that Allah is living and He doesn't die."

This message was so critical, so fundamental to the Ummah's understanding, that it had to be stated and restated. It addresses the traumatic shock this community experienced and would experience.

translation: And it is not [possible] for one to die except by permission of Allah at a decree determined. And whoever desires the reward of this world - We will give him thereof; and whoever desires the reward of the Hereafter - We will give him thereof. And we will reward the grateful
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَن تَمُوتَ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ كِتَـٰبًا مُّؤَجَّلًا ۗ وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا ۚ وَسَنَجْزِى ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ

وَمَا كَانَ لِنَفْسٍ أَن تَمُوتَ - "It is not becoming of any person to die." The phrase مَا كَانَ لِ with أَن together creates a meaning of something being inappropriate or unfitting. How could it not be appropriate for a person to die? إِلَّا بِإِذْنِ ٱللَّهِ - "Except by Allah's permission." You're not allowed to die until He gives the order, until the executive order is issued and the death sentence is passed. Until then, it's not becoming of any person to even taste death.

كِتَـٰبًا مُّؤَجَّلًا - "A written mandate with a declared deadline." The word كِتَاب means something written and mandated. The term مُّؤَجَّل comes from أَجَل, meaning a deadline or appointed time. Together, they emphasize that death is a written decree with a time that has been firmly established and declared by Allah ﷻ.

وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا - "And whoever would want the compensation of worldly life, We will grant it to him from it." The word ثَوَاب comes from a verb used frequently with the meaning of returning. ثَابَ، يَثُوبُ means to keep going back to someone as a reference point or for advice and consultation. The place people keep returning to is called مَثَابَة, as in the ayah وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا ٱلْبَيْتَ مَثَابَةً لِّلنَّاسِ - "And when We made the House a place of return for people."

From this same root we get ثَوَاب, which refers to compensation—whatever work you did finally came back to you, making its way back to compensate you. The verb carries within it the meaning of رُجُوع (return). So whoever wants the payback, the compensation of worldly life, Allah ﷻ says نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا - "We will grant it to him from it."

وَمَن يُرِدْ ثَوَابَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا - "And whoever would want the compensation of the next life, We will grant him from that too." Someone who puts in hard work, who is diligent, who studies and researches, who works hard in their company—Allah ﷻ allows that person to get promotions, to advance higher and higher. If you want it, you'll get it. You'll receive these worldly rewards.

This mirrors what we see today in self-help seminars about how to move up in the world, with real-life stories about how people made it. Yes, you have to put the work in. Allah ﷻ says if you do it for the dunya, you'll get it. If you put it in for the Akhirah, you'll get from that too.

وَسَنَجْزِى ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ - "And soon We will compensate those who are grateful." Notice how the word شَاكِرِين (grateful ones) keeps appearing in this discussion. The implication here is clear: you have the option. You want to work for the inferior thing? Notice Allah ﷻ uses the word دُنْيَا, which is the feminine form of أَدْنَى .أَدْنَىٰ by definition means something inferior, something closer but lesser. This closer life, this inferior life—you're going to put work into it? Or will you put work in for something that will last forever?

translation: And how many a prophet [fought and] with him fought many religious scholars. But they never lost assurance due to what afflicted them in the cause of Allah, nor did they weaken or submit. And Allah loves the steadfast
commentary:
وَكَأَيِّن مِّن نَّبِىٍّ قَـٰتَلَ مَعَهُۥ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ فَمَا وَهَنُوا۟ لِمَآ أَصَابَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَمَا ضَعُفُوا۟ وَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

وَكَأَيِّن مِّن نَّبِىٍّ - "And how many prophets..." This expression كَأَيِّن in the Quran is actually rhetorical. It's like when you say to someone, "How many times have I helped you?" It's not really a question—you're saying "I have helped you many times," but it's put in the form of a question for emphasis. So Allah ﷻ is saying: How many different prophets have there been?

قَـٰتَلَ مَعَهُۥ رِبِّيُّونَ كَثِيرٌ - "Alongside which many godly people fought." The word رِبِّيُّونَ is closely related to two other words: رَبَّانِيّ and رُبِّيّ. Both occur in the Quran and mean a godly person, someone associated with the Lord (رَبّ). This is the root from which the Jewish tradition derived the term "rabbi."

فَمَا وَهَنُوا۟ لِمَآ أَصَابَهُمْ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - "Then they didn't become weak because of whatever came at them in the path of Allah." They were supposed to remain strong, and they remained that way. They didn't get weakened despite whatever targeted them while fighting in Allah's cause.

وَمَا ضَعُفُوا۟ - "And they didn't become weakened." The verb ضَعُفَ is interesting because it doesn't have a passive form in the normal sense, so the meaning here is "they didn't allow themselves to become weakened."

وَمَا ٱسْتَكَانُوا۟ - "And they didn't give in." This is one of the most fascinating verbs in this Surah. The root is كَانَ (to be), and when you add the prefix إِسْتَ, creating إِسْتَكَانَ, it means to let things be, to give in to circumstances. It's the attitude of "whatever happens, happens." This is مِسْكِين at its core—someone who has given up, who lets things be, who surrenders to their environment passively.

The believers described here didn't become like that. They didn't let their environment get the best of them. They didn't adopt the fatalistic attitude of just accepting whatever came their way.

وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ - "And Allah loves those who persevere," those who remain constant and patient through trials.

translation: And their words were not but that they said, "Our Lord, forgive us our sins and the excess [committed] in our affairs and plant firmly our feet and give us victory over the disbelieving people
commentary:
وَمَا كَانَ قَوْلَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَإِسْرَافَنَا فِىٓ أَمْرِنَا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

وَمَا كَانَ قَوْلَهُمْ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوا۟ - "And their statement was nothing except that they said..." Now these patient, steadfast believers are being described further. What did they say in the midst of battle?

رَبَّنَا ٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَإِسْرَافَنَا فِىٓ أَمْرِنَا - "Our Master, cover for us our sins and our transgressions in our matter." The word ذُنُوب refers to sins in general, while إِسْرَاف means to go beyond, to excess. They're asking: "Our Lord, You commanded us to fight. Maybe we oppressed the enemy. Maybe we went too far. Maybe we killed an innocent person by mistake. Maybe we became arrogant because of victory." They're covering all possibilities of where they might have erred.

وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا - "And plant our feet firmly." This is a request for steadfastness in the literal and metaphorical sense. Interestingly, Allah ﷻ makes reference to previous prophets and believers making this same supplication in the same context, back in Surah Al-Baqarah: رَبَّنَآ أَفْرِغْ عَلَيْنَا صَبْرًا وَثَبِّتْ أَقْدَامَنَا وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ.

وَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ - "And aid us against the disbelieving nation." This is their complete supplication on the battlefield.

translation: So Allah gave them the reward of this world and the good reward of the Hereafter. And Allah loves the doers of good
commentary:
فَـَٔاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ ثَوَابَ ٱلدُّنْيَا وَحُسْنَ ثَوَابِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ
