They're speaking to them as though mourning them: "Oh, if only you were here." The phrasing captures that false sense of remorse.

"And they sat still" - وَقَعَدُوا۟ from the root ق ع د, meaning to sit for a long time, to remain seated, to settle in comfortably. These people sat back, relaxed, and now all they do is talk. "If they had only followed us, they wouldn't have been killed."

But who are these dead they're referring to? Remember, three hundred walked off the battlefield initially. Were they the only weak ones? No, there were other weaklings too who felt the pressure. In a moment of fear, some got stuck behind enemy lines and ended up on top of the mountain. Some of these other hypocrites' friends died, and now the remaining hypocrites who stayed back are sitting around saying, "If they had only listened to us, they wouldn't have been killed."

Allah ﷻ instructs His Messenger ﷺ: "Say to them: 'Then push death away from yourselves!'" The word ٱدْرَءُوا۟  comes from دَرْء, which means to push someone forcefully out of harm's way - like when you see a truck coming and you shove someone out of danger. It's done to protect someone else, an urgent act of defense. Allah ﷻ tells them: Go ahead, push death away from yourself. Get rid of death entirely. You're so concerned that they wouldn't have died if they'd just stayed with you? How about you now? Save yourself from death. Go ahead, defend yourself from it - "if in fact you're truthful."

translation: And never think of those who have been killed in the cause of Allah as dead. Rather, they are alive with their Lord, receiving provision
commentary:
وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ قُتِلُوا۟ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ أَمْوَٰتًۢا ۚ بَلْ أَحْيَآءٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ يُرْزَقُونَ

"Don't you dare assume those who died in Allah's path to be dead." The instruction وَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّ is emphatic - don't assume, don't you dare assume, never let that thought cross your mind. This is a powerful prohibition about how we think of the martyrs.

Notice something crucial about the grammar here. In Surah Al-Baqarah, we read "And do not say about those who are killed in the path of Allah that they are dead" (2:154). There, the word أَمْوَاتًا (dead) was protected by the structure of the sentence, as if in quotation marks: "Don't say the word 'dead.'" It was about what we say.

But here in Surah Aal-E-Imran, أَمْوَٰتًۢا appears as the second object of the verb "to assume." The construction is: Don't assume the murdered to be dead. This isn't just about what you say - it's about what you think. Where do you experience more loss and more sense of death - before battle or after, especially when things have gone wrong? After, of course. In Surah Al-Baqarah, the pre-battle commentary said "Don't say it." Here, the post-battle instruction is far tougher: "Don't even think it."

Someone who doesn't say something might still be thinking it. This is the harder command. And unlike in Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ said "Rather, they are alive, but you do not perceive" and then moved on to another subject, here He doesn't stop. He gives a full account. In Al-Baqarah, we weren't told what to think - only what not to say, with the acknowledgment that we don't understand. Here, we're told don't even think it, and then Allah ﷻ teaches us exactly how we should be thinking.

"Rather, they are alive with their Lord, being provided for." The word يُرْزَقُونَ (being provided for) is particularly beautiful when you consider who died at Uhud. Take Mus'ab ibn Umayr (RA), for instance. He was a wealthy young man in Makkah - well-dressed, impressive, the kind of person who wore expensive imported colognes, someone everyone knew and admired. When he became Muslim, his mother disowned him completely. She told him that if he couldn't hold on to the religion of his ancestors, he couldn't keep anything from her - not even the clothes on his back. He literally left his house practically naked because of the condition she placed on him.

When he died in battle at Uhud - and the pain he endured before passing was immense as he was one of the flag bearers - there wasn't even enough cloth to cover his body. If they covered his head, his feet showed. If they covered his feet, his head showed. This was a man who, in one part of his life, could be considered a multimillionaire. Now he couldn't even afford his own burial shroud. Yet Allah ﷻ says about him and those like him: they are not just with their Master, but they are being provided for. He's not poor anymore - no, he's completely taken care of. يُرْزَقُونَ - he's hooked up, he's being provided for.

translation: Rejoicing in what Allah has bestowed upon them of His bounty, and they receive good tidings about those [to be martyred] after them who have not yet joined them - that there will be no fear concerning them, nor will they grieve
commentary:
فَرِحِينَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ وَيَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ أَلَّا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ

The word فَرِحِينَ is important to understand. In modern Arabic, it simply means overjoyed. In classical Arabic, it describes the kind of happiness that comes as a result of receiving a blessing. This joy can manifest in two ways: if your happiness from a blessing causes you to fail to give credit to Allah ﷻ, then that farah is not good. Allah ﷻ says "Indeed Allah does not love those who are overjoyed" (28:76) - referring to those who rejoice without recognizing the source. But when your joy is in recognition that it's coming from Allah ﷻ, when you actually acknowledge and realize it's from Him, then that farah is itself a blessing.

"They are overjoyed because of what Allah has given them from His bounty." The word فَضْل means what is above and beyond what you deserve. If you receive what you've earned, that's your due. Anything above that is fadl - extra favor, over-compensation. They are overjoyed at how Allah ﷻ has over-compensated them, giving them far more than they could have ever deserved or imagined.

"And they are congratulating each other because of those who haven't yet joined them." The verb يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ indicates mutual congratulation and rejoicing. But notice the next phrase carefully: بِٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَلْحَقُوا۟ بِهِم مِّنْ خَلْفِهِمْ (those who have not yet joined them from behind them).

The word يَلْحَقُوا۟ is significant. It comes from لَحِقَ, which means to join or catch up, but specifically when two similar things join together - like two bricks joining each other, or when a tree bears fruit that falls, and next season another fruit comes in the exact same spot, then falls, and another fruit comes. Each new fruit joins its predecessors. This is the imagery Allah ﷻ uses. The martyrs are described as fruit that has ripened and been received into Paradise. They're overjoyed by those who haven't yet joined them - the new fruit that will bear from coming generations and also attain martyrdom and enter Paradise.

There's more coming! Every time another martyr arrives, there's a celebration. It's as if they're wondering, "I wonder how many will come this year, how many will come next year?" They're overjoyed with every arrival.

Think of families waiting at an airport terminal late at night, holding posters and cameras, waiting for their loved ones to return from deployment. The reception, the joy, the anticipation in their faces - this is the kind of welcome, the kind of joy the martyrs are experiencing. And here's the thing: that's just family. But there's no stronger family than people who share "La ilaha illallah." The martyrs are waiting in anticipation just like people at the airport eager to meet their loved ones. This is the scene Allah ﷻ is describing with يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ - they're celebrating those still behind them, those who have a long journey ahead but who will eventually join them.

"That there will be no fear upon them, nor will they grieve." Right before they tasted shahada, they were in a situation of fear and grief. The moment their martyrdom happened, the instant Allah ﷻ took their lives at Uhud, immediately fear and grief vanished. Angels come congratulating them. In some cases, even the Messenger ﷺ has to look away because their spouses from Paradise are coming to greet them - and that's not for him to look at. 

This is the joy that the people of Paradise are feeling even now, in this very moment. This is what you should think about those who died in the path of Allah ﷻ. This is what you should think about those who were killed in the way of Allah ﷻ. The last thing you should ever think about them is that they are dead. Allah ﷻ completely rearranges the way we think about martyrdom, transforming our understanding of what it means to give one's life in His path.

translation: They receive good tidings of favor from Allah and bounty and [of the fact] that Allah does not allow the reward of believers to be lost
commentary:
۞ يَسْتَبْشِرُونَ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

The ayahs describing the joy of those in Paradise continue. These believers are congratulating themselves, feeling deep contentment because of the نعمة - the favor of Allah ﷻ. This favor can be understood as Islam itself, which they enjoyed in this world. The additional فضل mentioned could be argued to be something above and beyond the basic favor.

Understanding the distinction between two related Arabic terms enriches our comprehension here. نعمة and نعماء are closely connected but distinct. Na'ma refers specifically to means of ease and comfort in life - the things that make life comfortable. Ni'mah is more comprehensive, encompassing all kinds of blessings and gifts that are opened to someone.

There are different scholarly interpretations of what constitutes the ni'mah and fadl in this context. The favor from Allah ﷻ could be Islam in this world, with the additional favor being entry into Paradise. Alternatively, some scholars argue that the favor is martyrdom itself, and the additional favor (fadl) is immediate entry into Paradise, unlike the typical deceased who must wait.

The ayah concludes with a profound reassurance: وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - "and that Allah ﷻ does not waste the compensation of the believers." This ending is particularly meaningful because the ayah begins by mentioning something extra, something above and beyond, yet it concludes with the most basic expectation - compensation. The word أجر is used in Arabic even for ordinary wages, like when you work for someone and receive a paycheck.

What Allah ﷻ is communicating here is powerful: the believers are enjoying the highest bonuses from Him, yet simultaneously, He will never waste anything less than that either. The verb يُضِيعُ means to waste completely, for any reason whatsoever. Allah ﷻ is saying there will be no reason, no justification, no circumstance under which the rewards of believers will be forsaken.

translation: Those [believers] who responded to Allah and the Messenger after injury had struck them. For those who did good among them and feared Allah is a great reward
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْقَرْحُ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ

This ayah refers to a specific historical incident. After the Battle of Uhud, Abu Sufyan and the Quraysh army had retreated. The Companions (RA) were injured, battered, and demoralized. Yet at this critical moment, two things happened simultaneously: Abu Sufyan began considering whether to return and attack the Muslims a second time, and the Messenger of Allah ﷺ received an intuition that the Quraysh might not be finished with them yet. Perhaps the enemy wanted the Muslims to relax and lower their guard before launching a surprise attack.

In this state - with his Companions wounded and exhausted - the Prophet ﷺ gave an extraordinary command: "Get up. Let's go after them." The purpose was to demonstrate that the Muslims were not deterred, that they remained ready for battle. This would send a clear message and potentially scare off any plans for a counterattack.

Consider the psychological state of these Companions (RA). They were physically injured and emotionally at a low point. The last scene of this battle had been them retreating up a mountain. Yet when their Messenger ﷺ called, they responded. Intelligence came through a Muslim spy that Abu Sufyan was indeed considering a return. Meanwhile, one of the enemy's spies reported back to Abu Sufyan that the Muslims were actually coming after him, which frightened him into abandoning his plans and retreating further.

Some scholars connect this ayah to another incident. At the end of Uhud, Abu Sufyan had declared, "We'll see you at Badr next year!" It's narrated that the following year, the Messenger ﷺ prepared the army and marched to Badr for this appointed confrontation. Abu Sufyan did not show up, but the believers' resolve was tested regardless.

Allah ﷻ says: ٱلَّذِينَ ٱسْتَجَابُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱلرَّسُولِ - "Those who responded to Allah and the Messenger." The conjunction here teaches us something profound: responding to Allah ﷻ practically manifests as responding to what the Messenger ﷺ asks you to do. You aren't merely responding to a human leader; you are responding to Allah ﷻ by responding to His Messenger ﷺ.

مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَآ أَصَابَهُمُ ٱلْقَرْحُ - "even after injury had touched them." The word قَرْح refers to a wound that causes swelling and pain but is less severe than جُرْح, which penetrates the skin and bleeds. Allah ﷻ is essentially minimizing their injuries, as if to say, "It's still not a big deal. You can handle this."

It's like when a child falls off a chair and looks at the parent before deciding whether to cry. If the parent shows concern and rushes over, the child perceives the fall as serious and begins to cry. But if the parent calmly says, "You're okay, it happens," the child toughens up and continues playing. Allah ﷻ is toughening up His believers here: "Yes, you got injured, but you responded to the Messenger ﷺ. You can handle this."

لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ وَٱتَّقَوْا۟ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ - "For those who excelled among them and had taqwa, there is a great compensation." Notice the sequence: Allah ﷻ mentions إحسان (excellence) before تقوى, even though typically we think of taqwa as the foundation that leads to ihsan. What's the wisdom in this reversal?

When you show the highest degree of resolution and loyalty to the Messenger ﷺ in such difficult circumstances - saying "Yes, sir. Ready to go" despite your injuries - this is a manifestation of ihsan. This is your excellence being demonstrated. Then, when you go out to face the enemy and confront legitimate reasons to be afraid, you remind yourself that the only One to truly fear is Allah ﷻ. At that moment, your taqwa comes to life. First comes the action of ihsan, then comes the consciousness of taqwa.

translation: Those to whom hypocrites said, "Indeed, the people have gathered against you, so fear them." But it [merely] increased them in faith, and they said, "Sufficient for us is Allah, and [He is] the best Disposer of affairs
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالَ لَهُمُ ٱلنَّاسُ إِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱخْشَوْهُمْ فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ

News began spreading - some of it from hypocrites and others who said to the believers: إِنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ قَدْ جَمَعُوا۟ لَكُمْ فَٱخْشَوْهُمْ - "Indeed, the people have gathered against you, so fear them." They were told, "You think this is over? It's just beginning. They've already assembled against you. Be afraid."

This ayah contains universal lessons that transcend its historical context. How many Muslims today are obsessed with what various groups and powers are plotting against them? What governments are doing, what organizations are planning, what media outlets are saying, what books are being written. People can spend their entire lives consumed with talk about who's doing what and how "they" are out to get us.

These conspiracy discussions are entertaining. It's captivating to sit and analyze who's plotting what. But Allah ﷻ is teaching us a different response entirely.

What did these believers of ihsan do when they heard such warnings? فَزَادَهُمْ إِيمَـٰنًا - "It increased them in faith." Why would warnings of enemy plots increase their faith? Because they had heard the Messenger of Allah ﷺ tell them that trials would come upon them like waves crashing against a meal. When they saw this prophecy materializing, they thought, "The Messenger ﷺ was speaking the truth! This is another confirmation that he really is the Messenger of Allah ﷻ." Every fulfilled warning became evidence strengthening their certainty.

وَقَالُوا۟ حَسْبُنَا ٱللَّهُ وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ - "And they said: 'Allah is sufficient for us, and He is the best Disposer of affairs.'" After reaffirming their faith, what did they do? They declared that Allah ﷻ alone is enough. حَسْبُنَا means "sufficient for us" - Allah ﷻ Himself is enough. وَنِعْمَ ٱلْوَكِيلُ - "and the best Wakeel." The Wakeel is the One who takes care of your affairs, the One who manages your matters when you leave them entirely to Him.

translation: So they returned with favor from Allah and bounty, no harm having touched them. And they pursued the pleasure of Allah, and Allah is the possessor of great bounty
commentary:
فَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ لَّمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوٓءٌ وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ

فَٱنقَلَبُوا۟ - "Then they returned." They marched out after the enemy, but the enemy had already fled. So they turned back. بِنِعْمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَفَضْلٍ - "with favor from Allah and additional favor." Allah ﷻ rewarded them for showing that courage and zeal, but additionally favored them as though they had actually fought in the path of Allah ﷻ. He gave them the reward for the battle they were prepared to fight.

لَّمْ يَمْسَسْهُمْ سُوٓءٌ - "No harm even touched them." Some scholars note that this phrase itself describes the additional favor (fadl): not only were they rewarded, but they were also protected from any evil befalling them.

وَٱتَّبَعُوا۟ رِضْوَٰنَ ٱللَّهِ - "And they pursued the pleasure of Allah." This explains why no harm came to them. They weren't pursuing victory or revenge; they were pursuing the extreme pleasure of Allah ﷻ. When your intention is purely for His pleasure, He protects you in ways you cannot imagine.

وَٱللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ - "And Allah is the Possessor of great favor." Think about how worldly compensation works. You might earn a $50,000 salary and receive a $5,000 bonus. The bonus is much smaller than the salary, but you're still grateful for it. Allah ﷻ calls Himself the Possessor of فَضْلٍ عَظِيمٍ - tremendous favor. The bonus itself is enormous, not just what you earned. The additional gifts above and beyond what was promised are themselves massive.

Consider the proportions scattered throughout our religion: for every letter of Quran recited, ten good deeds; prayer in Masjid al-Haram multiplied twenty-seven times; and countless other multiplications. This is أجر - compensation that can at least be counted. But on top of all that comes فضل - favor that cannot even be counted. It's beyond human calculation.

translation: That is only Satan who frightens [you] of his supporters. So fear them not, but fear Me, if you are [indeed] believers
commentary:
إِنَّمَا ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥ فَلَا تَخَافُوهُمْ وَخَافُونِ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ

Some believers were nervous about going out to confront the enemy. They fought their fear internally and eventually came out. Allah ﷻ addresses those who stayed behind or were initially reluctant: إِنَّمَا ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ - "That was only the Shaytan." That internal struggle you experienced, that voice telling you to be afraid - it was nothing but Shaytan.

يُخَوِّفُ أَوْلِيَآءَهُۥ - This phrase can be understood in two ways. First: "He instills fear of his protective friends" - meaning Shaytan wants you to be afraid of the disbelievers who are his allies. Second: "He only successfully instills fear into his own friends" - meaning if you truly became afraid and stayed behind, that would prove you are not the friend of Allah ﷻ but rather the friend of Shaytan, because he can only successfully frighten his own people.

فَلَا تَخَافُوهُمْ وَخَافُونِ إِن كُنتُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ - "So do not fear them, but fear Me, if you are truly believers." The command is absolute and unambiguous. Fear is to be directed solely toward Allah ﷻ. If you genuinely possess iman, this is where your fear must lie.

translation: And do not be grieved, [O Muhammad], by those who hasten into disbelief. Indeed, they will never harm Allah at all. Allah intends that He should give them no share in the Hereafter, and for them is a great punishment
commentary:
وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا ۗ يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَلَّا يَجْعَلَ لَهُمْ حَظًّا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ

وَلَا يَحْزُنكَ ٱلَّذِينَ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْكُفْرِ - "And do not let those who rush into disbelief grieve you." These aren't passive disbelievers sitting idle in their rejection. They're actively engaged in projects, racing and rushing to pursue the cause of disbelief, working to annihilate Islam and the Muslims.

Allah ﷻ tells His Messenger ﷺ - and through him, all believers - don't let this worry you. Don't let it make you sad.

إِنَّهُمْ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا - "Indeed, they will never harm Allah at all." This is a profound reframing. Allah ﷻ is belittling their efforts and reminding us of a crucial reality: efforts made against Islam are no different than efforts made against Allah ﷻ Himself. Attempts to harm Islam, to attack the truth, to undermine the Messenger ﷺ, the Book, the teachings, the religion - all of these are equivalent to efforts made against Allah ﷻ Himself.

Once you realize this, you understand: how can anyone harm Allah ﷻ? Someone might think they're going to harm Islam or harm the truth. But when they remind themselves that they're actually in battle against Allah ﷻ Himself, they're no longer worried. They learn to relax.

This perspective doesn't mean we become passive or indifferent to attacks on our religion. Rather, it means we don't become consumed by anxiety and fear. We can speak out against falsehood, we can respond with truth, but we do so from a position of confidence in Allah's protection, not from panic and desperation.

يُرِيدُ ٱللَّهُ أَلَّا يَجْعَلَ لَهُمْ حَظًّا فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ - "Allah intends that He should not grant them any portion in the Hereafter." The word حَظ refers to a portion or share of good fortune. It's used for inheritance, for an unexpected windfall, for good luck that comes your way. Allah ﷻ is stating that He intends for these active workers of disbelief to have no good portion whatsoever in the afterlife. He allows them to engage in their worldly projects because doing so guarantees there's absolutely no good left for them in the next life.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ عَظِيمٌ - "And for them is a tremendous punishment." This echoes the lesson taught earlier in the surah about how Allah ﷻ extends the rope for the wrongdoers, allowing them to increase in their transgression, which only increases their ultimate punishment.

translation: Indeed, those who purchase disbelief [in exchange] for faith - never will they harm Allah at all, and for them is a painful punishment
commentary:
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

This ayah continues the themes of the previous verses. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ ٱلْكُفْرَ بِٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ - "Indeed, those who purchased disbelief in exchange for faith." This doesn't refer only to the external disbelievers actively working against Islam. It includes the hypocrites, those who walked away from faith, those who entered Islam and then left it, and those who pretended to embrace Islam only to publicly renounce it later.

The verb ٱشْتَرَوُا۟ - "purchased" - is striking. They made a transaction, an exchange. They had faith, or the opportunity for faith, and they traded it for disbelief. They chose disbelief as though it were merchandise they preferred over the priceless treasure of iman.

لَن يَضُرُّوا۟ ٱللَّهَ شَيْـًٔا - "They will never harm Allah at all." Once again, Allah ﷻ emphasizes the futility of their efforts. Regardless of their writings, their speeches, their "Why I Left Islam" narratives, their attempts to cast doubt - none of it will harm Allah ﷻ or His religion in any lasting way.

The Quran is protected by Allah ﷻ, not by us. We will come and go, but this Book will remain because Allah ﷻ Himself guards it. If we fail to carry it, teach it, or protect it adequately, Allah ﷻ will raise others far more qualified to memorize, preserve, and teach His Book. The Book will endure. We're merely here to serve; we're not actually accomplishing the preservation on our own.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ - "And for them is a painful punishment." The ayah concludes with the certainty of their recompense. While they may cause temporary confusion or lead some astray in this world, their ultimate destination is a punishment described as أَلِيمٌ - intensely painful, afflicting both body and soul.

translation: And let not those who disbelieve ever think that [because] We extend their time [of enjoyment] it is better for them. We only extend it for them so that they may increase in sin, and for them is a humiliating punishment
commentary:
وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّمَا نُمْلِى لَهُمْ خَيْرٌ لِّأَنفُسِهِمْ ۚ إِنَّمَا نُمْلِى لَهُمْ لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِثْمًا ۚ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ

Those who have engaged in disbelief should not dare assume that the extensions We give them are better for them. Understanding this ayah requires examining the concept of إملاء, which means to extend or grant a prolonged period. The root carries the meaning of vast stretches, like an extensively long desert as far as the eye can see. When applied to time, it refers to giving someone a long life or an extended era to pass.

Allah ﷻ warns the disbelievers not to misunderstand His wisdom. These criminals deserve punishment, yet they continue operating freely in the world. They have been given an extension, a respite before Judgment Day. But this extension should never be interpreted as divine approval or blessing. The ayah states clearly: We only extend their time لِيَزْدَادُوٓا۟ إِثْمًا - so they themselves may increase in sin.

The choice of يَزْدَادُوٓا۟ is significant. This verb form indicates that they themselves increase, becoming worse and worse as persons. It describes a transformation within their character, not merely an accumulation of sinful deeds. If the ayah wanted to say they committed more sins, it would use different phrasing. Instead, it emphasizes that they become more sinful in their very essence.

The ayah concludes with a warning: وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (and for them there is humiliating punishment). The word مُّهِينٌ describes a specific type of humiliation - one that happens publicly, a disgrace witnessed by all. This stands as one of the central lessons of this surah, appearing in various forms throughout its passages.

translation: Allah would not leave the believers in that [state] you are in [presently] until He separates the evil from the good. Nor would Allah reveal to you the unseen. But [instead], Allah chooses of His messengers whom He wills, so believe in Allah and His messengers. And if you believe and fear Him, then for you is a great reward
commentary:
مَّا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيَذَرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ عَلَىٰ مَآ أَنتُمْ عَلَيْهِ حَتَّىٰ يَمِيزَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ ۗ وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْتَبِى مِن رُّسُلِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ ۚ وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ

This ayah addresses a fundamental truth about Allah's relationship with the believers. The phrase مَّا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ - "it is not becoming of Allah" - establishes that certain actions are beneath His wisdom and majesty. Here, Allah ﷻ declares it is not becoming of Him لِيَذَرَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - to abandon the believers or leave them be.

The word يَذَرَ means to leave someone alone, to let them be without intervention. Allah ﷻ promises He will never simply leave believers to their own devices, allowing them to remain in whatever state they happen to be in. Notice the remarkable shift in pronouns: "Allah will not leave the believers in the state that you are in" - switching from third person (the believers) to second person (you). This transition reveals a profound truth: not everyone who appears among the believers is a true believer. The community contains both genuine faith and hidden hypocrisy, and Allah ﷻ will not leave this mixture unchecked.

The purpose is clear: حَتَّىٰ يَمِيزَ ٱلْخَبِيثَ مِنَ ٱلطَّيِّبِ (until He distinguishes the filthy from the good). The word يَمِيزَ describes a specific type of separation - like removing bones from meat or picking pebbles from rice. You separate what is useless from what is valuable. The term ٱلْخَبِيثَ refers to filth that develops on something originally pure, like rust forming on iron. The metal underneath is good, but a layer of corruption has accumulated on its surface. Similarly, most Muslims enter Islam for sincere reasons, but some allow their iman to deteriorate into hypocrisy. Allah ﷻ will cleanse the community by distinguishing the corrupt from the pure.

The ayah then addresses a natural question: Why doesn't Allah ﷻ simply inform us about everything beforehand? Why allow trials and tests when He could just reveal who the hypocrites are? The response comes: وَمَا كَانَ ٱللَّهُ لِيُطْلِعَكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْغَيْبِ (it is not becoming of Allah to inform you about the unseen). The word يُطْلِعَ means to make someone aware of a situation. Allah ﷻ operates on a need-to-know basis, and that information flows through the proper channel: His messengers.

وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَجْتَبِى مِن رُّسُلِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ (However, Allah does choose from among His messengers whoever He wills). This establishes the institution of prophethood as the authorized channel for receiving guidance about the unseen. The believers are then commanded: فَـَٔامِنُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ وَرُسُلِهِۦ (believe in Allah and His messengers). This relationship - receiving knowledge through the messenger rather than directly - is not new but represents the eternal protocol of revelation.

The ayah concludes with encouragement: وَإِن تُؤْمِنُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَلَكُمْ أَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (If you truly believe and maintain taqwa, then you will have great compensation). The path forward requires both genuine faith and consciousness of Allah ﷻ.

translation: And let not those who [greedily] withhold what Allah has given them of His bounty ever think that it is better for them. Rather, it is worse for them. Their necks will be encircled by what they withheld on the Day of Resurrection. And to Allah belongs the heritage of the heavens and the earth. And Allah, with what you do, is [fully] Acquainted
commentary:
وَلَا يَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَبْخَلُونَ بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ هُوَ خَيْرًا لَّهُم ۖ بَلْ هُوَ شَرٌّ لَّهُمْ ۖ سَيُطَوَّقُونَ مَا بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۗ وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ

This ayah addresses those who are يَبْخَلُونَ - miserly and cheap. A bakheel is someone who refuses to spend money where it deserves to be spent, even for their own legitimate needs. In the context of Allah's path, it describes those who have the ability to spend in service of the deen but choose to hold back, clinging to their wealth.

The warning begins: Let not those who are cheap بِمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ (with what Allah has given them from His additional favor) assume that saving this wealth is better for them. The emphasis on "additional favor" reminds us that wealth doesn't come purely through our own effort - it arrives as divine blessing. They didn't fully earn it; it came to them as fadl from Allah ﷻ.

Allah ﷻ corrects their assumption directly: بَلْ هُوَ شَرٌّ لَّهُمْ (Rather, it is worse for them. It is evil for them). Then comes a chilling description of their fate: سَيُطَوَّقُونَ مَا بَخِلُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ (They will be collared with what they were stingy with on the Day of Resurrection). The word يُطَوَّقُونَ comes from tawq, meaning to put something around the neck like a collar or necklace. The Arabs would say a dog remains a dog even if you put a gold necklace around its neck. But tawq also refers to anything that constricts the neck - including a noose used for hanging.

The imagery is powerful: they will be choked or hung by the very wealth they hoarded. Some scholars explain this as the wealth transforming into a serpent that wraps around the miser's neck, choking them. The thing they clung to in this life becomes their torment in the next.

The ayah then provides profound perspective on ownership: وَلِلَّهِ مِيرَٰثُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (To Allah alone belongs the inheritance of the heavens and the earth). Miraath means inherited assets. Everything we possess will eventually pass to someone else. The shirt you wear, the car you drive, the house you live in - all of it will transfer to others. Whatever patch of land you call "my property" has passed through countless hands before you and will continue passing through hands after you're gone. We are merely temporary custodians. In the end, everything returns to its true Owner.

This realization should transform how we view our possessions. Nothing is truly "mine" in any permanent sense. Hoarding wealth becomes absurd when you understand it will all return to Allah ﷻ anyway. The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ خَبِيرٌ (Allah is fully aware of what you do). He has complete knowledge, especially regarding how we handle the resources He has entrusted to us.

translation: Allah has certainly heard the statement of those [Jews] who said, "Indeed, Allah is poor, while we are rich." We will record what they said and their killing of the prophets without right and will say, "Taste the punishment of the Burning Fire
commentary:
لَّقَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ قَوْلَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَقِيرٌ وَنَحْنُ أَغْنِيَآءُ ۘ سَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَالُوا۟ وَقَتْلَهُمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَنَقُولُ ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ

This ayah exposes an outrageous statement made by some among the Jews. لَّقَدْ سَمِعَ ٱللَّهُ (Certainly Allah has heard) - there is no doubt about it. He heard the speech of those who declared: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ فَقِيرٌ وَنَحْنُ أَغْنِيَآءُ (Indeed, Allah is poor and we are wealthy).

How did such blasphemy arise? When the Quran encouraged spending in Allah's path and asked مَّن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يُقْرِضُ ٱللَّهَ قَرْضًا حَسَنًۭا "Who will give Allah a beautiful loan?" (64:17, 57:11), some responded with mockery: Why does Allah need our money? He must be bankrupt if He's asking us for financial help. We are the wealthy ones - He should be giving to us, not the other way around. This represents a catastrophic misunderstanding of the metaphor, twisted into an insult against Allah ﷻ Himself.

The response is swift and severe: سَنَكْتُبُ مَا قَالُوا۟ (We will document what they have said). Allah ﷻ will record their words alongside another crime: وَقَتْلَهُمُ ٱلْأَنۢبِيَآءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ (their killing of prophets without any justification). Both crimes - blasphemy against Allah ﷻ and violence against His messengers - will be preserved in their record.

Then comes the punishment, and notice the remarkable shift in how it is announced. Normally, when describing the fate of disbelievers, Allah ﷻ maintains distance by using passive voice: "It will be said to them, 'Enter the Fire.'" But here, because they insulted Allah ﷻ directly, He makes it personal: وَنَقُولُ (And We will say) ذُوقُوا۟ عَذَابَ ٱلْحَرِيقِ (Taste the punishment of the burning fire).

They were going to taste punishment regardless, but when Allah ﷻ Himself commands them to taste it, an additional dimension of torment is added. It becomes something unprecedented - a direct statement from the One they mocked. Al-Hariq describes fire that completely consumes whatever it touches, leaving nothing behind. Yet these individuals will be thrown into this all-consuming fire and will not be consumed. Allah ﷻ will not allow them the mercy of being destroyed by it. They will burn continuously in flames designed to eliminate everything, yet they will remain.

translation: That is for what your hands have put forth and because Allah is not ever unjust to [His] servants
commentary:
ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ

This ayah provides the justification for the punishment described previously. ذَٰلِكَ (That) - referring to the burning punishment - comes بِمَا قَدَّمَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ (because of what your hands have sent forward). The imagery of hands sending things forward suggests investment and preparation. Just as a person invests in their future in this world, these individuals invested in their punishment. Their hands dispatched their deeds ahead of them, and now they meet the consequences.

The ayah concludes with a critical statement about divine justice: وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ لِّلْعَبِيدِ (and that Allah is not unjust to His slaves). The term عَبِيد originally meant physical slaves but evolved into a religious term. The Quran uses it to refer to all creation, all human beings. Everyone is abd (slave/servant) to Allah ﷻ by virtue of being His creation. Some graduate from this baseline servitude to become conscious, devoted servants (عِبَاد), but all remain fundamentally His abeed.

The phrase لَيْسَ بِظَلَّامٍ - "not a wrongdoer" - uses an intensive form. Allah ﷻ is not merely free from injustice; He is completely removed from any possibility of wronging anyone. Whatever punishment comes is entirely deserved, completely justified, and perfectly measured. No one can claim they received more than what their actions warranted.

translation: [They are] those who said, "Indeed, Allah has taken our promise not to believe any messenger until he brings us an offering which fire [from heaven] will consume." Say, "There have already come to you messengers before me with clear proofs and [even] that of which you speak. So why did you kill them, if you should be truthful
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنَآ أَلَّا نُؤْمِنَ لِرَسُولٍ حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَنَا بِقُرْبَانٍ تَأْكُلُهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۗ قُلْ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِى بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَبِٱلَّذِى قُلْتُمْ فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

Throughout Surah Aal-E-Imran, various objections raised by the People of the Book have been addressed. This ayah presents another rationale used by some Jews for rejecting the message of Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. They claimed: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَهِدَ إِلَيْنَآ (Allah has taken a covenant from us) that أَلَّا نُؤْمِنَ لِرَسُولٍ (we should not believe in any messenger) until he meets a specific condition.

That condition was: حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَنَا بِقُرْبَانٍ تَأْكُلُهُ ٱلنَّارُ (until he brings us a sacrifice consumed by fire). The word قُرْبَان comes from قُرْب, meaning closeness. A qurban is a sacrificial animal offered to draw near to Allah ﷻ. Previous prophets were often given visible, physical miracles - staff turning into serpent, water parting, dead returning to life, clay bird taking flight. Among these signs was a particular miracle: when someone offered an animal sacrifice, if it was acceptable to Allah ﷻ, fire would descend from the heavens and consume it completely. This served as divine confirmation that both the sacrifice and the person offering it were accepted.

The Jews argued they had been conditioned to expect this specific sign. They claimed they couldn't accept any prophet unless he demonstrated this miracle. The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to respond: قُلْ قَدْ جَآءَكُمْ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِى (Say: Messengers came to you long before me) بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ (with clear proofs) وَبِٱلَّذِى قُلْتُمْ (and with exactly what you are asking for).

Your own history records prophets who came with this very miracle you claim to require. So the devastating question follows: فَلِمَ قَتَلْتُمُوهُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (Then why did you kill them if you are truthful?). If you truly were seeking truth, if you genuinely wanted this sign as confirmation, why did your forefathers murder the very prophets who brought it? The objection is exposed as a mere excuse. They don't reject the message because the sign is absent; they reject it despite the sign being present. Their history of prophet-killing reveals that no miracle would ever be sufficient for those determined to disbelieve.

translation: Then if they deny you, [O Muhammad] - so were messengers denied before you, who brought clear proofs and written ordinances and the enlightening Scripture
commentary:
فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ فَقَدْ كُذِّبَ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ جَآءُو بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ وَٱلزُّبُرِ وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُنِيرِ

After dismantling the objections and exposing the insincerity of the rejectors, Allah ﷻ turns His attention back to His Messenger ﷺ with words of consolation. فَإِن كَذَّبُوكَ (If they call you a liar, if they deliberately lie against you) - the verb كَذَّبَ is intensive, meaning they didn't just disbelieve but actively worked to discredit and defame you.

The response provides historical perspective: فَقَدْ كُذِّبَ رُسُلٌ مِّن قَبْلِكَ (then messengers were certainly called liars long before you). This is nothing new. You walk a path worn by every messenger before you. Rejection and slander are not signs of your failure but markers of authentic prophethood.

These previous messengers جَآءُو بِٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتِ (came with the clearest proofs). They arrived with overwhelming evidence, yet were still rejected. They came وَٱلزُّبُرِ (with the Scriptures) - the word زُبُر refers to written revelations, including the Psalms given to Dawud (AS). They came وَٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُنِيرِ (with the enlightening Book) - books that illuminate everything around them, that bring light to darkness and clarity to confusion.

Despite bringing clear evidence, written Scripture, and illuminating revelation, these messengers were still branded as liars. The message to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is clear: their rejection of you says nothing about the truth you carry and everything about the state of their hearts. You stand in noble company. Every bearer of divine truth has faced the same resistance, the same slander, the same denial. Let this history strengthen your resolve rather than shake your confidence.

translation: Every soul will taste death, and you will only be given your [full] compensation on the Day of Resurrection. So he who is drawn away from the Fire and admitted to Paradise has attained [his desire]. And what is the life of this world except the enjoyment of delusion
commentary:
كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَإِنَّمَا تُوَفَّوْنَ أُجُورَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۖ فَمَن زُحْزِحَ عَنِ ٱلنَّارِ وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ فَقَدْ فَازَ ۗ وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ

**Every Soul is a Taster of Death**

The ayah begins with a profound grammatical choice: كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ (Every soul is a taster of death). Most translations say "every person *will* taste death," but the Arabic uses the present tense active participle ذَآئِقَةُ. This form doesn't just describe a future action—it describes what something *is*. The feminine form is used because نَفْسٍ (soul) is grammatically feminine in Arabic.

This grammatical structure carries significant meaning. When you say "I am standing" versus "he is a scholar," the difference is that one describes a temporary action while the other describes a permanent state. Allah ﷻ is saying that every single individual *is already* tasting death. Every breath we take is a small taste. Every night when we sleep is a minor experience of death. The present tense here also functions as an emphatic future—a guarantee and a verdict. It's like telling your child, "You *are* going to school tomorrow," not "you *will* go." The present tense emphasizes that this is final, non-negotiable. Every person is tasting death—this is the permanent reality of our existence.

**Full Recompense on the Day of Judgment**

وَإِنَّمَا تُوَفَّوْنَ أُجُورَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ—And you will only truly be given your full compensation on the Day of Resurrection. While we receive some consequences for our good and bad deeds in this life, the complete recompense only comes on the Day of Judgment. We taste partial results here, but the full taste comes in the Hereafter.

**Being Moved Far From the Fire**

فَمَن زُحْزِحَ عَنِ ٱلنَّارِ—So whoever has been pushed a long distance away from the Fire. The word زُحْزِحَ comes from a root meaning to remove someone and place them at a long distance. Allah ﷻ doesn't simply say "removed from the Fire"—He says moved *far, far away* from it. This teaches us about the intensity of that Fire. You don't just want to escape it—you want to be nowhere near it, not even within long range of it. The passive voice here is significant: this person doesn't move themselves away through their own power; rather, they are moved by Allah's mercy.

We see this concept elsewhere in the Quran. In Surah Al-Anbiya, we learn of someone who hasn't even entered the Fire yet but is crying just from being outside it. The distance from Hellfire is itself a mercy.

**Entering Paradise Through Allah's Grace**

وَأُدْخِلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ—And has been entered into Paradise. Notice the grammatical construction: أُدْخِلَ is passive voice. If Allah ﷻ had said دَخَلَ ٱلْجَنَّةَ, it would mean "he entered Paradise" on his own merit. But أُدْخِلَ means *Allah entered him* into Paradise. This person doesn't walk into Paradise claiming credit for themselves. Rather, Allah ﷻ places them inside it. It's entirely by His grace and permission.

**True Success**

فَقَدْ فَازَ—Then he has succeeded. The word فَازَ deserves special attention. This is the ultimate success, but what does it really mean? Success in Arabic farming terminology means being saved from catastrophic failure *and then* experiencing prosperity. It's the combination of rescue from disaster with the arrival of blessing. There couldn't be anything worse than Hellfire, and there couldn't be anything better than Paradise. This person has been saved from the absolute worst and granted the absolute best. That is فَوْز—true, complete success.

**Worldly Life: Means of Deception**

وَمَا ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَآ إِلَّا مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ—And what is the worldly life except means of deception. The word دُنْيَا means "the closer life," the inferior life, the lowest life. It's described as مَتَاعُ ٱلْغُرُورِ —utilities and means by which people become deluded.

Understanding غُرُور is essential. This word appears earlier in the Quran when Allah ﷻ says that the hypocrites try to deceive Allah: يُخَادِعُونَ ٱللَّهَ. The root غ-ر-ر describes being taken advantage of when you're not paying attention. It's the kind of deception that happens when your guard is down—like street games where someone moves cups around and you lose track, or card games where someone cheats while you're distracted.

Allah ﷻ is saying that worldly life deceives you because you're غَافِل —heedless, not truly paying attention. If you were really sharp and alert, it couldn't fool you. But when you become immersed in it, distracted by it, thinking that worldly success itself is the ultimate goal—that's when you fall for the deception. The dunya can only deceive those who aren't vigilant. May Allah ﷻ protect us from being among the heedless.

translation: You will surely be tested in your possessions and in yourselves. And you will surely hear from those who were given the Scripture before you and from those who associate others with Allah much abuse. But if you are patient and fear Allah - indeed, that is of the matters [worthy] of determination
commentary:
۞ لَتُبْلَوُنَّ فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ وَلَتَسْمَعُنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَذًى كَثِيرًا ۚ وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ

**The Guarantee of Testing**

After guaranteeing death in the previous ayah, Allah ﷻ now gives a second guarantee: لَتُبْلَوُنَّ—you will *definitely, absolutely, without any doubt* be tested. The structure of this verb is remarkable. From the root ب-ل-و comes يَبْلُو, then the heavier form تُبْلَوْنَ, then the even heavier تُبَلَّوْنَ, and finally the most emphatic لَتُبْلَوُنَّ. This is the strongest possible way to express inevitability in Arabic.

The present tense form implies something continuous and repeated—over and over and over again, you will go through exhausting tests. There is no escape from this reality.

**Tests in Wealth and Lives**

فِىٓ أَمْوَٰلِكُمْ وَأَنفُسِكُمْ—In your wealth and in your own selves. This echoes an earlier passage in Surah Al-Baqarah where Allah ﷻ says: وَلَنَبْلُوَنَّكُم بِشَىْءٍ مِّنَ ٱلْخَوْفِ وَٱلْجُوعِ وَنَقْصٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْوَٰلِ وَٱلْأَنفُسِ وَٱلثَّمَرَٰتِ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ—"And We will surely test you with something of fear and hunger and a loss of wealth and lives and fruits, but give good tidings to the patient" (2:155). Now that theme returns, reminding us that tests in our resources and our very lives are inescapable parts of the believer's journey.

**Hearing Hurtful Words**

وَلَتَسْمَعُنَّ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوٓا۟ أَذًى كَثِيرًا—And you will definitely hear from those who were given the Scripture before you and from those who associate partners with Allah much harm. Again, we see the emphatic لَتَسْمَعُنَّ—you *will* hear, without question, repeatedly.

The word أَذًى is significant. In Arabic, there are different words for pain. أَذَى refers to minor pain—not life-threatening injuries, but irritations and hurts that sting emotionally and spiritually as well as physically. It can mean hurtful words, insults, slander, mockery. The word is used elsewhere in the Quran for the time of menstruation for women because it involves minor physical discomfort alongside the spiritual pain of being unable to pray.

Allah ﷻ says أَذًى كَثِيرًا—*much* harm, lots and lots of hurtful words. But the choice of أَذَى rather than أَلَم (severe pain) also contains a mercy: these words will hurt, but they won't destroy you. You'll get over it. You'll be fine.

This is still happening today. Muslims continue to hear offensive, hurtful speech from People of the Book and from those who associate partners with Allah ﷻ. The tests haven't changed.

**The Response: Patience and Taqwa**

وَإِن تَصْبِرُوا۟ وَتَتَّقُوا۟—And if you are patient and maintain taqwa. This is the divine response to all the provocations and pain. Not retaliation. Not erupting emotionally. Not getting rattled and reactive. Simply: صَبْر and تَقْوَىٰ.

Patience here means intellectual and emotional composure. It means not letting hurtful words cause you to explode or abandon your principles. Taqwa means maintaining consciousness of Allah ﷻ and staying within the boundaries He has set, even when provoked.

The lessons of tolerance from the Prophet ﷺ are profound here. One of the most offensive statements to the Islamic creed is the claim that Allah ﷻ has taken a son. Yet when a delegation of Christians came to Madinah and the Prophet ﷺ allowed them to pray in his masjid according to their own manner of worship—even though that worship contained elements of shirk—this demonstrated an extraordinary level of courtesy and restraint. He didn't demand they change their prayer. He showed them respect and hospitality despite fundamental theological differences.

This is so far from how many of us think today. We've become intolerant not just toward non-Muslims, but even toward fellow Muslims who practice differently. There are countless stories of non-Muslims being treated harshly when they visit our masajid out of genuine curiosity. Someone comes, tries to bow down in the only way they know how, and gets dragged out and yelled at. What kind of dawah is that? What incentive does that person have to return to Islam or to Allah's house?

The command here is clear: when you hear offensive things, when you face mockery or insults, maintain your composure. Respond with wisdom, not with anger. Don't be reactionary. This is what صَبْر and تَقْوَىٰ look like in practice.

**High Resolve**

فَإِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ مِنْ عَزْمِ ٱلْأُمُورِ—Then indeed, that is from the most resolute of matters. The word عَزْم refers to determination and reaching a decision after thought, but it also carries the meaning of high resolve and lofty aims. A person with عَزِيمَة is someone who thinks big, who doesn't get caught up in petty concerns but sees the larger picture and aims for higher goals.

Allah ﷻ is saying that if you can maintain patience and taqwa in the face of constant provocation, then you have truly learned to aim high. You're not getting bogged down in petty reactions. You can see beyond the smokescreen. You understand what Islam's higher objectives really are and how you're supposed to contribute to achieving them.

This is especially crucial for young Muslims today—to see clearly through the noise and distractions, to understand what we're really supposed to be aiming for, and to develop the maturity and wisdom not to be pulled into every petty conflict or controversy that arises.

translation: And [mention, O Muhammad], when Allah took a covenant from those who were given the Scripture, [saying], "You must make it clear to the people and not conceal it." But they threw it away behind their backs and exchanged it for a small price. And wretched is that which they purchased
commentary:
وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ لَتُبَيِّنُنَّهُۥ لِلنَّاسِ وَلَا تَكْتُمُونَهُۥ فَنَبَذُوهُ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ وَٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا ۖ فَبِئْسَ مَا يَشْتَرُونَ

**The Covenant to Clarify**

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ—And [recall] when Allah took the covenant of those who were given the Scripture. The word مِيثَاق  refers to a mutually understood contract, a solemn agreement between parties.

لَتُبَيِّنُنَّهُۥ لِلنَّاسِ—You must make it clear to the people. The emphasis is heavy here: لَتُبَيِّنُنَّهُۥ—you *will definitely* clarify it. This is the core of the covenant: those given Scripture have an obligation to teach it, explain it, and make it accessible to all of humanity.

وَلَا تَكْتُمُونَهُۥ—And not conceal it. Notice that this phrase lacks the same grammatical emphasis as the first. The command not to hide the book isn't as heavily stressed because if you fulfill the first obligation—if you're constantly clarifying and teaching—then hiding it becomes impossible. The second prohibition is almost unnecessary because the first command, when fulfilled, naturally prevents the second violation.

This was the job of the People of the Book: clarify, clarify, clarify. Teach, teach, teach. Make the Scripture accessible and understood by everyone.

**Throwing It Behind Their Backs**

فَنَبَذُوهُ وَرَآءَ ظُهُورِهِمْ—But they threw it behind their backs. The pronoun "it" could refer to the Book itself or to the covenant, the command to clarify. Both interpretations work. They either abandoned the Scripture entirely, or they abandoned their responsibility to teach it to humanity. Either way, they threw this sacred trust behind their backs—discarding it completely.

**Selling Out for a Small Price**

وَٱشْتَرَوْا۟ بِهِۦ ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا—And they exchanged it for a small price. They sold the Book, or they sold their responsibility to the Book, for petty worldly gain. This could mean literal financial benefit—religious leaders taking bribes to hide certain teachings. Or it could mean prestige, status, avoiding conflict, or maintaining power. Whatever they gained, Allah ﷻ calls it ثَمَنًا قَلِيلًا—a paltry price. Nothing they gained could possibly be worth betraying this covenant.

**A Terrible Trade**

فَبِئْسَ مَا يَشْتَرُونَ—How wretched is that which they purchased! The word بِئْسَ is a verb of disgust used when something is truly unacceptable and painful. It's the opposite of نِعْمَ, which expresses excellence. Allah ﷻ is saying: what a horrible, disgraceful trade they made.

**Lessons for the Muslim Ummah**

This ayah carries a powerful warning and lesson for Muslims today. We inherited the same responsibility the People of the Book once had. Where is the culture of teaching and clarifying the Quran in our communities today? How accessible have we made Islamic knowledge to the masses—not just to Muslims, but to all of humanity?

Yes, we might have an occasional lecture here, a class there. But are our children learning? Are we teaching in every available forum—public libraries, universities, media platforms, television? Are we asking the questions that Allah ﷻ wants humanity to explore?

Dawah has multiple levels. Level one is our personal character—our honesty, integrity, and behavior in daily interactions and business dealings. Level two is the quality of our institutions and community affairs. Level three is our ability to produce and disseminate clear, accessible Islamic educational content through every available medium. Level four is personal conversations with friends and acquaintances when opportunities naturally arise. If we're functioning properly at all these levels, there would be a positive stir in society. People would notice. There would be impact.

But we can't skip straight to handing out pamphlets to strangers while neglecting everything else. And even when people do accept Islam, what do we have to offer them? Do we have strong educational systems, supportive communities, and clear pathways for learning?

The responsibility to clarify the Book is not just about answering questions—it's about teaching proactively, making knowledge accessible, and building institutions that can carry this mission forward. This is part of the أَمَانَة, the trust that we must guard like a gardener carefully tends a garden. If we fail in this, if we throw this responsibility behind our backs for the sake of comfort, convenience, or small worldly gains, then we risk falling into the same trap as those who came before us.

May Allah ﷻ make us from those who fulfill this covenant, who clarify His Book to humanity with wisdom and patience, and who never trade away this sacred trust for anything less than His pleasure.

translation: And never think that those who rejoice in what they have perpetrated and like to be praised for what they did not do - never think them [to be] in safety from the punishment, and for them is a painful punishment
commentary:
لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أَتَوا۟ وَّيُحِبُّونَ أَن يُحْمَدُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا۟ فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّهُم بِمَفَازَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ ۖ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

*Don't you dare assume in regards to those who are overjoyed by what they have committed and love to be praised for what they have not done—never think them safe from the punishment. For them is a painful torment.*

One of the most fascinating aspects of studying the Quran is recognizing how beliefs about Allah ﷻ directly impact our daily behavior and attitudes. When we think about concepts like the Oneness of Allah ﷻ, His power to create, or His ownership of the heavens and the earth, we often categorize these as philosophical or metaphysical realities that we simply accept. However, the Quran presents these beliefs as principles that should fundamentally shape how we think and act in everyday life. These two dimensions are inseparable in the Quranic framework.

Consider how later in Surah Al-Kahf, a conversation between two men about their assets suddenly shifts to discussing shirk. One man boasting about his wealth leads directly to a discussion about belief in the One who created from dust. The Quran consistently connects our behavior with our foundational beliefs about Allah ﷻ.

This ayah addresses a specific characteristic that was prominent among the hypocrites: taking credit for good that comes the Muslims' way while distancing themselves from any difficulty. When something beneficial happened to the believers, the hypocrites would claim they were right there alongside them. When hardship came, they would say, "Thank God we took precautions and didn't rush in foolishly like you." They essentially loved getting credit for what they hadn't actually done themselves.

Even victory in battle cannot be claimed as personal credit because that victory belongs to Allah ﷻ. Yet in the early Muslim community, some hypocrites began claiming that the growing influence of the Messenger ﷺ was a product of their support. They would say, "Look at these Muhajiroun—they have to live in our homes. They have nothing to their name. Islam is only surviving because we're here as its foundation." Some even used vile language, calling the Companions (RA) "the dogs of Makkah" who had strayed into Madinah.

Allah ﷻ responds with a decisive warning: لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ —"Don't you dare assume in regards to those..." The phrase يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَآ أَتَوا۟ describes people who are excessively happy and proud of their own accomplishments. The word أَتَوا۟ means "what they have committed" or "what they have come up with"—essentially their own actions and contributions.

These individuals وَّيُحِبُّونَ أَن يُحْمَدُوا۟ بِمَا لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا۟ —they love to receive praise and thanks for what they haven't done themselves. This is a profound spiritual disease.

### Balancing Praise and Gratitude

The Prophet's ﷺ teachings present what might seem like a paradox. On one hand, we're instructed not to seek praise, and the Prophet ﷺ even gave instructions about throwing dirt in the face of someone who excessively praises you. On the other hand, we're told that whoever doesn't thank people hasn't thanked Allah ﷻ. Different variations of this principle appear throughout the hadith literature: whoever doesn't appreciate people hasn't appreciated Allah ﷻ.

How do we balance these teachings? For Muslims, the best way to appreciate people is through sincere dua for them. This is the highest form of appreciation we can offer. It's not merely a polite gesture when we say "may Allah ﷻ bless you"—it's something tangible and meaningful in our worldview.

Beyond dua, we must be intelligent about when praise is beneficial and when it becomes harmful. A young man struggling to maintain even the bare minimum of his religion—barely holding onto his prayers—needs encouragement. If his father says, "I'm very proud of how you're carrying yourself, and I want you to keep it up," that reinforcement can be crucial. It won't corrupt his intentions; he needs that support.

However, if a gifted speaker delivers an excellent khutbah at a conference, approaching him afterwards and saying, "That was the best thing I've ever heard in my entire life! You've changed my life forever! You're so awesome! Can I take a picture with you?"—these things don't help anyone. They don't help you, and they certainly don't help the speaker. That is precisely the occasion where you should simply make dua for such a person: "O Allah ﷻ, protect them. Keep them sincere. Keep them from making mistakes. Don't let this praise get to their head."

Speakers are in actual danger of letting praise affect them. Nobody is immune from this corruption. What happens when you become accustomed to praise and then someone comes along and says your speech was offensive, stupid, or ridiculous? You're completely thrown off. How dare they criticize you? This reveals that you couldn't handle the criticism because you had become dependent on the praise.

### The Reality of Our Accomplishments

One of the most beautiful lessons embedded in this ayah is that we don't technically deserve praise anyway, because we ourselves haven't truly accomplished anything. Any good that emerges is a blessing from Allah ﷻ. If someone's heart is moved by what you write or say, that transformation isn't something you caused. We call Allah مقلب القلوب ﷻ—the Turner of Hearts—for a reason. Both the listener and the speaker must understand this reality.

Unfortunately, in our contemporary dawah culture, we've created a problematic dynamic. Speakers and scholars are sometimes treated like celebrities. The constant requests for photos, the elevation of certain personalities, the inaccessibility that develops—all of this can reinforce the very culture of loving praise that this ayah warns against.

I've encountered students of knowledge who openly admitted, "I want to be just like you—getting invited to conferences, flying first class." If our intentions in studying the highest word—the Word of Allah ﷻ—have been reduced to such petty aspirations, it's a profound disgrace and disservice to what we're learning.

This is why checks and balances are essential, especially for younger people involved in any form of Islamic work. Having sincere friends, teachers, and mentors who can guide you, who can stop you and say, "I think you're going too fast—slow down" or "You shouldn't have said that"—these relationships are invaluable. Having people in your life who can put you in your place, who can say "You need to be quiet on this issue," is a crucial mechanism for maintaining sincerity and humility.

### The Disease of Titles and Recognition

We've become a people obsessed with titles and accomplishments. There are individuals who cannot introduce themselves without immediately mentioning their credentials. Education—whether religious or secular—naturally brings more recognition as you advance through its ranks. You become a teacher, a doctor, you earn certifications or ijazahs in religious sciences. You achieve a PhD, become a researcher, scholar, or professor, and with each level, recognition increases.

However, knowledge (ilm) is supposed to humble us. In modern culture, both religious and secular, the more knowledge you acquire, the more you are elevated and the more you expect to be elevated. This is the exact opposite of what knowledge should produce in a believer. We must be vigilant against allowing our sincere learning to be ruined by becoming people who live for praise.

### Practical Applications

This ayah also covers plagiarism—taking credit for work you didn't do. It encompasses cheating on exams, claiming credit for a project that someone else completed at work to secure a promotion, stealing others' ideas. All of these fall under the warning of this ayah.

These principles apply throughout our lives: in the workplace, in academic settings, in business dealings. Even with children, when one child cleans the room but another takes credit, this pattern begins. If reinforced from a young age, it manifests in concerning ways throughout one's career.

The ayah concludes with a severe warning: فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّهُم بِمَفَازَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلْعَذَابِ —"Never think them safe from the punishment." The word مَفَازَة is an emphatic form derived from فَوْز, which means to escape difficulty and end up in a good situation. Don't ever assume they will escape in any way from the punishment.

وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ —"And they exclusively have painful torment." This isn't merely a consequence of their hypocrisy in the time of the Prophet ﷺ. It's a universal principle about the spiritual danger of seeking credit for what we haven't truly accomplished.

translation: And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

*And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and Allah is over all things Competent.*

The connection between this ayah and the previous warning is profound and deliberate. After addressing those who love taking credit for accomplishments, Allah ﷻ immediately reminds us: وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ —"To Allah alone belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth."

If you want to give credit, give it to the One who actually owns all things, including everything you've accomplished that might be considered praiseworthy. It all belongs to Him. Your proper understanding of Allah's actual dominion, ownership, and sovereignty will naturally prevent you from wanting to claim credit for yourself. 

A weakness in this belief—in truly comprehending Allah's complete ownership—is what makes people behave in the manner described in the previous ayah. The desire to take credit and seek praise is a direct consequence of weak tawhid. This is precisely why the Quran consistently connects beliefs about Allah ﷻ with our daily attitudes and behaviors.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ —"And Allah has complete control over all things." When we internalize this reality—that Allah ﷻ has power over everything, including whatever we perceive as our own accomplishments—the desire for personal recognition naturally diminishes.

This reminder of Allah's sovereignty serves as both a warning and a cure. It's a warning to those who arrogantly claim credit for themselves, reminding them that punishment comes from the One who owns everything. It's also a cure for believers, because when we truly grasp that every success, every moment of eloquence, every positive outcome flows from Allah's will and power, we're freed from the burden of seeking validation from creation.

The smallest of our dealings should remind us of this principle. Even in something as minor as an academic test, this ayah should echo in our consciousness: Don't you dare assume about those who are happy because of what they've done and love getting credit for what they didn't do. In every aspect of life—whether we're at work, in school, giving a speech, or simply interacting with family—we must remember that all credit ultimately belongs to Allah ﷻ, the Owner of the heavens and the earth, the One who has power over all things.

translation: Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of the night and the day are signs for those of understanding
commentary:
إِنَّ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱخْتِلَـٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

These ayahs hold a special place in the Quran, carrying with them a beautiful story that reveals their profound impact. After the passing of the Prophet ﷺ, some of the companions (RA) found themselves missing him deeply. They approached Aisha (RA), the Mother of the Believers, asking her to share something unique about the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, something they hadn't heard before. She responded that everything about him was unique, but then she chose to share a particularly intimate memory.

One night, as they lay together, the Prophet ﷺ turned to her and courteously asked permission to leave and spend time in prayer with his Lord. Despite Allah ﷻ calling His slave, the Prophet ﷺ first sought permission from his young wife—a remarkable demonstration of courtesy and respect for her rights. When she granted permission, he made wudu and stood in prayer, reciting these very ayahs.

As he recited, he began to cry. His tears flowed so abundantly that his entire beard became soaked and dripping. When he went into prostration, a puddle formed where his blessed face touched the ground. He remained in that state for so long that by the time Bilal (RA) came to call him for Fajr prayer, the Prophet ﷺ, overwhelmed by what Allah ﷻ had revealed to him, said, "How can you call me when Allah has revealed these ayahs to me?" These were the ayahs that moved the heart of the best of creation ﷺ so profoundly.

The ayah begins with emphasis: "Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of the night and the day are signs for those of understanding." This theme was touched upon earlier in Surah Al-Baqarah, but there it came with an extensive list of signs—the rain, the ships, the winds, the clouds—concluding with "for a people who use reason." The phrasing there suggested struggle, effort to understand.

Here, the list is dramatically shortened. The sky, the earth, the alternation of night and day—that's all that's mentioned. Yet these few signs are deemed sufficient for أُولِي الْأَلْبَابِ, the possessors of sound minds. This is a higher level of consciousness, a purer state of awareness.

Let's examine this term الْأَلْبَاب carefully. The word لُبّ refers to a sound mind, a pure intellect—one that is سَالِم , free from distractions and vanities. It describes a mind uncluttered by the noise of trivial concerns, capable of contemplating higher realities. The earlier passage required more reminders because those addressed were struggling to grasp the message. Here, Allah ﷻ is describing people who possess such clarity of thought that they need only look at the sky and the earth to become lost in profound reflection.

The challenge we face is that our minds have become repositories of clutter—entertainment, gossip, sports statistics, endless details about others' lives. When our consciousness is filled with useless information, we lose the capacity to appreciate beneficial knowledge. It's like someone who constantly watches horror films becoming desensitized to fear—when they hear about the punishment of Hellfire, it fails to move them because they've numbed themselves to such concepts.

My teacher used to give the analogy of diet: if you eat junk food constantly and then throw in a vegetable or two, are you suddenly healthy? No. True health requires not just adding good foods but removing the harmful ones. Similarly, learning beneficial knowledge while remaining immersed in entertainment and distractions doesn't purify the mind. The good gets lost among the clutter, unable to transform us.

لُبّ سَالِم—a pure mind—belongs to someone who has made a conscious decision not to waste their mental and spiritual energy on empty pursuits. They've chosen to step back from the noise and dedicate their faculties to deeper contemplation. These are people who look at the sky and actually think. How many of us truly observe the heavens and reflect? How many consider the morning and remember وَالْفَجْرِ (By the dawn)? Even in places like Texas where the sky dominates the landscape, we pass through our days without this awareness.

This ayah describes a special breed of people—those who have achieved a balance between spiritual and intellectual awakening. This theme of Ulul-Albab that appears here will return at the end of the surah, creating a frame around these teachings: "And none will remember except those of understanding."

translation: Who remember Allah while standing or sitting or [lying] on their sides and give thought to the creation of the heavens and the earth, [saying], "Our Lord, You did not create this aimlessly; exalted are You [above such a thing]; then protect us from the punishment of the Fire
commentary:
ٱلَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ ٱللَّهَ قِيَـٰمًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِهِمْ وَيَتَفَكَّرُونَ فِى خَلْقِ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ رَبَّنَا مَا خَلَقْتَ هَـٰذَا بَـٰطِلًا سُبْحَـٰنَكَ فَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ

Allah ﷻ now describes two defining characteristics of these people of sound minds, and both are profound. First: الَّذِينَ يَذْكُرُونَ اللَّهَ قِيَامًا وَقُعُودًا وَعَلَىٰ جُنُوبِهِمْ—"Those who remember Allah standing, sitting, and lying on their sides."

Standing and sitting can be understood as positions of prayer, but lying on one's side is not a prayer position—it's the position of sleep, of rest. The implication is clear: these people remember Allah ﷻ in every state they find themselves. Whether standing, sitting, or lying down, whatever position they're in, they are engaged in the remembrance of their Lord.

Before we continue, we must understand what dhikr truly means. In the Quran, remembrance is described as an activity of the heart. It's a spiritual exercise, not merely a verbal one. Dhikr means reminding yourself of who Allah ﷻ is, keeping His presence alive in your consciousness. The Quran tells us: أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ—"Verily, in the remembrance of Allah do hearts find tranquility." Conversely, Allah ﷻ warns: وَلَا تُطِعْ مَنْ أَغْفَلْنَا قَلْبَهُ عَن ذِكْرِنَا—"And do not obey one whose heart We have made heedless of Our remembrance."

The heart can be made aware or unaware of Allah's mention. The more you remember Allah ﷻ, the more your heart becomes fortified, protected, at peace.

So the first activity of people of sound minds is this spiritual discipline—constant remembrance. But notice: remembrance by definition is not new information. You're not learning something you didn't know. You already know Allah ﷻ is One. If I woke you in the middle of the night and asked how many gods there are, you'd correctly answer "One." The knowledge is there in your mind. But dhikr moves that knowledge from the mind to the heart.

Imagine you're a young person living away from home for the first time, in a college dorm with no parental supervision. You can do whatever you want, whenever you want, and no one will say anything. Your friends are all going to a party, to a concert, to a movie. Your heart pulls you in that direction. But if you've truly strengthened your remembrance of Allah ﷻ, your heart finds satisfaction in that remembrance instead. أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ—hearts find rest in the remembrance of Allah ﷻ. You can smile and say to your friends, "You guys go ahead, it's okay," and you're genuinely at peace with that choice.

This reinforcement must happen throughout the day. We can begin building this aspect of our personality through two accessible practices. First, the morning and evening adhkar—the supplications taught by the Prophet ﷺ. There are duas for waking up, going to sleep, entering your home, leaving your home, getting in your car, getting out of your car, eating, changing clothes, entering the bathroom, leaving the bathroom. There's virtually no moment of your day left uncovered.

And here's the beautiful thing: when you're about to do something and you feel uncomfortable making dua to Allah ﷻ before it, what does that tell you? Probably that you shouldn't be doing it. The adhkar become a gauge, a measure of what will benefit your heart and what will harm it.

The second practice is developing a deep relationship with the Quran. We should become people who don't just recite it or memorize it, but who think through it. The Quran should become the way we think—not just something we quote in speeches, but the lens through which we view everything.

I once tried an experiment with myself. I was driving and decided to see if I could connect everything around me to the Quran. Someone was speeding past, driving recklessly—immediately بَلْ تُحِبُّونَ الْعَاجِلَةَ came to mind: "Rather, you love what is immediate." I looked out at cows grazing in a field near the highway. Cars zoomed by just feet away. What did the cows do? Nothing. They didn't even look up, completely oblivious to the danger.
